nodes.name: Architectural Isolation
nodes.content:
# Architectural Isolation

Defences that impose system-level structural guarantees to prevent injected instructions from reaching privileged execution paths. Unlike prompt-level approaches, these techniques enforce separation outside the model's context window — through separate model instances with restricted capabilities, trusted executable artifacts that pre-commit agent logic before untrusted data is read, representation-level query structuring, deterministic validation of structured outputs, instruction authentication, or OS-level process isolation in multi-app agentic systems. The guarantee is structural: even a model that "wants" to follow an injection cannot do so if the injection never reaches the privileged component. This decisive reduction in attack success rate comes at the cost of non-trivial deployment complexity and, in some cases, fine-tuning, token overhead, or secret-management burden.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Role Separation\|Role Separation]] | Splits agent execution into a Privileged LLM that plans and calls tools and a Quarantined LLM that processes untrusted content but has no tool-calling authority, so injected instructions in external data cannot trigger privileged actions. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Role-Separated Information Flow Control\|Role-Separated Information Flow Control]] | Combines a trusted/untrusted agent split with taint-style provenance checks at the boundary, so low-trust content is both confined to a restricted agent and re-checked before it can influence privileged tools, trusted context, or final outputs. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Code-Then-Execute\|Code-Then-Execute]] | Commits privileged agent logic to a formal executable artifact before reading untrusted data — from sandboxed, capability-tracked programs to fixed or dynamically validated tool plans — so later observations can fill parameters but cannot freely rewrite the action structure. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Structured Queries\|Structured Queries]] | Separates trusted instructions from untrusted data at the interface or representation level — from API-level split fields to explicit labelled regions with reserved tokens and model adaptation — so external content is handled as data rather than executable instructions. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Structured Output Validation\|Structured Output Validation]] | Treats model-generated structured artifacts as untrusted until a deterministic parser/validator checks them against developer-authored schemas or execution constraints, blocking or repairing unsafe outputs before they reach downstream systems. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Instruction Authentication\|Instruction Authentication]] | Encodes authorised commands into privileged aliases or signed forms that the adapted model alone treats as executable, so attacker-written plain-language commands stay inert even when they appear in untrusted content. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Execution Isolation\|Execution Isolation]] | Confines each third-party app in an agentic system to its own OS-isolated process with a dedicated LLM instance; a trusted non-LLM hub mediates all cross-app requests and surfaces user permission dialogs for unexpected actions, preventing cross-app injection propagation. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Information Flow Control\|Information Flow Control]] | Hooks the agent runtime to track how data propagates from external observations to tool-call parameters, then enforces a formal security policy (privilege specification or confidentiality/integrity lattice) to block any call whose parameters are tainted by untrusted content — without modifying the backbone LLM. Subdivided into Taint-Tracking IFC and Policy-Specification IFC. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Defence-in-Depth Architectures\|Defence-in-Depth Architectures]] | Groups broad practitioner and framework sources that layer parameterized tool use, least privilege, sandboxing, user confirmation, plugin vetting, and other hardening controls around LLM systems, rather than proposing a single formal enforcement primitive. |

## Advantages

N/A

## Limitations

All techniques require substantial deployment changes: Role Separation demands dual model instances and a routing layer; Code-Then-Execute spans both Sandboxed Script Execution (CaMeL), which requires a custom interpreter, capability system, and manually maintained security policies, and Plan-Then-Execute systems such as IPIGuard and DRIFT, which require a trusted planner plus a graph or validator layer, incur roughly 2x or higher token overhead, and still struggle on open-ended tasks such as AgentDyn; Structured Queries range from simple API/interface redesign proposals to stronger implementations that require a specialised front-end and sometimes fine-tuning; Structured Output Validation adds a deterministic parser/validator layer, but its protection is bounded by the supported grammar/schema and the correctness of developer-authored constraints; Instruction Authentication additionally depends on application-side command rewriting, privileged-vocabulary training or prompting, and secrecy of the authenticated aliases; Execution Isolation (IsolateGPT) requires OS-level process isolation per app and a hub orchestration layer; Information Flow Control (AgentArmor, Progent, RTBAS) enforces formal security policies at the tool-call boundary without modifying the backbone LLM — but the approaches differ in cost and scope: AgentArmor's PDG-based dynamic analysis incurs significant token and latency overhead; Progent's Z3-backed policy proxy adds only ≈0.0008 s per task; RTBAS adds one extra LM call per generation (LM-Judge variant) or local attention-model inference (attention-based variant), and its security guarantees are only as strong as the developer's label assignments. All IFC approaches may over-restrict legitimate flows under static policies, and none defends against attacks that operate entirely within permitted tool-call scope. RTBAS additionally reduces utility to 22–33% on tasks that inherently require acting on untrusted external data (Slack suite). None of the approaches is fully immune to adaptive attacks: CaMeL's residual failures include data-flow-to-control-flow attacks and text-to-text injections outside its threat model; StruQ's training-time defence is partially broken by optimisation-based attacks (GCG at 58% ASR, TAP at 9% ASR); Signed-Prompt's security collapses if the privileged alias leaks and its evidence remains limited to a narrow command set; IsolateGPT's residual risk requires a user to approve a malicious permission dialog; AgentArmor's LLM-based dependency analysis may be manipulated by an adaptive attacker and cannot defend against DoS-style attacks; IPIGuard's argument mis-estimation leaves a ~0.64% residual ASR. Policy maintenance fatigue, ecosystem dependency on capability-aware tools, and user approval fatigue are additional operational burdens.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | ASR (↓) | CaMeL (Sandboxed Script Execution) | ~0% | ~16% undefended | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Near-zero ASR; 1-2 residual attacks outside threat model; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | Task completion (↑) | CaMeL (Sandboxed Script Execution) | ~77% | ~84% undefended | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | ~7pp utility cost; ~3x token overhead; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| Manual injection test suite (15 attack types) | ASR (↓) | StruQ (Structured Queries) | <2% (13/15 types) | ~50% undefended | Llama-2 variants | Near-zero utility loss; source: @chen2025struq |
| GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) | ASR (↓) | StruQ (Structured Queries) | 58% | 97% undefended | Llama-7B | Optimization-based attack partially breaks through; source: @chen2025struq; attack: @zou2023universaltransferableadversarialattacks |
| InjecAgent (extended, 1,598 attacks) | Attack success rate (↓) | IsolateGPT (Execution Isolation) | 7.6% (permission warning rate) | 20.2% VanillaGPT | GPT-4 (v0613) | 100% of attack-flow dialogs included a warning; source: @wu2025isolategptexecutionisolationarchitecture; benchmark: @zhan2024injecagentbenchmarkingindirectprompt |
| InjecAgent — App compromise (financial harm) | Attack success rate (↓) | IsolateGPT (Execution Isolation) | 0.0% | 9.8% VanillaGPT | GPT-4 | N=153; source: @wu2025isolategptexecutionisolationarchitecture; benchmark: @zhan2024injecagentbenchmarkingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | AgentArmor (Taint-Tracking IFC) | 3% | 17% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | PDG-based runtime enforcement; 1% utility loss; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| ASB (OPI) | ASR (↓) | AgentArmor (Taint-Tracking IFC) | 0% | 41–50% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Perfect detection on observation prompt injection; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | Progent (Policy-Specification IFC) | 0% | 39.9% no defence | GPT-4o | Deterministic policy enforcement; provably guaranteed; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | Utility (no attack) ↑ | Progent (Policy-Specification IFC) | 79.4% | 79.4% no defence | GPT-4o | Zero utility loss; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | IPIGuard (Plan-Then-Execute, avg 4 attacks) | 0.69% | No Defense 13.16% | GPT-4o-mini | Near-zero ASR; highest benign utility (67.01%) among all tested defences; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | UA ↑ | IPIGuard (Plan-Then-Execute, avg 4 attacks) | 58.77% | No Defense 54.30% | GPT-4o-mini | Best utility-security balance of all evaluated methods; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| ASB | ASR (↓) | Progent (Policy-Specification IFC) | 0% | 70.3% no defence | GPT-4o | Malicious-tool scenario; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | DRIFT (Plan-Then-Execute) | 1.4% | 30.7% undefended; 0.0% CaMeL; 15.5% Progent | GPT-4o-mini | Three-layer: Secure Planner + Dynamic Validator + Injection Isolator; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDojo | Benign Utility (↑) | DRIFT | 57.3% | 38.4% CaMeL | GPT-4o-mini | Best benign utility among planning/policy-based defences tested; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| ASB | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 4.8% | 40.8% undefended; 15.8% Progent | GPT-4o-mini | source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDyn | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 3.1% | 50.0% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Open-ended benchmark; best ASR-utility balance among tested defences; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentBench OS | Secure-utility rate (SUR) ↑ | PFI (Role-Separated IFC) | 68.42% | ReAct 0.00%; IsolateGPT 10.53%; f-secure 10.53% | GPT-4o | Head-to-head OS-suite comparison; higher SUR than IsolateGPT and f-secure; source: @kim2025promptflowintegrity |
| AgentDojo + AgentBench OS | Context/data-injection ASR ↓ | PFI (Role-Separated IFC) | 0.00% in all reported runs | ReAct 34.02%-58.62% | Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 1.5 Pro | Zero unsafe-data-flow / context-injection success across both benchmarks; source: @kim2025promptflowintegrity |
| AgentDojo (weighted avg, under attack) | ASR (↓) | RTBAS LM-Judge (Taint-Tracking IFC) | 0% | ~92% attacks succeed undefended | GPT-4o | IFC lattice enforcement; <2% utility loss under attack; also extends to confidentiality; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| Accidental Leakage (authors' benchmark, 37 tasks) | FPR ↓ | RTBAS LM-Judge (Taint-Tracking IFC) | 8.1% | 29.7% GPTs baseline | GPT-4o | Privacy-leakage defence via confidentiality lattice; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| MTST (SafeFlowBench) | Accunsafe ↓ | SAFEFLOW (Taint-Tracking IFC) | 0.0% | 56–79% undefended | 10 VLMs (GPT-4.1 … Mistral Small 3.1) | SF-Level labelling + Verifier gate; multimodal adversarial scenarios; source: @li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy |
| AgentHarm | Harm Score ↓ | SAFEFLOW (Taint-Tracking IFC) | 0.0% | 56.4–82.2% undefended | GPT-4.1 | 440 adversarial task variants; cross-benchmark generalisation; source: @li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy |
| Anthropic Red-Teaming + Helpful (balanced 200) | Harmful prompts blocked ↑ | NeMo Guardrails input + output moderation rails | close to 99% | gpt-3.5-turbo without rails: 93% | gpt-3.5-turbo | Defence-in-depth guardrail runtime; helpful-request blocking was 2%; source: @rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable |
| Anthropic Red-Teaming + Helpful (balanced 200) | Helpful requests blocked ↓ | NeMo Guardrails input + output moderation rails | 2% | gpt-3.5-turbo without rails: 0% | gpt-3.5-turbo | Defence-in-depth safety/utility trade-off; source: @rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable |
| Custom jailbreak dataset (50 attacks) | Attack prompts blocked ↑ | Full GUARDIAN (Defence-in-Depth Architecture) | 100% | 0% blocked undefended | Llama-2-7b-chat | 50/50 blocked on a small custom jailbreak set; authors caution this may not generalize; no clean-utility evaluation reported; source: @rai2024guardian |
| Unseen attacks | ASR % ↓ | PromptGuard full pipeline (Defence-in-Depth Architecture) | 4.5% | 28.1% no defence | GPT-4, Claude 3, LLaMA 2 (aggregate / model-unspecified) | Integrated end-to-end summary for a four-layer black-box-compatible pipeline; source: @alzahrani2026promptguard |
| In-paper prompt injection benchmark (847 cases) | ASR ↓ | Full defense (Defence-in-Depth Architecture) | 8.7% | 73.2% baseline RAG | 7 models (aggregate) | Three-layer RAG-agent stack; headline end-to-end result with an 88.1% relative reduction from baseline; source: @ramakrishnan2025securingaiagentsprompt |
| MMLU, HellaSwag, and domain-specific QA | Task performance retention ↑ | Full defense (Defence-in-Depth Architecture) | 94.3% | 100.0% baseline RAG | 7 models (aggregate) | Utility remains high despite the full stack; benign retrieval false-positive rate is 5.7%; source: @ramakrishnan2025securingaiagentsprompt |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | OpenAI Multi-Layer Defence (safety training + monitors + sandboxing + confirmation gates) | — | — | GPT-family (unspecified) | No quantitative results reported; qualitative practitioner overview of the deployed five-layer defence stack; source: @openai2025promptinjections |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Refrain, Restrict, and Trap (RRT) design guidance | — | — | LLM-integrated applications | Qualitative practitioner guidance only; recommends avoiding unnecessary LLM use, sharply restricting permissions and autonomy, and trapping risky inputs/outputs; source: @hamiel2023reducing |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Amazon Bedrock Agents multi-layer defence (PVE + Guardrails + user confirmation + nonce tags) | — | — | Amazon Bedrock (unspecified) | No quantitative results reported; practitioner architecture guide; source: @chaudhry2025securing |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Code-Then-Execute
nodes.content:
# Code-Then-Execute

> **Conceptual anchor** — Code-Then-Execute groups defences that commit privileged agent logic to a formal executable artifact before the agent reads untrusted data. In this taxonomy, the two main forms are Sandboxed Script Execution and Plan-Then-Execute.

Defences that move privileged decision-making out of free-form natural-language reasoning and into a trusted executable artifact — a restricted program, ordered tool plan, dependency graph, or equivalent structure — produced before the agent processes external content. Because later observations can only fill typed variables or approved parameters inside that artifact, injected instructions cannot freely rewrite the task's control structure at runtime. Some systems execute full restricted programs with capability tracking; others enforce a fixed or privilege-bounded tool plan.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@beurerkellner2025designpatternssecuringllm\|Design Patterns for Securing LLM Agents (Beurer-Kellner et al.)]] | Formalises Code-Then-Execute as a general architectural pattern, explicitly stating that it generalises Plan-Then-Execute and citing Debenedetti et al. (CaMeL) as a concrete instantiation. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution\|Sandboxed Script Execution]] | Synthesises a restricted program and runs it inside a custom interpreter that tracks provenance and enforces capability-aware policies at tool-call time; represented here by CaMeL. |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Plan-Then-Execute/Plan-Then-Execute\|Plan-Then-Execute]] | Commits to a fixed or privilege-bounded tool plan before reading untrusted data, blocking runtime introduction of new high-privilege actions; includes IPIGuard and DRIFT. |

## Advantages

- Converts privileged behaviour into an auditable artifact enforced outside the model's conversational context.
- Structural protection against control-flow hijacking: untrusted observations can fill variables or arguments, but cannot freely invent new privileged actions.
- Model-agnostic and training-free: the main changes live in orchestration, typing, and runtime enforcement rather than in the backbone model.
- Supports a spectrum of rigidity/utility trade-offs: full program interpreters provide stronger semantic control, while plan-based variants retain more flexibility on dynamic tasks.

## Limitations

- Requires strong upfront planning or code generation; missing steps, weak tool schemas, or poor abstractions reduce utility.
- Deployment overhead is substantial: implementations need a custom interpreter, typed intermediate representation, plan validator, or equivalent runtime machinery between the model and its tools.
- Most systems in this family do not address text-only attacks with no tool-call or data-flow consequence.
- Open-ended tasks remain difficult: rigid plans fail when legitimate trajectories depend heavily on intermediate results, while dynamic variants must carefully restrict which plan updates are allowed.
- Security still depends on correct policy authoring, privilege classification, and artifact generation; mistakes in those layers weaken the guarantee.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | ASR ↓ | CaMeL (Sandboxed Script Execution) | ~0% | ~16% undefended | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Near-zero ASR; 1-2 residual attacks outside threat model; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | IPIGuard (Plan-Then-Execute, avg 4 attacks) | 0.69% | No Defense 13.16% | GPT-4o-mini | Near-zero ASR with the strongest benign utility among tested planning-based methods; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | Benign Utility ↑ | DRIFT (Plan-Then-Execute) | 57.3% | 38.4% CaMeL | GPT-4o-mini | Dynamic plan updates recover much of the utility lost by more rigid artifact-enforcement variants while keeping ASR near zero (1.4%); source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Plan-Then-Execute
nodes.content:
# Plan-Then-Execute

Defences that construct an authorised execution plan — as an ordered tool sequence, dependency graph, constraint checklist, or equivalent structured artifact — before the agent accesses any external content. Injected instructions encountered during execution cannot introduce new privileged tool calls because the plan defines the trusted baseline enforced outside the model's context window. Purely static variants fix the full trajectory upfront; dynamic variants allow low-risk (read-only) deviations to extend the plan while blocking higher-privilege (write/execute) deviations, preserving utility on complex multi-step tasks without relaxing the core security guarantee.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency\|IPIGuard]] | Reformulates agent execution as a traversal of a pre-planned Tool Dependency Graph (TDG/DAG); injected instructions cannot introduce Command tools at runtime, while Argument Estimation infers missing parameters from legitimate upstream context and Fake Tool Invocation neutralises injected tasks that overlap with the user's plan. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense\|DRIFT]] | Combines a pre-execution Secure Planner (control and data constraints), a Dynamic Validator that enforces privilege-tiered (Read/Write/Execute) per-call checks and updates the plan for low-risk deviations, and a per-turn Injection Isolator that scrubs injected content from agent memory — achieving 1.4% ASR on AgentDojo with higher benign utility (57.3%) than static alternatives such as CaMeL (38.4%). |

## Advantages

- Security guaranteed by plan structure rather than model behaviour: even if the LLM processes injected text, it cannot act on it if the required tool is absent from the pre-approved plan.
- No model fine-tuning required: the defence operates entirely at the planning and execution-orchestration layer.
- Model-agnostic: validated across reasoning and non-reasoning LLMs without architecture changes.
- Near-zero ASR: IPIGuard achieves ≤1% average ASR across all evaluated attack types while preserving or improving benign utility relative to the undefended baseline.
- Dynamic variants (DRIFT) resolve the utility collapse that purely static plans suffer on complex multi-step tasks by allowing low-risk plan updates while blocking high-privilege deviations.
- Multi-layer coverage (DRIFT): combining pre-execution constraint generation, privilege-aware runtime validation, and per-turn memory isolation addresses control-level, data-level, and memory injection vectors within a single system.
- Adaptive-attack resilience: DRIFT's manual bypass prompts raise ASR by only 0.7% and PAIR black-box optimisation by only 0.62%, indicating the multi-layer design is difficult to defeat even under adaptive pressure.

## Limitations

- Requires strong planning capability in the backbone LLM; a mis-specified plan or TDG reduces utility and may leave the agent unable to complete the user task.
- Significant token overhead: IPIGuard incurs ~2× token overhead (~14,605 vs ~6,165 input tokens on GPT-4o-mini); DRIFT's three-component system (Secure Planner, Dynamic Validator, Injection Isolator) each consume additional LLM tokens.
- Scope limited to attacks that trigger tool calls; attacks that manipulate only textual outputs without invoking tools are outside the threat model.
- Argument mis-estimation edge cases leave a small residual ASR (~0.64% under the hardest IPIGuard attack; 1.4–4.8% across benchmarks for DRIFT).
- Open-ended agentic tasks where the full trajectory cannot be anticipated upfront remain an unsolved challenge for all defences in this category (DRIFT achieves 3.1% ASR on AgentDyn but all defences still struggle on this benchmark).
- Dynamic plan updates (DRIFT) require correct privilege classification of all tool calls; privilege-misclassification could allow an adaptive attacker to smuggle write-level instructions past a Read-tier check.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | IPIGuard (avg, 4 attacks) | 0.69% | No Defense 13.16% | GPT-4o-mini | Average over Ignore Previous, InjecAgent, Tool Knowledge, Important Instruction; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | UA ↑ | IPIGuard (avg, 4 attacks) | 58.77% | No Defense 54.30% | GPT-4o-mini | Utility Under Attack; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | BU ↑ | IPIGuard | 67.01% | No Defense 68.04% | GPT-4o-mini | Benign Utility (no attacks); highest among all defences tested; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | IPIGuard (Important Instruction) | 0.64% | No Defense 27.19% | GPT-4o-mini | Hardest single-attack scenario; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Spotlighting | 11.31% | No Defense 13.16% | GPT-4o-mini | Comparison baseline; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Tool Filter | 2.06% | No Defense 13.16% | GPT-4o-mini | Comparison baseline; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | UA ↑ | Tool Filter | 53.36% | No Defense 54.30% | GPT-4o-mini | Comparison baseline; source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 1.4% | 30.7% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | important_instruction attack; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDojo | Benign Utility (↑) | DRIFT | 57.3% | 63.6% undefended; 38.4% CaMeL | GPT-4o-mini | Higher benign utility than CaMeL (38.4%) while matching near-zero ASR; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDojo | Utility Under Attack (↑) | DRIFT | 50.9% | 35.4% CaMeL | GPT-4o-mini | source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 1.4% | 15.5% Progent | GPT-4o-mini | DRIFT lower ASR than Progent on same benchmark; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| ASB | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 4.8% | 40.8% undefended; 15.8% Progent | GPT-4o-mini | OPI attack; DRIFT lower ASR than Progent; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDyn | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 3.1% | 50.0% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Open-ended tasks; best balance among tested defences; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDyn | Benign Utility (↑) | DRIFT | 47.3% | 46.7% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| outperforms:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters]] | IPIGuard achieves 0.69% average ASR vs Spotlighting's 11.31% on the same AgentDojo benchmark (GPT-4o-mini); source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency. |
| outperforms:: [[Prompt Massaging/Instruction Reinforcement\|Instruction Reinforcement]] | IPIGuard achieves 0.69% average ASR and 58.77% UA vs Sandwich's 5.25% ASR and 44.01% UA on AgentDojo (GPT-4o-mini); source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency. |
| outperforms:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution\|Sandboxed Script Execution (Tool Filter)]] | IPIGuard achieves 0.69% average ASR and 58.77% UA vs CaMeL's Tool Filter baseline (2.06% ASR, 53.36% UA) on the same AgentDojo benchmark (GPT-4o-mini); source: @an2025ipiguardnoveltooldependency. |
| outperforms:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution\|Sandboxed Script Execution]] | DRIFT achieves 57.3% benign utility vs CaMeL's 38.4% and 50.9% utility under attack vs CaMeL's 35.4%, while matching near-zero ASR (1.4% vs 0.0%) on AgentDojo (GPT-4o-mini); source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense. |
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution\|Sandboxed Script Execution]] | DRIFT directly addresses CaMeL's stated limitation of static control/data flow policies that cannot adapt to complex multi-step tasks, replacing fixed capability rules with dynamically updated constraint sets; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense. |
| outperforms:: [[Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Information Flow Control\|Information Flow Control]] | DRIFT achieves 1.4% ASR vs Progent's 15.5% on AgentDojo (GPT-4o-mini), and 4.8% vs 15.8% on ASB; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense. |
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Information Flow Control\|Information Flow Control]] | DRIFT adds pre-execution constraint generation and a per-turn Injection Isolator, addressing Progent's identified weakness of legacy injected instructions persisting in agent memory and influencing future reasoning steps; source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense. |

nodes.id: Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution
nodes.name: Sandboxed Script Execution
nodes.path: Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution.md
nodes.content:
# Sandboxed Script Execution

Compiles agent plans into restricted executable programs that run inside a custom interpreter with provenance and capability tracking. Because tool outputs are handled as typed program values rather than fed back into privileged natural-language reasoning, the runtime can enforce per-value data-flow and tool-use policies before side effects occur.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign\|CaMeL]] | Introduces CaMeL, which translates agent plans into restricted Python scripts executed by a custom interpreter that tracks data provenance via capabilities and enforces security policies at tool-call time, achieving near-zero ASR on AgentDojo. |

## Advantages

N/A

## Limitations

- **Text-to-text attacks out of scope**: attacks with no control/data flow consequence are not addressed. Examples: an injected email tricks the agent into summarizing it into something different. This is a non-goal of @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign but it is a serious concern if we consider content with mixed sources.
- **Data-flow → control-flow**: if the user query instructs the agent to follow instructions found in data (e.g. "do the action specified in this email"), Dual LLM isolation is bypassed entirely and an attacker gains arbitrary code execution by controlling the data.
- **Side channels**: private data can leak via loop iteration counts over external requests, exception-based 1-bit leaks, or timing channels. STRICT mode mitigates the first two but not all cases.
- **Manual policy maintenance**: security policies must be written and maintained by developers; no automatic derivation from context.
- **User fatigue**: overly strict policies require frequent user confirmation, which can lead to desensitization and inadvertent approval of malicious actions.
- **Ecosystem dependency**: all tools must support capabilities; third-party tools not designed with this in mind require the agent to act as a central capability authority.
- **No atomicity / rollback**: side-effecting tool calls already executed before a failure cannot be undone.
- **Redacted exceptions degrade recoverability**: when an exception's content depends on untrusted data, it is redacted before being shown to the P-LLM. The P-LLM must guess the cause of failure, increasing the likelihood of exhausting the re-prompt budget without recovering.
- **Hard re-prompt limit**: the P-LLM is given at most 10 attempts to produce working code; if all fail the task is abandoned entirely.
- **Exceptions break capability tracking**: using exceptions for error signaling is structurally at odds with dependency propagation — an exception terminates execution and severs the dependency chain, enabling side-channel leaks (see Side Channels §2). The proper fix would require replacing exceptions with explicit result types, which in turn requires a different host language.
- **Token overhead**: ~3× more tokens than native tool calling.
- **ROP-like attacks**: an attacker may chain individually-allowed control flow blocks to approximate a malicious program, analogously to Return-Oriented Programming attacks. Examples: a policy allows reading files and a policy allows sending emails to contacts — an attacker crafts a sequence of individually-benign steps (read file A, extract a substring, look up a contact, send a reminder) that together exfiltrate data without any single step being blocked; or a malicious program is split across multiple emails sent one at a time, each containing one innocuous instruction, so the agent never sees the full intent at once.



### Side Channels

CaMeL is vulnerable to side channels (a known limitation of capability-based systems):

1. **Indirect inference** — attacker makes the agent fetch an external resource N times conditional on a private variable; request count reveals the value. **Mitigated by STRICT mode.**
2. **Exception-based 1-bit leak** — attacker injects Q-LLM to raise an exception conditional on private data; if execution halts before an observable side-effect, adversary learns one bit. **Mitigated by STRICT mode.** Long-term fix: replace exceptions with explicit result types (Rust's `Result`, Haskell's `Either`).
3. **Timing side channel** — `time.sleep(private_var)` leaks value via elapsed time. Not currently exploitable (`time` module excluded from CaMeL's Python subset).

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | ASR ↓ | CaMeL (no policies) | 2/949 | Native tool-calling API (high ASR) | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Both residual attacks outside CaMeL's threat model (data-flow→control-flow; text-to-text); source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | ASR ↓ | CaMeL (with policies) | 1/949 | Native tool-calling API (high ASR) | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Residual attack outside threat model (travel-suite print injection); source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | Utility ↑ | CaMeL | 77% | ~84% undefended | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | ~7pp utility cost; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | Token overhead ↓ | CaMeL | 2.82× input / 2.73× output | 1× native tool-calling | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Median task; highest token overhead among all compared defences; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | Successful attacks ↓ | Spotlighting | ~5 (±0.6) | ~44 undefended | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Comparison baseline run alongside CaMeL in Table 7; CaMeL (no policies) achieves 2 vs Spotlighting's ~5; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | ASR ↓ | Tool Filter only | 8/949 | Native tool-calling API (high ASR) | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Best non-CaMeL defence; CaMeL (no policies) outperforms with 2/949; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |
| AgentDojo (949 attacks) | Successful attacks ↓ | CaMeL (no policies) | 0/949 | ~300 undefended | Gemini 2.5 Pro | Cross-model result; CaMeL eliminates all attacks on Gemini 2.5 Pro backbone; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Role Separation\|Role Separation (Dual LLM)]] | The Dual LLM pattern protects control flow but not data flow; CaMeL adds capability-tracked data flow and security policies to close this gap. |
| outperforms:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters]] | On AgentDojo (Claude 3.5 Sonnet), CaMeL (no policies) allows 2 successful attacks vs Spotlighting's ~5 (Table 7); CaMeL additionally provides structural guarantees that encoding-only approaches like Spotlighting cannot offer; source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign. |

---

## Beyond Prompt Injection

CaMeL also defends against stronger threat models:

- **Spy tools**: a malicious tool documentation-prompt-injects the agent to forward all processed data → blocked by policies preventing external tools from reading private data
- **Rogue/compromised users**: a user modifies a prompt to exfiltrate documents → blocked by corporate security policies enforced by CaMeL

nodes.name: Defence-in-Depth Architectures
nodes.content:
# Defence-in-Depth Architectures

System-level designs and practitioner guidance that combine several hardening layers around an LLM-integrated system — such as sanitization, parameterized tool invocation, privilege restriction, sandboxing, human confirmation, plugin vetting, and exfiltration controls. Unlike the more narrowly scoped mechanism families elsewhere under Architectural Isolation, these sources describe multi-control architectures or security design patterns rather than a single enforcement primitive.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@harang2023securing\|Securing LLM Systems Against Prompt Injection]] | Treats LLM outputs as attacker-controlled when untrusted text can reach the prompt, then recommends sanitizing inputs and outputs, strictly parameterizing downstream calls, applying least privilege, and avoiding risky cross-plug-in compositions. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@hamiel2023reducing\|Reducing The Impact of Prompt Injection Attacks Through Design]] | Presents the practitioner RRT pattern — Refrain, Restrict, Trap — by avoiding unnecessary LLM use, sharply limiting permissions and autonomy, and validating risky inputs and outputs to reduce blast radius. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@willison2023recommendations\|Recommendations to help mitigate prompt injection]] | Practitioner guidance that treats prompt injection as an unsolved condition and limits blast radius instead: remove or tightly constrain exfiltration paths, allow-list trusted links, and red-team workflows for user-mediated leakage. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@beurerkellner2025designpatternssecuringllm\|Design Patterns for Securing LLM Agents (Beurer-Kellner et al.)]] | Catalogues six composable architectural design patterns (Action-Selector, Plan-Then-Execute, LLM Map-Reduce, Dual LLM, Code-Then-Execute, Context-Minimization) that together provide structural resistance to prompt injection across varied agent designs. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@paverd2025how\|How Microsoft Defends Against Indirect Prompt Injection (Paverd)]] | Documents Microsoft's production three-layer architecture that combines prompt-level prevention and detection with deterministic impact-mitigation controls such as human confirmation and exfiltration-channel blocking. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@rai2024guardian\|GUARDIAN]] | Wraps Llama-2 in three sequential safeguards: a system-prompt ethical reminder, a BERT classifier that can divert unsafe inputs to an ethical rewrite generator, and a final harmful-output screen before display. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@alzahrani2026promptguard\|PromptGuard]] | Combines four runtime layers in one black-box-compatible pipeline: regex + MiniBERT input screening, JSON/ChatML role separation, LLM-as-critic output validation, and Adaptive Response Refinement before release. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@ramakrishnan2025securingaiagentsprompt\|Securing AI Agents Against Prompt Injection Attacks]] | Proposes a three-layer RAG-agent stack that first filters retrieved content with embedding- and keyword-based screening, then rebuilds the prompt with hierarchical guardrails and document boundaries, and finally verifies generated answers before release. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@deng2026tamingopenclawsecurityanalysis\|OpenClaw Five-Layer Architecture (Deng et al.)]] | Proposes a lifecycle-spanning architecture for OpenClaw that layers plugin vetting, privilege-separated input handling, memory protection, formal plan verification, and isolated execution. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@openai2025promptinjections\|OpenAI Multi-Layer Defence (OpenAI)]] | Describes OpenAI's deployed five-layer stack spanning safety training, automated monitors, sandboxing, confirmation gates, and attack-surface reduction across multiple products. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@neelou2025a2asagenticairuntime\|A2AS]] | Proposes a modular runtime security framework for agentic systems that combines behavior certificates, authenticated prompts, security boundaries, in-context defenses, and codified policies into one deployable self-defense layer. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@openai2026lockdownmode\|Lockdown Mode & Elevated Risk Labels (OpenAI)]] | Adds enterprise capability restriction and explicit residual-risk labelling on top of OpenAI's broader defence stack, reducing exposure by disabling higher-risk features and limiting browsing behaviour. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable\|NeMo Guardrails]] | Open-source programmable guardrail runtime that lets developers define conversational, retrieval, execution, and output rails in Colang, combining LLM checks with symbolic flow control inside one deployable middleware layer. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@rajpal2023guardrailsai\|Guardrails AI]] | Open-source runtime guardrail middleware that combines input guards, output guards, and schema-constrained structured generation so applications can validate or reject model interactions before trusting them downstream. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@smith2025webuiltsecuritylayermcp\|mcp-context-protector]] | MCP wrapper/proxy that layers trust-on-first-use pinning of server metadata, optional guardrail scanning with quarantine/release, and ANSI escape-sequence sanitization around downstream MCP servers without requiring changes to host apps or servers. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@vijil2026agenttrustplatform\|Vijil AI Agent Trust Platform]] | Enterprise lifecycle platform that combines hardened starting components (Depot), pre-deployment evaluation (Diamond), production runtime policy enforcement (Dome), and telemetry-driven improvement (Darwin) instead of exposing only a single detector. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@chaudhry2025securing\|Securing Amazon Bedrock Agents (Chaudhry et al.)]] | Describes a seven-control defence-in-depth architecture for Bedrock Agents that combines prompt tagging, guardrails, plan verification, confirmation gates, least-privilege sandboxing, and monitoring. |

## Advantages

- Layers deterministic blast-radius controls around the model, so a single prompt-level failure need not become a high-privilege system action.
- Applies to closed-weight and commercial models because the main controls live in orchestration, tool mediation, and surrounding infrastructure.
- Captures practical engineering risks that narrow mechanism papers often omit, including plug-in composition, exfiltration channels, and privilege scoping.

## Limitations

- Most sources are broad design guidance or framework papers rather than narrowly specified algorithms with controlled head-to-head benchmarks.
- Deployment is application-specific and can require substantial engineering, policy writing, sandboxing, and security review.
- Capability restriction, confirmation gates, and conservative allow-lists can reduce automation or product functionality.
- End-to-end assurance still depends on correct configuration of every layer; cross-tool and cross-plugin trust interactions remain hard to reason about.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LangChain chain case studies | Vulnerabilities identified ↓ | Harang systems hardening guidance | 3 | — | LLMs (unspecified) | Guidance is derived from three verified vulnerable chain patterns leading to RCE, SSRF, and SQL injection; source: @harang2023securing |
| `llm_math` | CVSS ↓ | Harang systems hardening guidance | 9.8 | — | LLMs (unspecified) | Vulnerable Python-evaluating chain motivating strict parameterization and least privilege; source: @harang2023securing |
| Anthropic Red-Teaming + Helpful (balanced 200) | Harmful prompts blocked ↑ | NeMo Guardrails input + output moderation rails | close to 99% | gpt-3.5-turbo without rails: 93% | gpt-3.5-turbo | Helpful-request blocking was 2%; Appendix G.2.1; source: @rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable |
| Anthropic Red-Teaming + Helpful (balanced 200) | Helpful requests blocked ↓ | NeMo Guardrails input + output moderation rails | 2% | gpt-3.5-turbo without rails: 0% | gpt-3.5-turbo | Safety/utility trade-off; Appendix G.2.1; source: @rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable |
| Custom jailbreak dataset (50 attacks) | Attack prompts blocked ↑ | Full GUARDIAN (3 layers) | 100% | 0% blocked undefended | Llama-2-7b-chat | 50/50 blocked on the authors' small custom jailbreak set; authors caution this may not generalize; no clean-utility evaluation reported; source: @rai2024guardian |
| Unseen attacks | ASR % ↓ | PromptGuard full pipeline | 4.5% | 28.1% no defence | GPT-4, Claude 3, LLaMA 2 (aggregate / model-unspecified) | Integrated end-to-end summary for the four-layer black-box-compatible pipeline; source: @alzahrani2026promptguard |
| In-paper prompt injection benchmark (847 cases) | ASR ↓ | Full defense | 8.7% | 73.2% baseline RAG | 7 models (aggregate) | Three-layer RAG-agent stack; headline end-to-end result with an 88.1% relative reduction from baseline; source: @ramakrishnan2025securingaiagentsprompt |
| MMLU, HellaSwag, and domain-specific QA | Task performance retention ↑ | Full defense | 94.3% | 100.0% baseline RAG | 7 models (aggregate) | Utility remains high despite the full stack; benign retrieval false-positive rate is 5.7%; source: @ramakrishnan2025securingaiagentsprompt |
| Qualitative framework comparison (Appendix A) | Guardrail capability coverage ↑ | Guardrails AI | — | Llama Guard; NeMo Guardrails | Various | Reported in @dong2024buildingguardrailslargelanguage; compared qualitatively on monitoring rules, enforcement, multimodal support, output checking, and scalability. |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | OpenAI Multi-Layer Defence | — | — | GPT-family (unspecified) | Deployed five-layer practitioner stack; no quantitative evaluation reported; source: @openai2025promptinjections |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Refrain, Restrict, and Trap (RRT) design guidance | — | — | LLM-integrated applications | Qualitative practitioner guidance only; recommends avoiding unnecessary LLM use, sharply restricting permissions and autonomy, and trapping risky inputs/outputs; source: @hamiel2023reducing |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Amazon Bedrock Agents multi-layer defence | — | — | Amazon Bedrock (unspecified) | Seven-control practitioner architecture; no quantitative evaluation reported; source: @chaudhry2025securing |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Vijil AI Agent Trust Platform | — | — | — | Public product pages describe Depot, Diamond, Dome, and Darwin, but publish no reproducible benchmark numbers for the platform as a whole; third-party results concern only one embedded prompt-injection guardrail component, not the full stack; source: @vijil2026agenttrustplatform |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Structured Queries\|Structured Queries]] | PromptGuard shows how a defence-in-depth pipeline can take structured instruction/data separation as one inner layer, then extend it with front-door screening and post-generation validation instead of relying on the structured interface alone. |
| embeds:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Classifier-Based Detection\|Classifier-Based Detection]] | GUARDIAN shows that a defence-in-depth stack can use a trained prompt classifier as only one inner layer inside a broader pipeline that also adds prompt-level hardening and post-generation checks. |
| embeds:: [[Detection/Response-Space Detection/Output Screening\|Output Screening]] | Ramakrishnan and Balaji's RAG defence ends with a response-verification layer that inspects the generated answer for suspicious disclosure patterns before release, showing how defence-in-depth architectures can include response-space screening as a final containment layer. |
| embeds:: [[Architectural Isolation/Structured Output Validation\|Structured Output Validation]] | Guardrails AI shows that a broader guardrail middleware can include schema-constrained generation and deterministic output validation as one inner control alongside input and output guards. |
| embeds:: [[Architectural Isolation/Instruction Authentication\|Instruction Authentication]] | A2AS shows that a defence-in-depth runtime can include authenticated prompts as one inner control, using prompt-integrity metadata alongside certificates, boundaries, and policy enforcement rather than relying on authentication alone. |

nodes.name: Execution Isolation
nodes.content:
# Execution Isolation

Confines each third-party app in an agentic system to its own isolated process (a *spoke*), with all cross-app interactions mediated by a trusted central hub. Because no spoke's LLM context is visible to any other spoke, a prompt injection attack that successfully hijacks one app cannot propagate instructions or data exfiltration requests to other apps or to the system at large.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wu2025isolategptexecutionisolationarchitecture\|IsolateGPT]] | Proposes a hub-and-spoke architecture in which each app runs in an OS-isolated process with its own LLM instance; cross-app requests are routed through a non-LLM hub that validates format, checks against the user's original query, and surfaces a user permission dialog for unexpected actions. |

## Advantages

- Containment by design: a compromised app's injected instructions are confined to its own process and cannot alter other apps or the hub.
- No functionality loss: IsolateGPT matches a non-isolated baseline on all tested benchmarks.
- Hub-mediated screening uses the trusted user query and vetted app descriptions as a reference, making manipulation non-trivial.
- Format validation of inter-spoke messages allows the hub to automatically reject malformed or unexpected cross-app requests without user involvement.
- Protects against both adversarial prompt injection and inadvertent safety issues from natural language ambiguity (conflicting app instructions, unintended data sharing).

## Limitations

- Performance overhead: IsolateGPT adds latency due to extra planning, memory extraction, and inter-process communication; overhead exceeds 30% for ~24% of queries (90th percentile: 1.24×, 95th: 1.80×); cost is ~1.85× higher for 10% of queries.
- Overhead scales with the number of apps involved; multi-app collaboration is most expensive.
- The permission model requires user involvement for cross-app messages, risking approval fatigue; a fully automated permission model remains an open problem.
- Attacks *within* a single app are explicitly out of scope.
- The inter-spoke communication protocol does not fully prevent side-channel inference (e.g., response timing or format fingerprinting).
- Evaluation uses GPT-4 (v0613) and LangChain benchmarks; generalisability to other LLMs is unknown.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| InjecAgent (extended, 1,598 attacks) | Attack success rate (↓) | IsolateGPT | 7.6% (permission warning rate) | 20.2% VanillaGPT | GPT-4 (v0613) | 100% of permission dialogs for attack flows included a warning; success requires user to grant permission; benchmark: @zhan2024injecagentbenchmarkingindirectprompt |
| InjecAgent — App compromise (financial harm) | Attack success rate (↓) | IsolateGPT | 0.0% | 9.8% VanillaGPT | GPT-4 | N=153; benchmark: @zhan2024injecagentbenchmarkingindirectprompt |
| InjecAgent — App data stealing (financial data) | Attack success rate (↓) | IsolateGPT | 19.1% | 33.0% VanillaGPT | GPT-4 | N=102; residual success requires user to grant malicious permission; benchmark: @zhan2024injecagentbenchmarkingindirectprompt |
| LangChain benchmarks — All app categories | Correctness (↑) | IsolateGPT | 1.00 (single app), 1.00 (multi-app), 0.95 (multi-app collab.) | VanillaGPT: identical | GPT-4 | No functionality loss vs non-isolated baseline |
| LangChain benchmarks — Query resolution time | Overhead vs baseline (↓) | IsolateGPT | <30% overhead for 75.73% of queries | VanillaGPT (1×) | GPT-4 | 90th pct: 1.24×; 95th pct: 1.80× |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Role Separation\|Role Separation (Dual LLM)]] | Role Separation confines untrusted data to a single quarantined LLM instance within a shared system; Execution Isolation extends this to multi-app settings by giving each app its own fully isolated process, preventing cross-app injection propagation that a single Q-LLM boundary cannot stop. |

nodes.id: Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Information Flow Control
nodes.name: Information Flow Control
nodes.path: Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Information Flow Control.md
nodes.content:
# Information Flow Control

Defences that intercept agent tool calls at runtime and block any call whose parameters are influenced — directly or transitively — by untrusted external content, as determined by a formal information-flow analysis. Rather than isolating model instances or restricting the execution environment, these techniques instrument the agent's own runtime: they track how data propagates from external observations through the agent's reasoning to tool-call parameters, then apply a security policy (a privilege specification or a confidentiality/integrity lattice) to decide whether each call is permitted. The policy check runs before execution, so injected instructions cannot trigger privileged actions even if the model's reasoning has already been influenced by them. **Boundary criterion:** IFC defences are distinguished by their reliance on *data-provenance tracking* — they decide safety by tracing where data came from, rather than primarily evaluating data content (though some hybrid implementations, such as AgentSpec and ShieldAgent, combine provenance tracking with content-level enforcement).

The two main enforcement paradigms — taint-tracking (propagate security labels through data flows) and policy-specification (compile declarative rules into enforcement monitors) — differ in authoring model and failure mode: taint-tracking systems require labelling data and risk label creep, while policy-specification systems require writing rules and risk policy incompleteness. Most practical systems include elements of both, but are classified by their primary mechanism.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Taint-Tracking IFC\|Taint-Tracking IFC]] | Propagates security labels (integrity levels, confidentiality classes, or scalar trust scores) through the agent's data flow and blocks tool calls whose effective label violates a lattice constraint; includes PDG-based analysis (AgentArmor), variable-level taint tracking (FIDES), dependency screening (RTBAS), subset enumeration (Permissive IFC), and Bayesian trust levels (SAFEFLOW). |
| down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Policy-Specification IFC\|Policy-Specification IFC]] | Compiles developer-authored declarative policies (Z3 schemas, Datalog rules, linear-trace constraints, or Markov logic) into enforcement monitors that deterministically block policy-violating tool calls; includes Progent, PCAS, Invariant Guardrails, AgentSpec, and ShieldAgent. |

## Advantages

- Operates entirely at runtime by hooking the execution trace; requires no model fine-tuning and no changes to the backbone LLM or the agent's core execution pipeline.
- Dependency-aware enforcement: the policy check is grounded in a formal model of how data actually propagated through the agent's reasoning, not in surface-level heuristics.
- Model-agnostic: applicable across arbitrary backbone LLMs.
- Provable, deterministic security (Progent, FIDES, PCAS): blocked tool calls cannot execute regardless of LLM behaviour.
- Negligible to moderate runtime overhead depending on approach: Progent adds ≈0.0008 s per task; AgentArmor's PDG construction is heavier.
- Unified integrity and confidentiality defence (RTBAS, SAFEFLOW): the same framework simultaneously blocks injected commands and accidental privacy leakage.

## Limitations

- Manual policy authoring or label assignment requires upfront effort to enumerate trust boundaries per deployment; completeness is not guaranteed against novel attack vectors.
- LLM-based dependency reasoning (AgentArmor) and LLM-assisted policy generation (Progent-LLM) may themselves be manipulated by a sufficiently adaptive attacker.
- Static policy specifications may be over-restrictive, reducing utility on legitimate tasks; dynamic approaches (Progent self-modifying policies, SAFEFLOW Bayesian trust) mitigate but do not eliminate this.
- Text-to-text attacks are not blocked: policies are enforced only on tool calls; prompt injections that shape only the agent's text response without triggering a tool call are outside scope.
- Security guarantees depend entirely on correct labelling (RTBAS) or correct policy specification (Progent, PCAS): bad policies negate all protection.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | FIDES★ (Taint-Tracking) | 1/949 (0 policy-relevant) | 163/949 Basic (undefended) | gpt-4o | Taint-tracking with label lattice; source: @costa2025securingaiagentsinformationflow |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | AgentArmor (Taint-Tracking) | 3% | 17% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | PDG-based; 1% utility loss; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| ASB (OPI) | ASR ↓ | AgentArmor (Taint-Tracking) | 0% | 41–50% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Perfect detection; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Progent (Policy-Specification) | 0% | 39.9% no defence | GPT-4o | Deterministic; provably guaranteed; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | Utility (no attack) ↑ | Progent (Policy-Specification) | 79.4% | 79.4% no defence | GPT-4o | Zero utility loss; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo (weighted avg, under attack) | ASR (↓) | RTBAS LM-Judge (Taint-Tracking) | 0% | ~92% attacks succeed undefended | GPT-4o | IFC lattice enforcement; <2% utility loss under attack; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| Accidental Leakage (37 tasks) | FPR ↓ | RTBAS LM-Judge (Taint-Tracking) | 8.1% | 29.7% GPTs baseline | GPT-4o | Privacy-leakage defence; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| MTST (SafeFlowBench) | Accunsafe ↓ | SAFEFLOW (Taint-Tracking) | 0.0% | 56–79% undefended | 10 VLMs | Multimodal adversarial scenarios; source: @li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy |
| τ²-bench (90 trials) | Compliance (↑) | PCAS (Policy-Specification) | 93% | 48% non-instrumented | Claude Opus 4.5 / GPT-5.2 / Gemini 3 Pro | Datalog policy enforcement; source: @palumbo2026policycompilersecureagentic |
| ASB | ASR ↓ | Progent (Policy-Specification) | 0% | 70.3% no defence | GPT-4o | Malicious-tool scenario; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Code-Then-Execute/Sandboxed Script Execution/Sandboxed Script Execution\|Sandboxed Script Execution]] | Sandboxed Script Execution (CaMeL) enforces data-flow policies via capability tracking in a sandboxed interpreter; Information Flow Control applies the same intuition directly to the live execution trace via PDG analysis, requiring no sandboxed interpreter and preserving far more utility (72% vs. 48% on AgentDojo). |
| outperforms:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters]] | Progent achieves 0% ASR on AgentDojo (GPT-4o) vs Spotlighting's 23.3% in the same head-to-head comparison; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol. |
| outperforms:: [[Prompt Massaging/Instruction Reinforcement\|Instruction Reinforcement]] | Progent achieves 0% ASR on AgentDojo (GPT-4o) vs the Sandwich defence's 25.1% in the same head-to-head comparison; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol. |

nodes.name: Policy-Specification IFC
nodes.content:
# Policy-Specification IFC

Defences that compile developer-authored declarative policies — Z3 constraint schemas, Datalog rules, linear-trace constraints, or rule-based trace specifications — into enforcement monitors that intercept and evaluate every tool call against the policy set before execution. The common mechanism is *policy-check-before-execution*: developers write rules describing permitted actions, and a reference monitor deterministically blocks any call that violates the specification. Approaches differ in policy language expressiveness and enforcement mechanism: SMT-backed JSON Schema (Progent), Datalog over causal dependency graphs (PCAS), rule-based trace constraints (Invariant Guardrails), linear-trace constraint language (AgentSpec), and probabilistic Markov logic over policy documents (ShieldAgent).

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol\|Progent]] | Enforces developer-specified privilege policies at the tool-call boundary via a Z3 SMT-backed proxy: each call is evaluated against a JSON Schema policy set before execution, providing provable, deterministic security guarantees with negligible overhead; a companion Progent-LLM mode generates per-query policies automatically. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@palumbo2026policycompilersecureagentic\|PCAS]] | Compiles operator-authored Datalog policies against a monotonically growing causal dependency graph covering the entire agent execution; a reference monitor intercepts every tool call and blocks policy-violating actions deterministically — achieving 93% compliance on τ²-bench (vs 48% undefended) and 0% ASR on ad-hoc prompt injection trials across three frontier models. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@invariantlabs2024invariant\|Invariant Guardrails]] | Open-source rule-based framework for specifying trace-level data-flow requirements and tool-call restrictions over a single LLM agent's execution history; predecessor to PCAS's cross-agent causal dependency model. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2025agentspeccustomizableruntimeenforcement\|AgentSpec]] | Customizable runtime enforcement for LLM agents using a linear-trace constraint language; achieves >90% prevention of unsafe code-agent executions. Cited here for its linear-trace constraint language and information-flow policies; also cited under Detection > Agent Execution Analysis > Action-Level Guardrails for its action-level trigger/check/enforce rules. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable\|ShieldAgent]] | Shields agents via verifiable safety policy reasoning backed by probabilistic Markov logic; achieves 90% rule recall on its own benchmark. Uses probabilistic (not deterministic) policy evaluation, but shares the core mechanism of checking agent actions against declarative policy documents at runtime. Also cited under Detection > Agent Execution Analysis > Action-Level Guardrails for its per-action compliance verdicts via rule circuits. |

## Advantages

- Provable, deterministic security (Progent, PCAS): blocked tool calls cannot execute regardless of LLM behaviour; Z3 SMT-based conflict detection (Progent) and Datalog policy compilation (PCAS) both provide hard guarantees.
- Negligible runtime overhead (Progent): policy enforcement adds ≈0.0008 s per task, three orders of magnitude below average agent execution time.
- Dynamic policy updates (Progent): policies can self-modify after a triggering tool call, enabling context-aware privilege tightening at runtime without redeployment.
- Cross-agent causal tracking (PCAS): the causal dependency graph spans multiple agents and tools, enabling policy enforcement in multi-agent workflows — a capability single-agent frameworks lack.
- Verifiable safety reasoning (ShieldAgent): Markov-logic-backed rule circuits provide probabilistic guarantees with explainable verdicts grounded in policy documents.

## Limitations

- Static policy specifications (Progent) may be over-restrictive and context-unaware, reducing utility even on legitimate tasks; static Progent achieves 64% utility vs. 72% for AgentArmor's flow-aware approach on AgentDojo (GPT-4o-mini).
- Manual policy authoring (Progent, PCAS, Invariant Guardrails) requires upfront effort to enumerate permitted actions per deployment; completeness is not guaranteed against novel or unanticipated attack vectors.
- LLM-assisted policy generation (Progent-LLM) may itself be manipulated by a sufficiently adaptive attacker; quality degrades with weaker backbone LLMs.
- Probabilistic enforcement (ShieldAgent) does not provide the hard guarantees of deterministic approaches; rule recall is 90%, leaving a 10% gap.
- Policy languages require developer expertise: writing correct Z3 schemas, Datalog rules, or linear-trace constraints demands familiarity with formal methods that most application developers lack.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Progent (manual policies) | 0% | 39.9% no defence | GPT-4o | Provably guaranteed via deterministic policy enforcement; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | Utility (no attack) ↑ | Progent (manual policies) | 79.4% | 79.4% no defence | GPT-4o | Zero utility loss; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Progent-LLM | 1.0% | 39.9% no defence | GPT-4o | Per-query LLM-generated and dynamically updated policies; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | Utility (no attack) ↑ | Progent-LLM | 76.3% | 79.4% no defence | GPT-4o | Small utility trade-off vs manual policies; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Progent (manual policies) | 2% | 17% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Fig. 8(e); source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo | Utility no attack (UAR) ↑ | Progent | 64% | 73% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Fig. 8(j); 9% utility drop vs. AgentArmor's 1%; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Progent (manual policies) | 0% | 39.9% no defence | claude-sonnet-4 | Effective across all tested agent LLMs (Fig. 8); source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| ASB | ASR ↓ | Progent (manual policies) | 0% | 70.3% no defence | GPT-4o | Malicious-tool attack scenario; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| AgentPoison (EHRAgent) | ASR ↓ | Progent (manual policies) | 0% | 54.0% no defence | GPT-4o | Knowledge-base poisoning attack; source: @shi2025progentprogrammableprivilegecontrol |
| τ²-bench (combined, 90 trials) | Compliance (↑) | PCAS instrumented | 93% | 48% non-instrumented | Claude Opus 4.5 / GPT-5.2 / Gemini 3 Pro | Causal dependency graph + Datalog policy enforcement; average across airline and retail tasks and all three models; source: @palumbo2026policycompilersecureagentic |
| Prompt injection (ad-hoc, 5 trials) | ASR (↓) | PCAS – Toxic Flow | 0/5 | 5/5 non-instrumented | GPT-4.1-mini | Taint propagation blocks external exfiltration email after untrusted + sensitive read; 5/5 benign tasks unaffected; source: @palumbo2026policycompilersecureagentic |
| MALADE pharmacovigilance (15 trials) | Accuracy (↑) | PCAS instrumented | 15/15 | 14/15 non-instr. | GPT-4.1 | Multi-agent approval workflow; 42 unauthorized FDA API accesses blocked in non-instrumented runs; source: @palumbo2026policycompilersecureagentic |
| AgentDojo | Information-flow policy expressiveness ↑ | Invariant Guardrails | Supports trace-level data-flow rules; no cross-agent causal dependencies | — | — | Reported in @palumbo2026policycompilersecureagentic |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Taint-Tracking IFC
nodes.content:
# Taint-Tracking IFC

Defences that propagate security labels — integrity levels, confidentiality classes, or scalar trust scores — through the agent's data flow, then block any tool call whose effective label violates a lattice constraint. The common mechanism is *label propagation*: every observation or variable inherits the security label of its data sources, and a runtime check compares the resulting label against the target tool's policy before allowing execution. Approaches differ in *how* the label is computed: full Program Dependence Graph analysis (AgentArmor), variable-level taint tracking in a planner (FIDES), LLM-judge or attention-based saliency screening (RTBAS), influence-based subset enumeration (Permissive IFC), or scalar trust levels updated via Bayesian estimation (SAFEFLOW).

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis\|AgentArmor]] | Hooks the agent runtime to capture the full execution trace, builds a Program Dependence Graph (PDG) encoding both control flow and data flow across all messages, annotates each node with a confidentiality/integrity security type via lattice propagation, and blocks any tool call whose parameters violate lattice constraints — achieving near-zero ASR on ASB and 3–4% ASR on AgentDojo with only 1% utility loss. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@costa2025securingaiagentsinformationflow\|FIDES]] | Instruments a variable-passing agent planner with dynamic taint-tracking over a confidentiality/integrity label lattice; conceals untrusted data behind opaque handles (selective hiding), exposes it through capacity-limited LLM queries (constrained inspection), and deterministically blocks any tool call that violates a trusted-action or permitted-flow policy — achieving 0 policy-relevant attacks on AgentDojo while preserving or improving task completion relative to an undefended planner on reasoning models. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents\|RTBAS]] | Wraps every agent generation step with a dependency screener (LM-judge or attention-based saliency classifier) that identifies which labelled history regions influenced the pending tool call, joins their security labels, and checks the result against a per-tool integrity/confidentiality policy; a redactor masks irrelevant tainted regions to avoid label creep, and the system escalates to user confirmation only when a policy violation is detected — defending against both prompt injection and accidental privacy leakage with <2% utility loss on AgentDojo. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@siddiqui2026permissiveinformationflowanalysislarge\|Permissive IFC for LLMs]] | Proposes a permissive information-flow analysis for LLMs that enumerates relevant input subsets to reduce label creep compared with naive taint propagation; introduced the dependency-subset enumeration concept that RTBAS later replaced with a constant-overhead single-pass screener. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy\|SAFEFLOW]] | Assigns every agent entity and data item a scalar SF-Level trust label; three flow rules (Full Trust, Skeptical Read, No Access) gate all information transitions through a Verifier component; adds transactional write-ahead logging with DAG-based rollback and a global mutex concurrency system for multi-agent coordination — achieving 0% unsafe-action rate across ten VLM backbones on multimodal adversarial scenarios. |

## Advantages

- Dependency-aware enforcement: the policy check is grounded in a formal model of how data actually propagated through the agent's reasoning, not in surface-level heuristics.
- Selective hiding preserves utility (FIDES): only data that would raise the context label is concealed behind opaque handles, avoiding the steep utility losses of strict variable-passing; constrained LLM queries over hidden data further recover task completion.
- Two generic policies suffice (FIDES): a trusted-action policy (P-T) and a permitted-flow policy (P-F) applied uniformly across all tools block all policy-relevant attacks without per-tool customisation.
- Composable with enterprise infrastructure (FIDES): labels can be sourced from document-classification systems (Microsoft Purview, Google Workspace) and standard MCP tool annotations.
- Unified integrity and confidentiality defence (RTBAS): the same label-lattice framework simultaneously blocks injected commands (integrity violations) and accidental privacy leakage (confidentiality violations), achieving near-oracle leakage detection with <2% utility loss.
- Selective user confirmation (RTBAS): users are only prompted when a genuine policy violation is detected, avoiding the confirmation fatigue of confirm-every-call models.
- Label-creep avoidance (RTBAS): the redactor masks irrelevant tainted regions before generation, so only dependencies that genuinely contributed to the current output raise the context security label.
- End-to-end multimodal coverage (SAFEFLOW): SF-Level semantics apply equally to visual and textual tokens, blocking visually embedded prompt injections that bypass text-only filters — eliminating unsafe actions to 0% across ten VLM backbones.
- Dynamic Bayesian trust estimation (SAFEFLOW): entity trust scores adapt to runtime compliance history via a Beta-Bernoulli model, avoiding brittle hard-coded rules.
- Transactional recovery (SAFEFLOW): a write-ahead log and DAG-based rollback enable localised failure recovery in multi-step agentic tasks without cascading faults.

## Limitations

- Dynamic analysis is computationally expensive: PDG construction and LLM-based dependency inference (AgentArmor) dominate runtime and token overhead, giving higher latency than prompt-level methods.
- Manual label assignment (FIDES, RTBAS) requires upfront effort to enumerate trust boundaries per deployment; completeness is not guaranteed against novel or unanticipated attack vectors.
- LLM-based dependency reasoning (AgentArmor) may be manipulated by a sufficiently adaptive attacker; quality degrades with weaker backbone LLMs.
- Transfer-execution reasoning patterns — where a legitimate file or observation delegates control to a subsequent action — are difficult to distinguish from injected transfers without additional task-alignment context.
- Text-to-text attacks are not blocked (FIDES): policies are enforced only on tool calls; a prompt injection that shapes only the agent's final text response without triggering a tool call is outside scope.
- Non-interference for confidentiality is not achieved (FIDES): the paper deliberately settles for explicit secrecy, which does not prevent implicit leaks through the observable sequence of tool calls.
- Utility cost under reasoning models (FIDES): task completion rate drops up to 24.5% for o3/o4-mini when all policies are enforced, since the planner must correctly construct `query_llm` calls to access hidden data.
- Security guarantees depend entirely on correct labelling (RTBAS): if a developer mislabels high-integrity user input that already contains an injection, RTBAS cannot intercept it; bad policies negate all protection.
- High confirmation rate on tasks requiring untrusted content (RTBAS): tasks that inherently act on external data (e.g., inviting a contact found via web search) trigger frequent confirmations, reducing utility to 22–33% on the Slack suite.
- Attention screener accuracy is limited (RTBAS): the 81% offline accuracy of the attention-based classifier means real deployments have both false positives (unnecessary confirmations) and silent false negatives (missed dependencies).
- Verifier dependency (SAFEFLOW): the entire framework's security rests on the Verifier component — if the Verifier is compromised or unavailable, all flow-rule enforcement collapses.
- Cold-start trust instability (SAFEFLOW): the Beta-Bernoulli trust model requires a warm-up window of operations before scores stabilise; agent behaviour during cold-start is uncharacterised.
- Residual task-capability cost on weaker models (SAFEFLOW): Accgold for open-source models (e.g. 92.2% for Mistral Small 3.1) remains below 100%, and no ablation separates the contributions of IFC, transactional logging, and concurrency control individually.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | FIDES★ (with policy) | 1/949 (0 policy-relevant) | 163/949 Basic (undefended) | gpt-4o | ★ = policy checks enabled; source: @costa2025securingaiagentsinformationflow |
| AgentDojo | TCR ↑ | FIDES★ (prompt-tuned) | ~24% higher than Basic★ | Basic★ | o1/o3/o4-mini | Prompt tuning guides better query_llm usage; approaches human oracle performance; source: @costa2025securingaiagentsinformationflow |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | AgentArmor | 3% | 17% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Fig. 6(e) overall; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | AgentArmor | 4% | 28% no defence | GPT-4o | Table 2 all-attack avg; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo | Utility no attack (UAR) ↑ | AgentArmor | 72% | 73% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | 1% utility drop; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| ASB (OPI) | ASR ↓ | AgentArmor | 0% | 41–50% no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Perfect detection; source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis |
| AgentDojo (weighted avg, under attack) | Utility ↑ | RTBAS LM-Judge | ~99% | ~76% undefended | GPT-4o | <2% utility degradation vs. undefended; 0% attack success rate; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| AgentDojo (weighted avg, under attack) | Integrity ↑ | RTBAS LM-Judge | 100% | ~8% undefended | GPT-4o | Blocks all attacks violating IFC policy; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| AgentDojo (weighted avg, under attack) | Utility ↑ | RTBAS Attention-Based | ~97% | ~76% undefended | GPT-4o + Phi-3-Mini-128K | ~3% utility degradation; 0% attack success rate; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| AgentDojo (weighted avg, no attack) | Utility ↑ | RTBAS LM-Judge | ~90% | ~100% undefended | GPT-4o | 10% utility degradation without attack; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| Accidental Leakage (authors' benchmark, 37 tasks) | FPR ↓ | RTBAS LM-Judge | 8.1% | 29.7% GPTs baseline | GPT-4o | Confidentiality lattice enforcement; Venmo, Flight Booking, Amazon tasks; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| Accidental Leakage (authors' benchmark, 37 tasks) | FNR ↓ | RTBAS LM-Judge | 10.8% | 51.4% Confirm-Never baseline | GPT-4o | Confirm-Never = always redact private data; source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents |
| MTST (SafeFlowBench) | Accunsafe ↓ | SAFEFLOW | 0.0% | 56–79% undefended | GPT-4.1 / Gemini 2.0 Flash / 10 VLMs | SF-Level labelling + Verifier gate; 332 multimodal adversarial scenarios; source: @li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy |
| MTST (SafeFlowBench) | Accgold ↑ | SAFEFLOW | 99.4% | 30.7% undefended | GPT-4.1 | Task success on multimodal adversarial scenarios; source: @li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy |
| AgentHarm | Harm Score ↓ | SAFEFLOW | 0.0% | 56.4–82.2% undefended | GPT-4.1 | 440 adversarial task variants across 11 harmful categories; cross-benchmark generalisation; source: @li2025safeflowprincipledprotocoltrustworthy |
| Synthetic key-value (authors') | Exact match ↑ | Permissive IFC (Prompt-based LP) | 85.94% | 1.59% introspection baseline | Llama-2-Chat 7B | Influence-based label propagation; 14 docs/question, 16k possible labels; source: @siddiqui2026permissiveinformationflowanalysislarge |
| LLM agent (authors') | Label improvement ↑ | Permissive IFC (Prompt-based LP) | 85.0%–95.0% | 0% naïve LP | Llama-2 7B / 70B | Percentage of improvable cases assigned a more permissive label than naïve join; source: @siddiqui2026permissiveinformationflowanalysislarge |
