## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Instruction Authentication
nodes.content:
# Instruction Authentication

Defences that authenticate privileged instructions by rewriting them into special non-natural-language forms — secret aliases, signed tokens, or equivalent markers — and adapting the model so only those authenticated forms can trigger sensitive actions. Unlike Structured Queries, which separates instructions and data into explicit labelled regions, these methods keep content in a shared prompt but make authority depend on possession of the privileged encoding.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@suo2024signedpromptnewapproachprevent\|Signed-Prompt]] | Rewrites authorised commands into rare aliases before they reach the model, and prompts or fine-tunes the model so only the signed alias maps to the real privileged action while the plain-language form becomes invalid. |

## Advantages

- Gives the model an explicit authenticity signal instead of asking it to infer which instructions are trusted from context alone.
- Keeps attacker-written natural-language commands inert when they lack the authenticated form.
- Can be implemented with either prompt engineering or fine-tuning rather than requiring a new model architecture.

## Limitations

- Security depends on the authenticated aliases remaining secret; if they leak, attackers can replay them.
- Requires both an application-side encoder and a model that has been adapted to the privileged vocabulary.
- Current evidence is narrow: the main demonstration centres on a single sensitive command and a custom dataset rather than a broad agent benchmark.
- Legitimate-command reliability depends on encoder and model quality, especially for multilingual or implied requests.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Delete Command Dataset (all four groups) | Unauthorized command success ↓ | Signed-Prompt (LLM-PE) | 0% | — | ChatGPT-4 | Unsigned attacker commands never triggered the deletion command; source: @suo2024signedpromptnewapproachprevent |
| Delete Command Dataset (all four groups) | Authorised command correctness ↑ | Signed-Prompt (LLM-PE) | 100% | — | ChatGPT-4 | Prompt-engineered adjusted model supporting signed commands; source: @suo2024signedpromptnewapproachprevent |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters]] | Instruction authentication makes privileged actions depend on an authenticated alias rather than on the model noticing textual boundaries alone, so attacker-written natural-language commands remain invalid even inside mixed-content prompts. |

nodes.name: Role Separation
nodes.content:
# Role Separation

> **Conceptual anchor** — Role Separation names the foundational privilege-separation principle that most Architectural Isolation defences build on. Its single source is a design proposal; empirical validation comes through its descendants (Sandboxed Script Execution, Execution Isolation, Information Flow Control).

Separates agent execution into a Privileged LLM and a Quarantined LLM so that untrusted external data is never processed by the component that has tool-calling authority, limiting the blast radius of any injected instruction.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@willison2023dual\|Dual LLM pattern]] | Proposes the Dual LLM pattern: a Privileged LLM that plans and calls tools but never sees external data, paired with a Quarantined LLM that processes untrusted content but has no tool-calling authority. |

## Advantages

- Conceptual simplicity: the Privileged/Quarantined split is easy to reason about and communicate to non-specialist developers.
- Provides a clear trust boundary that subsequent techniques (CaMeL, IsolateGPT) extend and formalise.

## Limitations

- No data-flow protection: the Privileged LLM receives summarised results from the Quarantined LLM, so data-flow attacks (e.g., exfiltration via summarisation) are not addressed.
- No empirical evaluation: the proposal is a conceptual design pattern without quantitative ASR or utility measurements.
- Subsequent systems (Sandboxed Script Execution, Execution Isolation, Information Flow Control) each address specific gaps left by the basic Dual LLM split.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Role Separation / Dual LLM pattern | — | — | — | This node's direct source (@willison2023dual) is a conceptual design proposal without quantitative ASR, utility, or benchmark results; empirical validation appears in later descendant techniques rather than in Role Separation itself. |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters]] | Role Separation enforces the instruction/data boundary structurally via separate model instances, rather than relying on in-context markers that the model may ignore. |

nodes.name: Role-Separated Information Flow Control
nodes.content:
# Role-Separated Information Flow Control

Hybrid defences that combine a trusted/untrusted agent split with explicit information-flow enforcement at the boundary between those roles. Unlike plain Role Separation, these systems do not rely only on the untrusted side summarising data safely; they preserve trust labels or proxy references across the split and check privileged sinks before low-trust content can influence high-privilege tool use, trusted context, or the final answer.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@kim2025promptflowintegrity\|Prompt Flow Integrity (PFI)]] | Open-source framework that splits execution between trusted and untrusted agents, restricts the untrusted side's plugin access by policy, seals low-trust values behind proxy tokens, and raises warnings when untrusted data reaches privileged sinks. |

## Advantages

- Closes the summarisation/data-reuse gap left by plain dual-agent separation by carrying provenance across the trusted/untrusted boundary.
- Combines least-privilege tool separation with taint-style sink checks, so both execution authority and later data reuse are constrained.

## Limitations

- Requires both split-agent orchestration and correct trust-labelling / policy configuration.
- The reviewed implementation note exposes only a combined SUR headline and leaves the default enforcement action for unsafe flows under-specified.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | Secure-utility rate (SUR) ↑ | PFI | 61.86% | ReAct 12.37%; IsolateGPT 10.31%; f-secure 40.21% | GPT-4o | Head-to-head agent comparison from the paper/workbook; backs the `outperforms:: IsolateGPT` relation. |
| AgentBench OS | Secure-utility rate (SUR) ↑ | PFI | 68.42% | ReAct 0.00%; IsolateGPT 10.53%; f-secure 10.53% | GPT-4o | Head-to-head OS-suite comparison from the paper/workbook; this row also backs the `outperforms:: IsolateGPT` relation. |
| AgentDojo + AgentBench OS | Prompt-injection ASR ↓ | PFI | 0.00% in all reported runs | ReAct 3.78%-57.89% | Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 1.5 Pro | Zero prompt-injection success across both benchmarks and all four tested models. |
| AgentDojo + AgentBench OS | Context/data-injection ASR ↓ | PFI | 0.00% in all reported runs | ReAct 34.02%-58.62% | Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 1.5 Pro | Workbook labels this family "Context Injection"; the paper frames it as unsafe-data-flow / data-injection attacks. |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Architectural Isolation/Role Separation\|Role Separation]] | Adds trust-labeled sink checks to the basic privileged/quarantined split, addressing the data-flow gap that plain role separation leaves open once summarised or transferred content returns to the trusted side. |
| embeds:: [[Architectural Isolation/Information Flow Control/Taint-Tracking IFC\|Taint-Tracking IFC]] | Uses taint-style trusted/untrusted labels carried by sealed proxy values and checked before privileged sinks, so provenance enforcement runs inside the split-agent architecture rather than as a standalone runtime monitor. |

nodes.name: Structured Output Validation
nodes.content:
# Structured Output Validation

Defences that treat model-generated structured artifacts as untrusted until a deterministic validator has parsed them and checked them against developer-authored schemas or execution constraints. Instead of changing the prompt interface itself, these approaches place a policy-enforcement layer between the model and the downstream system, blocking, repairing, or re-asking when the output violates the allowed structure.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@rajpal2023guardrailsai\|Guardrails AI]] | Wraps LLM calls with schema-aware validators and guarded re-ask/repair logic so applications can require typed, structured outputs before accepting the model response. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@amoffat2023heimdallm\|HeimdaLLM]] | Parses LLM-generated SQL locally and enforces schema-aware constraints on joins, columns, functions, identity filters, and row limits, rejecting or rewriting unsafe queries before execution. |

## Advantages

- Enforces safety outside the model with deterministic parsing and validation rather than relying on the model to self-police.
- Fits high-risk downstream actions such as SQL execution or API payload generation, where only a narrow structured subset should ever be accepted.
- Can often fail closed or repair minor violations automatically before the output reaches the real system.

## Limitations

- Protection is only as strong as the supported grammar/schema and the correctness of the developer-authored constraints.
- Narrowly scoped validators may cover only specific output types, forcing separate guard layers for different tools or data formats.
- Staying within an allowed structure does not guarantee the action is harmless if the permitted schema itself is overbroad.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qualitative framework comparison (Appendix A) | Guardrail capability coverage ↑ | Guardrails AI | — | Llama Guard; NeMo Guardrails | Various | Reported in @dong2024buildingguardrailslargelanguage; compared qualitatively on monitoring rules, enforcement, multimodal support, output checking, and scalability. |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | HeimdaLLM | — | — | — | No quantitative benchmark, false-positive-rate, or attack-success-rate results are published in the repository README or public docs; the source provides worked examples and attack-surface analysis only. |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Structured Queries
nodes.content:
# Structured Queries

Defends against prompt injection by changing the *interface or representation* of queries so that trusted instructions and untrusted data are passed in distinct, explicitly typed regions or channels, rather than mixed together in one free-form prompt string.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@willison2022ultimately\|Simon Willison on instruction/data separation]] | Proposes the core architectural idea qualitatively: API providers should accept separate instructional and data prompt fields, analogous to parameterized SQL queries, so untrusted content is not mixed with executable instructions in a single string. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@chen2025struq\|StruQ]] | Proposes StruQ: a secure front-end that encodes queries with reserved tokens (stripped from all user data) combined with structured instruction tuning of a base LLM, achieving <2% ASR on 13 of 15 manual attack types with near-zero utility loss. |

## Advantages

- Addresses the root cause of prompt injection by making instruction/data separation part of the interface or query format, rather than relying only on prompt wording.
- Can strengthen or replace prompt-level boundary markers by giving the system an explicit structural distinction between trusted control text and untrusted content.

## Limitations

- Requires API, front-end, or model-interface changes; stronger variants may also require specialised training or reserved-token handling.
- Structural separation only helps if the downstream model or runtime actually respects the typed boundary, which purely conceptual proposals do not by themselves guarantee.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Instruction/data prompt separation | — | — | LLM APIs (concept only) | Qualitative API-design proposal only; no implementation or quantitative evaluation; source: @willison2022ultimately |
| Manual injection test suite (15 attack types) | ASR ↓ | StruQ | <2% (13/15 types) | ~50% undefended | Llama-7B / Mistral-7B | Near-zero utility loss; source: @chen2025struq |
| GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) | ASR ↓ | StruQ | 58% | 97% undefended | Llama-7B | Optimization-based attack; only partially mitigated; source: @chen2025struq; attack: @zou2023universaltransferableadversarialattacks |
| GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) | ASR ↓ | StruQ | 56% | 99% undefended | Mistral-7B | Optimization-based attack; source: @chen2025struq; attack: @zou2023universaltransferableadversarialattacks |
| TAP (Tree-of-Attacks with Pruning) | ASR ↓ | StruQ | 9% | 97% undefended | Llama-7B | source: @chen2025struq |
| AlpacaEval | Win rate ↑ | StruQ | 67.6% | 67.2% undefended | Llama-7B | Near-zero utility loss; source: @chen2025struq |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters (Spotlighting)]] | Structured Queries move the boundary from prompt-level markers inside one string to interface- or representation-level separation between instructions and data, reducing reliance on the model inferring that boundary from text alone; StruQ further strengthens this with training-time enforcement. |

nodes.name: AI Control Protocols
nodes.content:
# AI Control Protocols

A framework for constructing and evaluating safety pipelines (called *protocols*) that remain safe even when the powerful LLM they rely on is itself actively trying to subvert them. Rather than assuming model alignment, AI control designs for the adversarial case: the untrusted model U may be trying to cause harm, and the protocol must prevent it using a trusted but weaker model T, a limited budget of human oversight H, and pipeline-level techniques alone — no model retraining required. Safety is measured as the probability of completing an entire run of tasks without a single successfully submitted harmful output, and usefulness is the fraction of high-quality outputs retained relative to using U alone.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@greenblatt2024aicontrolimprovingsafety\|Greenblatt et al. 2024 (AI Control)]] | Introduces the AI control evaluation methodology and demonstrates that trusted monitoring, untrusted monitoring, trusted editing, dynamic defer-to-trusted routing, and human auditing can be composed into protocols achieving 62–98% safety against an adversarially optimised attack policy in the APPS backdooring setting, with 83–97% usefulness retained. |

## Advantages

- Achieves strong worst-case safety guarantees (62–98%) against an adversarially optimised attack policy without any model fine-tuning or white-box access.
- Protocols compose: trusted editing and untrusted monitoring can be combined to Pareto-dominate each technique individually across the safety/usefulness frontier.
- The control evaluation methodology (red team replacing U with an attack policy) provides a principled worst-case safety estimate applicable to any agentic setting, not only code generation.
- Signal jamming (redacting identifying features before self-monitoring) is an effective, broadly applicable countermeasure against model collusion.

## Limitations

- Evaluated only in the APPS code-backdooring setting — a homogeneous, well-defined task with a clear correctness oracle; generalisability to heterogeneous agentic tasks is unconfirmed.
- Auditing quality is assumed near-perfect; weaker auditors would require explicit modelling of auditor error rates.
- The framework is asymmetric: as untrusted models grow more capable than trusted models, the control gap widens and the framework explicitly does not claim to be a permanent solution.
- All protocols are arms-race prone: signal jamming, redaction, and paraphrasing can in principle be defeated by a sufficiently capable red team exploiting subtle residual signals.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| APPS backdooring (5,000 problems) | Safety ↑ (P(no backdoor submitted)) | Trusted monitoring | 62% | 15% (upfront auditing) | GPT-4 (U) + GPT-3.5 (T) | T flags top 2% suspicious solutions for human audit; red team constrained to subtle backdoors; usefulness 97%. Source: @greenblatt2024aicontrolimprovingsafety |
| APPS backdooring (5,000 problems) | Safety ↑ | Untrusted monitoring + defer to trusted 20% | 98% | 62% (trusted monitoring) | GPT-4 (U) + GPT-3.5 (T) | U monitors itself with input/output redaction to prevent collusion; submits T's solution for top 20% suspicious outputs; usefulness 85%. Source: @greenblatt2024aicontrolimprovingsafety |
| APPS backdooring (5,000 problems) | Safety ↑ | Trusted editing 50% | 96% | 62% (trusted monitoring) | GPT-4 (U) + GPT-3.5 (T) | T edits top 50% suspicious U solutions to remove backdoors before submission; usefulness 89%. Source: @greenblatt2024aicontrolimprovingsafety |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Action-Level Guardrails\|Action-Level Guardrails]] | Action-level guardrails intercept individual actions against a fixed or slowly updated safety policy; AI control protocols address the strictly harder adversarial case where the agent itself is actively trying to subvert the safety system, combining trusted monitoring, trusted editing, and human oversight into composable worst-case-safe pipelines. |

nodes.name: Action-Level Guardrails
nodes.content:
# Action-Level Guardrails

A dedicated safety layer interposes between the primary LLM agent and the environment, evaluating each proposed action against a set of safety checks before execution is permitted. Unlike static LLM gates that return a single malicious/benign verdict on input text, these defences operate at the action level and cover both task-specific risks (e.g. role-based access violations) and systemic risks (e.g. prompt injection, environment-dependent side-effects). **Boundary criterion:** action-level guardrails are distinguished by their reliance on *action/input content evaluation* — they decide safety by inspecting what the agent is about to do, rather than primarily tracing data provenance (though some hybrid approaches, such as AgentSpec and ShieldAgent, also incorporate provenance signals). Implementation strategies include: **LLM-based** approaches that generate, revise, and accumulate checks dynamically — via manually specified trusted-context rules (GuardAgent) or test-time adaptation with a persistent memory module (AGrail); **DSL-based** approaches (AgentSpec) that specify `trigger → check → enforce` triples in a formal domain-specific language evaluated entirely externally to the LLM; **policy-synthesis-and-verification** approaches (VeriGuard) that compile natural-language safety requests into executable policy code, test and formally verify it offline, then run the verified monitor online; **formal-policy-reasoning** approaches (ShieldAgent) that extract LTL rules from policy documents, compile them into probabilistic rule circuits, and combine Markov Logic Network inference with model-checking to produce traceable, policy-grounded safety decisions; and **closed-loop detect-remediate-verify** approaches (AegisAgent) that wrap an LLM pipeline in an autonomous recovery cycle using lexical sanitisation, cross-modal consistency verification, and structured multi-path reasoning.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail\|AGrail]] | Uses two cooperating LLMs (Analyzer + Executor) and a persistent memory module updated via test-time adaptation to generate and refine action-level safety checks across agent interactions, achieving near-zero ASR against prompt injection while preserving >95% benign action accuracy. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents\|GuardAgent]] | A guard agent that translates manually specified safety requirements into executable action-checking code using knowledge-enabled reasoning, evaluated on web and medical task benchmarks. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2025agentspeccustomizableruntimeenforcement\|AgentSpec]] | A DSL and runtime enforcement framework that intercepts LLM agent actions at three lifecycle hooks (before action, after state change, at task completion) and applies user-defined `trigger → check → enforce` rules whose predicates are evaluated externally — achieving >90% prevention of unsafe code executions and 100% law-compliance on autonomous vehicle scenarios with millisecond overhead. Cited here for its action-level trigger/check/enforce rules; also cited under IFC for its linear-trace constraint language. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable\|ShieldAgent]] | Automatically extracts verifiable LTL rules from policy documents, organises them into action-based probabilistic rule circuits, and uses formal verification plus Markov Logic Network inference to determine whether each proposed agent action complies with policy — producing a binary label, a list of violated rules, and a textual explanation, without modifying the target agent. Cited here for its per-action compliance verdicts via rule circuits; also cited under IFC for its policy-document-driven verifiable safety reasoning. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@miculicich2025veriguardenhancingllmagent\|VeriGuard]] | Synthesises executable policy code and formal contracts from natural-language safety requests, validates and formally verifies them offline, then uses the verified policy as a runtime monitor that blocks or replans unsafe agent actions before execution. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2025aegisagentautonomousdefenseagent\|AegisAgent]] | Wraps an LLM-HAR pipeline in a closed-loop detect-remediate-verify cycle using lexical input sanitisation, cross-modal consistency verification, and structured multi-path reasoning — recovering correct predictions from attacked sensor-to-prompt pipelines without any model retraining. |

## Advantages

- Operates at action granularity — catches environment-dependent risks (e.g. file overwrite collisions, access-control violations) that are invisible at the input-text level.
- Can invoke external tools (OS environment detector, web HTML inspector, EHR permission detector) when LLM reasoning alone is insufficient.
- Adaptive variants (AGrail) generalise to out-of-distribution tasks without retraining; memory converges to a stable policy across random seeds (>98% mutual similarity).
- Handles heterogeneous risk types — task-specific and systemic — within a unified framework.
- DSL-based enforcement (AgentSpec) is completely external to the LLM's reasoning process, so injected content in retrieved documents cannot manipulate or bypass the safety rules; rules are human-readable, auditable text files.
- DSL-based enforcement (AgentSpec) incurs only millisecond-level overhead (rule parsing: 1.42 ms; predicate evaluation: 1–3 ms), negligible against agent runtimes of 10–25 s.
- LLM-assisted rule generation (AgentSpec): frontier models (o1) can generate correct rules with 95.56% precision from few-shot examples, reducing the manual authorship burden.

## Limitations

- LLM-based variants rely on off-the-shelf LLMs as the guardrail components; no specialised security fine-tuning is performed, leaving headroom for targeted attacks.
- Token overhead is non-trivial for LLM-based variants (~4,810 tokens per step for AGrail), sitting between a simple model-based baseline and richer guardrail systems.
- Static-rule variants (GuardAgent, AgentSpec) require manual specification of trusted contexts or DSL rules, limiting adaptability to novel tasks.
- Weaker foundation models show higher variance in check quality for LLM-based approaches, suggesting reliability is model-dependent.
- DSL-based enforcement (AgentSpec) has no trajectory-level foresight: enforcement fires at discrete checkpoints and cannot reason about whether a currently-safe action sequence will lead to an unsafe state several steps later.
- LLM-generated AgentSpec rules can over-fit to examples (e.g. flagging only specific listed file paths or banning entire action classes rather than checking context), and rule coverage gaps leave a bypass vector via novel action types with no matching trigger.
- Policy-document-driven variants (ShieldAgent) require well-formed policy inputs to populate the ASPM; informal or absent policies leave rule circuits empty, and rebuilding circuits after policy changes incurs multiple GPT-4o calls.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Safe-OS | PI ASR ↓ | AGrail | 0% | 14.3% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Prompt injection attack; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| Safe-OS | Norm accuracy ↑ | AGrail | 95.6% | 50.0% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Benign action pass rate; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| Safe-OS | Env ASR ↓ | AGrail | 5% | 20% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Environment-dependent attack; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| EIA | Grd ASR ↓ | AGrail | 6.0% | 40.0% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Action grounding attack; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| ASB | ASR ↓ | VeriGuard | 0.0% | 49.9% no defence | Claude-sonnet-4 | Average across DPI, IPI, Memory Poisoning, and PoT; highest-utility backbone reported, with 85.1% average TSR; source: @miculicich2025veriguardenhancingllmagent |
| Mind2Web-SC | LPA ↑ | GuardAgent | 90.0 | 82.5 (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Safety policy compliance for SeeAct; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| Mind2Web-SC | F1 ↑ | GuardAgent | 89.0 | — | GPT-4 | source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents; reported as baseline in @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail Table 2 |
| Mind2Web-SC | EA ↑ | GuardAgent | 80.0 | 65.0 (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Explanation accuracy: correctly identifies violated rules; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| Mind2Web-SC | FRA ↑ | GuardAgent | 100 | 100 (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Target agent task performance unaffected; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| Mind2Web-SC | F1 ↑ | AGrail | 95.8 | 89.0 (GuardAgent GPT-4) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Task-specific risk; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| Mind2Web-SC (OOD) | LPA ↑ | AGrail (TTA) | 94.6 | 89.7 (no memory) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | OOD generalisation via test-time adaptation; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| EICU-AC | LPA ↑ | GuardAgent | 98.7 | 92.1 (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Access control for EHRAgent; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| EICU-AC | F1 ↑ | GuardAgent | 98.7 | — | GPT-4 | source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents; reported as baseline in @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail Table 2 |
| EICU-AC | EA ↑ | GuardAgent | 97.5 | 67.9 (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Explanation accuracy: correctly identifies inaccessible databases; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| EICU-AC | FRA ↑ | GuardAgent | 100 | 100 (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Target agent task performance unaffected; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| EICU-AC | F1 ↑ | AGrail | 98.5 | 98.7 (GuardAgent GPT-4) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | AGrail comparable to GuardAgent on medical access control; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| CSQA | Overall recall ↑ | GuardAgent | 92.5 (74/80) | 82.5 (66/80) (Model-Guarding-Agent) | GPT-4 | Pseudo-rule guardrail on QA task; code generation generalises beyond agent action checking; source: @xiang2025guardagentsafeguardllmagents |
| RedCode-Exec (code agent, 25 categories × 30 cases) | Risky execution prevented ↑ | AgentSpec (manual rules) | >90% overall; 24/25 categories flagged | 0% undefended | CodeAct / GPT-4o | Residual failures in 2 categories where agent self-rejected the instruction; source: @wang2025agentspeccustomizableruntimeenforcement |
| SafeAgentBench (embodied agent, 10 hazard categories) | Hazardous task completion ↓ | AgentSpec (manual rules) | 0% across all 10 categories | 0–78.6% per category undefended | ReAct / GPT-4o | Safe task success drops only from 58.6% to 54.3%; source: @wang2025agentspeccustomizableruntimeenforcement |
| ShieldAgent-Bench (agent-based attack) | ACC@G ↑ | ShieldAgent | 90.4% | 82.0% (Rule Traverse) | GPT-4o | Best across all 7 risk categories; source: @chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable |
| ShieldAgent-Bench (agent-based attack) | FPR@G ↓ | ShieldAgent | 5.6% | 18.1% (Rule Traverse) | GPT-4o | source: @chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable |
| ST-WebAgentBench | ACC ↑ | ShieldAgent | 91.1% | 84.0% (GuardAgent) | GPT-4o | 234 safety-constrained web tasks; source: @chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable |
| AgentHarm | ACC ↑ | ShieldAgent | 86.9% | 78.4% (GuardAgent) | GPT-4o | 110 malicious agent tasks; source: @chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable |
| FixDrive (autonomous vehicle, 8 law-violation scenarios) | Traffic-law compliance ↑ | AgentSpec (manual rules) | 100% (8/8 scenarios) | 0% undefended | Apollo ADS | Rules translated directly from FixDrive law specifications; source: @wang2025agentspeccustomizableruntimeenforcement |
| USC-HAD / UCI HAR / PAMAP2 (avg across 5 LLM-HARs) | Detection Accuracy DA (↑) | AegisAgent (full system) | 85% | 64.9% multimodal detection-only; 58.7% classical HAR; 44.1% text-only | Gemma-2-9B | Averaged across 5 LLM-HAR systems and 15 attack types; source: @wang2025aegisagentautonomousdefenseagent |
| USC-HAD / UCI HAR / PAMAP2 (avg across 5 LLM-HARs) | Attack Success Rate ASR (↓) | AegisAgent (full system) | 27.2% | 74.3% text-only undefended | Gemma-2-9B | 30-point average ASR reduction; source: @wang2025aegisagentautonomousdefenseagent |
| USC-HAD / UCI HAR / PAMAP2 (avg across 5 LLM-HARs) | Recovery Rate RR (↑) | AegisAgent (full system) | 56.3% | 7.9% text-only; 21.7% multimodal detection-only | Gemma-2-9B | Measures ability to repair errors introduced by attacks; source: @wang2025aegisagentautonomousdefenseagent |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/LLM-Based Evaluation\|LLM-Based Evaluation]] | Action-level guardrails extend the static LLM gate pattern by operating at action granularity with dynamic, policy-driven, or formally verified safety checks rather than a single verdict on raw input text. |

nodes.name: Agent Execution Analysis
nodes.content:
# Agent Execution Analysis

Techniques that evaluate the agent's proposed actions, execution traces, or inter-turn dynamics at runtime to detect and mitigate indirect prompt injection. Rather than screening the raw input or inspecting the model's internal reasoning, these defences operate at the behavioural boundary between the agent and its environment — intercepting tool calls, comparing execution paths under controlled counterfactual conditions, verifying task alignment of proposed actions, enforcing safety policies at action granularity, or validating entries retrieved from persistent memory stores. This positions them to catch attacks that only manifest as anomalous actions rather than anomalous text or activations.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Dual-Execution Detection\|Dual-Execution Detection]] | Runs the agent on two parallel execution paths — one normal, one with the user task masked out — and flags the interaction when the tool calls converge, revealing that injected content has made the agent's actions independent of the user's original request. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Causal Counterfactual Detection\|Causal Counterfactual Detection]] | Uses causal ablations over user instructions and untrusted spans — via full boundary replays or action-triggered leave-one-out attribution — to detect when attacker-controlled context has become the main driver of a risky action, then sanitises only the implicated context and regenerates. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Task-Alignment Verification\|Task-Alignment Verification]] | Checks whether each incoming instruction contributes to the user's stated objectives via a formal ContributesTo relation; zero-contribution instructions are blocked before execution, catching benign-sounding injections that evade keyword filters. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Action-Level Guardrails\|Action-Level Guardrails]] | A dedicated safety layer evaluates each proposed action against dynamically generated, DSL-specified, policy-synthesised, or formally reasoned safety checks before execution is permitted — covering task-specific risks, access-control violations, and prompt injection at action granularity. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Predictive Trajectory Monitoring\|Predictive Trajectory Monitoring]] | Forecasts whether the current execution trajectory is likely to reach an unsafe state using learned state-transition models over symbolic execution states, enabling intervention before any single step is already a violation. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Agent Memory Defence\|Agent Memory Defence]] | Interposes a consensus validation layer at memory read time that generates parallel reasoning paths for each retrieved entry and flags those whose paths diverge from the majority, protecting against memory-poisoning attacks that bypass live-input filters. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/AI Control Protocols\|AI Control Protocols]] | Constructs safety pipelines that remain safe even when the LLM agent is itself actively trying to subvert them — combining trusted monitoring, trusted editing, dynamic defer-to-trusted routing, and human auditing to bound worst-case attack success without any model retraining. |

## Advantages

- Catches environment-dependent risks (file overwrite collisions, access-control violations, tool-call hijacking) that are invisible at the input-text or reasoning-trace level.
- Training-free approaches (Dual-Execution, Causal Counterfactual) require no model fine-tuning, no access to LLM internals, and operate on any black-box agent.
- Causal counterfactual detection enables safe task continuation by purifying only the causally implicated context rather than aborting the entire interaction.
- Some causal-attribution variants trigger only on privileged actions and can use smaller proxy models, reducing the over-defense cost of always-on sanitisation.
- Formal guarantees: MELON provides Hoeffding-bounded error rates; AgentSentry produces auditable per-boundary ACE/IE trajectories; ShieldAgent uses LTL rule circuits for policy-grounded verification.
- Task-alignment verification derives its security criterion from the user's own intent, generalising to semantically novel injections without requiring examples of malicious content.
- Memory defence closes a gap that all other detection layers leave: attacks that travel through the memory pipeline rather than arriving in the live input.

## Limitations

- Multiple re-executions per step (2× for dual-execution, up to 4× for heavier causal counterfactual variants) substantially increase API cost and latency in multi-tool workflows, though selective-action variants reduce this overhead.
- Dual-execution only monitors tool calls, not text responses; attacks that achieve their goal through text output alone evade detection.
- Task-alignment and adaptive guardrails rely on LLM calls for scoring, so performance degrades with weaker backbone models.
- Vague or broad user objectives make task-alignment extraction error-prone, causing false positives or false negatives.
- Memory defence degrades when too few memories are retrieved (k=2 insufficient) or when the memory store is sparse.
- Causal-attribution approaches assume clean provenance boundaries between trusted user intent and untrusted spans; muddier context assembly weakens their diagnostics.
- Not evaluated against adaptive attacks specifically designed to exploit the symmetry between parallel execution paths (dual-execution) or to achieve non-zero ContributesTo scores (task-alignment).

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo (avg. 3 attacks) | UA % (↑) | AgentSentry | 73.90% | Task Shield 53.13%; MELON-Aug 45.13% | GPT-4o | ASR = 0%; FPR = 0%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo | Benign latency ↓ | CausalArmor | 1.22× | 1.00× no defence; 5.82× DRIFT | Gemini-3-Pro | Selective privileged-action attribution keeps runtime overhead close to no defence; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| DoomArena (TauBench-Retail) | Benign utility ↑ | CausalArmor | 70.96% | 73.57% no defence; 65.04% Repeat Prompt; 55.13% PIGuard | Gemini-3-Pro | Adaptive privileged attacker; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| DoomArena (TauBench-Retail) | ASR ↓ | CausalArmor | 3.65% | 88.87% no defence; 72.70% Repeat Prompt; 5.57% PIGuard | Gemini-3-Pro | Adaptive conversational setting; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | MELON | 0.24% | 16.06% no defence | GPT-4o | Average across 4 attack types; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | Task Shield | 2.07% | 47.69% undefended | GPT-4o | Important Instructions attack; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| Safe-OS | PI ASR (↓) | AGrail | 0% | 14.3% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Prompt injection attack; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| ASB | ASR ↓ | VeriGuard | 0.0% | 49.9% no defence | Claude-sonnet-4 | Average across DPI, IPI, Memory Poisoning, and PoT; highest-utility backbone reported, with 85.1% average TSR; source: @miculicich2025veriguardenhancingllmagent |
| ShieldAgent-Bench | ACC@G (↑) | ShieldAgent | 90.4% | 82.0% (Rule Traverse) | GPT-4o | Best across all 7 risk categories; source: @chen2025shieldagentshieldingagentsverifiable |
| EHRAgent (AgentPoison) | ASR-r (↓) | A-MemGuard | 2.13% | 100% no defence | GPT-4o-mini + DPR | All compared defences left ASR-r at 100%; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |
| μDrive/LawBreaker autonomous-driving scenarios (7 scenarios) | Advance warning time AWT ↑ | ProbGuard (θ = 0.7) | 0.77–38.66 s | REDriver: 0.77–15.84 s; some bounded-response cases 0 s at lower thresholds | Apollo ADS + PRISM | Predictive trajectory monitoring surfaces autonomous-driving violations well before they occur; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| Embodied household tasks | Unsafe rate ↓ | ProbGuard stop (θ = 0.1) | 2.60% | 40.63% undefended | ReAct + SafeAgentBench low-level controller | Strongest reported safety setting for predictive trajectory monitoring; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| Embodied household tasks | Completion ↑ | ProbGuard reflect (θ = 0.1) | 47.74% | 10.42% ProbGuard stop (θ = 0.1) | ReAct + SafeAgentBench low-level controller | Utility-preserving intervention mode; unsafe rate remains 14.07%; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Reasoning Monitoring/Reasoning Monitoring\|Reasoning Monitoring]] | Agent execution analysis evaluates the agent's proposed actions and execution traces at the behavioural boundary, catching environment-dependent risks and tool-call hijacking that are invisible even when inspecting the model's internal reasoning process. |

nodes.name: Agent Memory Defence
nodes.content:
# Agent Memory Defence

Defences that protect LLM-based agents against **memory poisoning** — attacks that insert adversarially crafted entries into the agent's persistent memory store so that future retrievals silently bias the model's reasoning or actions. Because the injection travels through the memory pipeline rather than arriving in the live input, it evades filters that inspect only incoming messages. Techniques in this category interpose a validation layer at memory **read time**: before retrieved entries reach the model's context, they are checked for anomalous reasoning signal (e.g. by comparing reasoning paths across a consensus of retrieved entries). Detected anomalies are excluded from the context and archived as cautionary lessons that proactively block structurally similar failures in future turns.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework\|A-MemGuard]] | Wraps the agent's memory-to-action pipeline with a consensus validation layer that generates parallel reasoning paths for each retrieved memory entry and flags those whose paths diverge from the majority; detected anomalies are distilled into a lesson memory that proactively checks future action plans before execution. |

## Advantages

- Detects context-dependent poisoning that per-entry content audits miss: the adversarial signal only becomes visible when one entry's reasoning path is compared against the consensus of benign peers.
- Breaks self-reinforcing error cycles: storing negative lessons converts detected failures into adaptive corrections, preventing repeated exploitation of the same flaw across sessions.
- Non-invasive: operates as a wrapper around standard memory retrieval with no modification to the core LLM or agent architecture; compatible with multiple retrieval back-ends (DPR, REALM).

## Limitations

- Consensus quality degrades when too few memories are retrieved (k = 2 is insufficient) or when the memory store is sparse or cold-start.
- Generates K parallel reasoning paths per query, adding latency that may be unacceptable in strict real-time pipelines.
- The lesson memory grows unboundedly in long-running agents; no forgetting or pruning mechanism has been described.
- Coverage is bounded to memory-mediated attacks; direct prompt injection in tool observations or live user turns is not addressed.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EHRAgent (AgentPoison) | ASR-r ↓ | A-MemGuard | 2.13% | 100% no defence | GPT-4o-mini + DPR | All compared defences (LLM Auditor, Distil Classifier, PPL) left ASR-r at 100%; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |
| ReAct-StrategyQA (AgentPoison) | ASR-r ↓ | A-MemGuard | 0.00% | 37.50% no defence | LLaMA-3-8B + DPR | Near-zero ASR on knowledge-intensive QA; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |
| MMLU (MINJA indirect attack) | ASR ↓ | A-MemGuard | 0.256 | 0.667 no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Breaks self-reinforcing error cycle; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |
| MISINFOTASK (multi-agent) | Task Success ↑ | A-MemGuard | 0.950 | 0.800 no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Highest task success rate of all compared methods; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Classifier-Based Detection\|Classifier-Based Detection]] | Classifier-based detectors inspect each memory entry in isolation and miss poisoning that is only detectable when entries are compared to each other; A-MemGuard's consensus mechanism specifically closes this gap. |

nodes.name: Causal Counterfactual Detection
nodes.content:
# Causal Counterfactual Detection

Defences that use controlled counterfactual ablations to ask whether an untrusted span has become more causally responsible for a risky agent action than the user's original request. Some run multiple full re-executions at each tool-return boundary; lighter variants perform leave-one-out attribution only when a privileged action is proposed. When diagnostics show attacker-controlled content has become the dominant causal driver, the system selectively sanitises the implicated span, clears any now-poisoned downstream reasoning, and re-runs from the affected point instead of aborting the whole task.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt\|AgentSentry]] | Intercepts each tool-return boundary, runs four dry-run re-executions across combinations of original/masked user input and original/sanitised tool return, estimates average causal effect (ACE) and indirect effect (IE) to detect temporal causal degradation, then applies causally-gated context purification to repair agent state without terminating the task. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt\|CausalArmor]] | Triggers only when a privileged action is proposed, computes leave-one-out attribution for the user request and each untrusted span on a smaller proxy model, then sanitises only spans whose causal influence exceeds the user's and masks any poisoned reasoning before regeneration. |

## Advantages

- Causally gated: purification fires only when diagnostics confirm mediator-driven takeover, leaving inert boundaries untouched and preserving benign utility without over-sanitizing.
- Supports lighter-weight deployments than always-on replay: attribution can be restricted to privileged actions and offloaded to a smaller proxy model.
- Enables safe continuation rather than abort: once the causally implicated context is purified, the agent re-runs from the same boundary and finishes the original task.
- Produces auditable per-boundary ACE/IE trajectories that identify exactly when and where injected content became action-dominant.
- Black-box deployment: requires only inference-API access, with no access to model weights, activations, or fine-tuning.

## Limitations

- Heavyweight variants require multiple dry-run re-executions; even lighter attribution variants still add privileged-action checks and depend on proxy-model fidelity.
- Requires a provenance-tagged replay cache of all tool responses to support sanitised substitution, adding infrastructure complexity.
- Assumes the system can cleanly separate the trusted user request from untrusted spans and, after detection, regenerate from a well-defined rollback point.
- The instruction-stripping step is itself LLM-mediated: a sophisticated payload that mimics benign factual prose could evade sanitisation.
- Presupposes that re-executing up to a boundary without committing external effects is safe and feasible, which may not hold when read-only and side-effecting calls are hard to distinguish.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo (avg. 3 attacks) | UA % (↑) | AgentSentry | 73.90% | Task Shield 53.13%; MELON-Aug 45.13% | GPT-4o | ASR = 0%; FPR = 0%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo (avg. 3 attacks) | UA % (↑) | AgentSentry | 70.16% | Task Shield 36.59%; MELON-Aug 37.75% | GPT-3.5-turbo | ASR = 0%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo (avg. 3 attacks) | UA % (↑) | AgentSentry | 79.58% | Task Shield 50.31%; MELON-Aug 52.77% | Qwen3-Max | ASR = 0%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo — Important Instructions | ASR % (↓) | AgentSentry | 0.00% | MELON 0.00%; Task Shield 4.21%; No Defence 33.23% | GPT-4o | UA = 75.49%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo — Tool Knowledge | ASR % (↓) | AgentSentry | 0.00% | MELON 2.86%; Task Shield 5.71%; No Defence 30.00% | GPT-4o | UA = 63.57%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo | Benign latency (↓) | CausalArmor | 1.22× | 1.00× no defence; 5.82× DRIFT | Gemini-3-Pro | Relative benign latency; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| DoomArena (TauBench-Retail) | Benign utility (↑) | CausalArmor | 70.96% | 73.57% no defence; 65.04% Repeat Prompt; 55.13% PIGuard | Gemini-3-Pro | Adaptive privileged attacker; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| DoomArena (TauBench-Retail) | ASR (↓) | CausalArmor | 3.65% | 88.87% no defence; 72.70% Repeat Prompt; 5.57% PIGuard | Gemini-3-Pro | Adaptive conversational setting; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Dual-Execution Detection\|Dual-Execution Detection]] | Causal counterfactual detection extends the dual-execution idea from 2 runs to 4 controlled counterfactual paths, adds formal causal effect estimation (ACE/IE) across successive boundaries to localise temporal takeover, and replaces detect-then-abort with causally-gated context purification, enabling safe task continuation. |
| embeds:: [[Sanitization/LLM-Based Sanitization\|LLM-Based Sanitization]] | In both AgentSentry and CausalArmor, causal attribution is only the trigger; the actual recovery step depends on selectively sanitising the implicated untrusted span before regeneration. |

nodes.name: Dual-Execution Detection
nodes.content:
# Dual-Execution Detection

Detects indirect prompt injection by running the agent on two parallel execution paths — one normal, one with the user task masked out — and flagging the interaction as an attack when the tool calls produced by both paths converge. The intuition is that a successfully injected malicious task causes the agent's next tool call to become independent of the user's original request (state collapse); replacing the user task with a task-neutral prompt in the masking run makes that independence visible as a match.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect\|MELON]] | Runs a masked re-execution alongside the normal agent run at every step, caches all masking-run tool calls, and halts execution when any original-run tool call is semantically similar to a cached masking-run call; an ensemble variant provides Hoeffding-bounded formal error rates. |

## Advantages

- Training-free: requires no model fine-tuning, no access to LLM internals, and no external classifier — operates on any black-box agent.
- Provides formal error-rate bounds via Hoeffding's inequality when using an ensemble of masking prompts, offering provable guarantees without modifying the model.
- The masking run can be parallelised with the original run, limiting added latency to roughly one extra inference call per step.

## Limitations

- Approximately doubles API call costs compared to an undefended baseline.
- Only monitors tool calls, not text responses; attacks that achieve their goal through text output alone (e.g. persuasion responses) evade detection entirely.
- Tool call caching can create timing mismatches when prior outputs already contain partial results, causing false negatives.
- Not evaluated against adaptive attacks specifically designed to exploit the symmetry between the two execution paths.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | MELON | 0.24% | 16.06% no defence | GPT-4o | Average across Direct, Ignore Previous, System Message, Important Messages attacks; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |
| AgentDojo | UA ↑ | MELON | 58.78% | 69.08% no defence | GPT-4o | Utility under attack; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | MELON-Aug | 0.32% | 16.06% no defence | GPT-4o | MELON combined with Repeat Prompt augmentation; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |
| AgentDojo | UA ↑ | MELON-Aug | 68.72% | 69.08% no defence | GPT-4o | Closest to baseline UA among detection-based methods; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Predictive Trajectory Monitoring
nodes.content:
# Predictive Trajectory Monitoring

Defences that monitor a partially executed agent trajectory and estimate whether it is likely to reach an unsafe state before any individual step is already a rule violation. Rather than scoring each proposed action in isolation, these techniques maintain a state abstraction of the execution history, forecast future risk from offline traces or transition models, and intervene when the projected violation probability crosses a threshold.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring\|ProbGuard]] | Learns a DTMC over symbolic safety states from execution traces and uses runtime model checking to estimate whether the current trajectory is likely to reach an unsafe state, enabling early warning or intervention before the final hazardous action. |

## Advantages

- Proactive rather than purely reactive: can stop unsafe trajectories before any single step is an obvious violation.
- Produces interpretable risk scores over future safety, not just a binary allow/block verdict.
- Can be layered with different intervention policies such as stopping, reflection, or human escalation.

## Limitations

- Requires domain-specific unsafe-state definitions and abstraction predicates.
- Forecast quality depends on whether the symbolic state abstraction captures enough history; omitted context can bias risk estimates.
- Predicting future risk does not by itself guarantee that the chosen intervention will preserve utility or remain safe under distribution shift.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| μDrive/LawBreaker autonomous-driving scenarios (7 scenarios) | Advance warning time AWT ↑ | ProbGuard (θ = 0.7) | 0.77–38.66 s | REDriver: 0.77–15.84 s; some bounded-response cases 0 s at lower thresholds | Apollo ADS + PRISM | Predictive monitoring issues earlier violation warnings than the reactive baseline; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| Embodied household tasks | Unsafe rate ↓ | ProbGuard stop (θ = 0.1) | 2.60% | 40.63% undefended | ReAct + SafeAgentBench low-level controller | "Armor Mode" gives the strongest reported safety reduction, with completion dropping to 10.42%; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| Embodied household tasks | Completion ↑ | ProbGuard reflect (θ = 0.1) | 47.74% | 10.42% ProbGuard stop (θ = 0.1) | ReAct + SafeAgentBench low-level controller | Reflection preserves much more utility than hard stopping at the same threshold while keeping unsafe rate at 14.07%; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Action-Level Guardrails\|Action-Level Guardrails]] | Predictive trajectory monitoring extends reactive per-action guardrails by estimating whether a currently acceptable sequence of actions is likely to evolve into an unsafe state, enabling intervention before a rule would be violated outright. |

nodes.name: Task-Alignment Verification
nodes.content:
# Task-Alignment Verification

Defences that intercept indirect prompt injection by checking whether each incoming instruction **contributes to the user's stated objectives**, rather than by classifying content as malicious or applying a pre-defined safety policy. The core primitive is a formal *ContributesTo* relation: an instruction that scores zero contribution against every user goal is flagged as misaligned and blocked or corrected before execution. Because the security criterion is derived from the user's own intent, this approach catches benign-sounding injections that evade keyword filters, and it requires no model retraining.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask\|Task Shield]] | Extracts a User Task Set from the original request and, at every agent step, scores each directive extracted from assistant messages and tool outputs against that set using a fuzzy ContributesTo relation; zero-contribution instructions trigger structured feedback and block tool execution. |

## Advantages

- Security criterion is derived from the user's own intent, so the defence generalises to semantically novel injections without requiring examples of malicious content.
- Training-free and model-agnostic: operates entirely at inference time and is applicable to closed-source frontier models.
- Orthogonal to content-based filtering and structural defences; can be layered with other mitigations.

## Limitations

- Alignment scoring relies on an LLM call, so performance degrades with weaker backbone models and inherits their instruction-following failures.
- Susceptible to adaptive attacks that craft injections achieving a non-zero ContributesTo score (e.g. "complete the task, and also…").
- Each incoming message triggers additional LLM calls, increasing latency and token usage.
- Vague or broad user objectives make extraction error-prone, causing false positives or false negatives.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Task Shield | 2.07% | 47.69% undefended | GPT-4o | Important Instructions attack; overall across all four suites; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| AgentDojo | Utility under attack ↑ | Task Shield | 69.79% | 50.08% undefended | GPT-4o | Important Instructions attack; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| AgentDojo | Clean Utility ↑ | Task Shield | 73.20% | 69.07% undefended | GPT-4o | Benign (no attack) condition; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Task Shield | 2.23% | 27.19% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Important Instructions attack; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Task Shield | 1.11% | 5.72% undefended | GPT-4o | InjecAgent attack; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Task Shield | 0.48% | 5.41% undefended | GPT-4o | Ignore Previous attack; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Action-Level Guardrails\|Action-Level Guardrails]] | Task Shield replaces externally specified safety rules with a user-derived alignment criterion, addressing the limitation that static or accumulated safety policies must be manually specified or learned and may not generalise to novel injection goals. |

nodes.name: Detection
nodes.content:
# Detection

Defences that classify, evaluate, or monitor raw inputs, model-generated responses, reasoning traces, agent behaviour, or logged interactions for adversarial intent. The evaluator may be a lightweight fine-tuned classifier (sub-100 ms, no LLM inference), a full LLM instance given a security-focused system prompt, a hidden-state probe, an active canary-style check, or a cascade of several. Depending on deployment, suspicious activity may be blocked inline, quarantined for review, or surfaced as a monitoring signal for downstream action. This approach requires no changes to the primary task model or its prompt, and the evaluator can be tuned or replaced independently of the main model.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Input Screening\|Input Screening]] | Evaluates raw input text, tokens, or embeddings before the agent processes them (classifiers, LLM-as-judge, perplexity scoring, embedding drift, certified erasure). |
| down:: [[Detection/Response-Space Detection/Response-Space Detection\|Response-Space Detection]] | Detects attacks from the model's responses rather than only the raw input, including inferred-intent checks and mutation-based response-consistency analysis. |
| down:: [[Detection/Reasoning Monitoring/Reasoning Monitoring\|Reasoning Monitoring]] | Inspects the model's internal reasoning process (chain-of-thought scratchpad, hidden-state activations) to catch misalignment that forms after ingesting injected content. |
| down:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/Agent Execution Analysis\|Agent Execution Analysis]] | Evaluates the agent's proposed actions, execution traces, or inter-turn dynamics at runtime (guardrails, dual-execution, counterfactual analysis, task alignment, memory defence). |
| down:: [[Detection/Multi-Agent Anomaly Detection/Multi-Agent Anomaly Detection\|Multi-Agent Anomaly Detection]] | Identifies compromised agents within a multi-agent system by scoring behavioural deviation via graph neural network analysis. |
| down:: [[Detection/Interaction-Level Monitoring\|Interaction-Level Monitoring]] | Scores each prompt/response interaction for security risk and exposes the results as monitoring telemetry, dashboards, alerts, compliance artefacts, or threshold-based flag/block actions. |
| down:: [[Detection/Usage-Level Monitoring\|Usage-Level Monitoring]] | Aggregates many completed interaction records into LLM-generated summaries, then synthesises those summaries into a usage report that surfaces aggregate harms and emergent misuse patterns invisible to per-interaction classifiers. |
| down:: [[Detection/Security Assessment Frameworks\|Security Assessment Frameworks]] | Packages reusable red-team scanners and named security frameworks into executable assessments that systematically probe an application for prompt injection and adjacent risks, then report failures for recurring assurance. |

## Advantages

A dedicated evaluation step keeps security logic separate from the primary task model, allowing the evaluator to be tuned or replaced independently. Detection spans multiple granularities — from pre-processing input screening and response-space self-checks to runtime agent execution analysis, observability-oriented monitoring, and repeatable security-assessment scanners/frameworks — enabling layered defence without modifying the underlying model.

## Limitations

Detection accuracy depends entirely on the evaluator model's ability to recognise adversarial inputs, which is itself subject to adversarial manipulation — a sufficiently crafted injection may evade the evaluator just as it would the primary model. Every request incurs additional latency and API cost, block-based variants may degrade utility through false positives, and monitoring-only variants still require separate downstream enforcement or review logic to actually stop an attack.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | PromptGuard 2 86M + AlignmentCheck | 1.75% | 17.63% undefended | Combined (DeBERTa classifier + Llama 4 Maverick auditor) | Layered classifier + LLM auditor; >90% ASR reduction; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Perplexity filter (basic) | 0.00 | 0.79 undefended | Vicuna-7B | Training-free; 0/100 attacks pass filter; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Paraphrasing (ChatGPT gpt-3.5-turbo) | 0.05 | 0.79 undefended | Vicuna-7B | Matches no-attack baseline; ~10% utility degradation on AlpacaEval; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | F-beta ↑ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.998 | LLM Guard 0.989; Rebuff 0.997 | DeBERTa-v3 + YARA + VectorDB | Best F-beta (beta=1/11) at default config; FPR 0.000; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | LLM Guard (transformer, Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.999 | Vigil 0.838; Rebuff 0.981 | DeBERTa-v3 | Highest recall of three solutions; FPR 0.127; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | F-beta ↑ | Authors' composite policy (best-of-all scanners) | 0.998 | Vigil 0.998; Rebuff 0.997 | DeBERTa-v3 + GPT-4o | FPR 0.016, Recall 0.981; outperforms all individual solutions on F1; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| BAD test set | Unsafe F1 ↑ | Multi-turn Safety Classifier (Adv. Dialog) | 80.8 | 60.7 Safety Classifier+ | 622M classifier | Representative context-aware screening result propagated from Input Screening; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |
| Adversarial dialogue safety test set | OK rate ↑ | BST 2.7B + Adv. Dialogue Safety | 94.4% | 55.0% BST 2.7B | BST 2.7B | Representative response-space output-screening result in adversarial dialogue; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |
| Safe-OS | PI ASR ↓ | AGrail | 0% | 14.3% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Adaptive agent guardrail; prompt injection; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| Safe-OS | Norm accuracy ↑ | AGrail | 95.6% | 50.0% (Claude-3.5 0-shot) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Benign action pass rate; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| ASB | ASR ↓ | VeriGuard | 0.0% | 49.9% no defence | Claude-sonnet-4 | Average across DPI, IPI, Memory Poisoning, and PoT; highest-utility backbone reported, with 85.1% average TSR; source: @miculicich2025veriguardenhancingllmagent |
| Mind2Web-SC | F1 ↑ | AGrail | 95.8 | 89.0 (GuardAgent GPT-4) | Claude-3.5-Sonnet | Task-specific risk detection; source: @luo2025agraillifelongagentguardrail |
| EHRAgent (AgentPoison) | ASR-r ↓ | A-MemGuard | 2.13% | 100% no defence | GPT-4o-mini + DPR | Memory poisoning defence; all compared baselines left ASR-r at 100%; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |
| MMLU (MINJA indirect attack) | ASR ↓ | A-MemGuard | 0.256 | 0.667 no defence | GPT-4o-mini | Breaks self-reinforcing cross-session error cycle; source: @wei2025amemguardproactivedefenseframework |
| TaskTracker test set (public, OOD) | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker linear probe | 0.996 | Prompt Guard 0.974; Prompt Shields 0.988 | Phi-3 14B | Activation-delta detection; zero-shot generalisation to unseen attacks; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| Six jailbreak attacks (average) | TPR ↑ | Gradient Cuff (`σ = 5%`) | 0.883 ± 0.013 | 0.760 ± 0.017 Llama Guard | LLaMA-2-7B-Chat | Internal-signal jailbreak detector based on refusal-loss gradients; also keeps lower FPR (0.022 vs 0.040); source: @hu2024gradientcuffdetectingjailbreak |
| JailbreakHub | FPR ↓ | Random Forest + Snowflake embeddings | 0.0042 | PromptGuard: 0.5066; deberta-v3-base-injection: 0.9596; gelectra: 0.9729 | `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-long` + random forest | Representative low-false-positive input-screening result on a realistic public jailbreak benchmark; propagated from Input Screening; source: @galinkin2024improvedlargelanguagemodel |
| FIPI | ASR ↓ | Rennervate | 0.00% | 35.10% Spotlighting | Falcon | Attention-based token-level sanitization for indirect prompt injection; source: @zhong2025attentionneeddefendindirect |
| AgentDojo (avg. 3 attacks) | UA % (↑) | AgentSentry | 73.90% | Task Shield 53.13%; MELON-Aug 45.13% | GPT-4o | Causal counterfactual detection + purification; ASR = 0%; FPR = 0%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo (avg. 3 attacks) | UA % (↑) | AgentSentry | 79.58% | Task Shield 50.31%; MELON-Aug 52.77% | Qwen3-Max | ASR = 0%; source: @zhang2026agentsentrymitigatingindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo | Benign latency ↓ | CausalArmor | 1.22× | 1.00× no defence; 5.82× DRIFT | Gemini-3-Pro | Selective causal attribution reduces the replay overhead typical of counterfactual detection; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| DoomArena (TauBench-Retail) | Benign utility ↑ | CausalArmor | 70.96% | 73.57% no defence; 65.04% Repeat Prompt; 55.13% PIGuard | Gemini-3-Pro | Adaptive privileged-attacker benchmark; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| DoomArena (TauBench-Retail) | ASR ↓ | CausalArmor | 3.65% | 88.87% no defence; 72.70% Repeat Prompt; 5.57% PIGuard | Gemini-3-Pro | Adaptive conversational indirect prompt injection; source: @kim2026causalarmorefficientindirectprompt |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | MELON | 0.24% | 16.06% no defence | GPT-4o | Dual-execution detection; average across 4 attack types; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |
| AgentDojo | UA ↑ | MELON-Aug | 68.72% | 69.08% no defence | GPT-4o | MELON + Repeat Prompt augmentation; closest to baseline UA among detection-based methods; source: @zhu2025melonprovabledefenseindirect |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks) | FPR ↓ | DataSentinel | ≈ 0.00 | 0.08–0.10 KAD | Mistral-7B detection / LLaMA3-8B backend | Known-answer detection with minimax fine-tuning; near-zero FPR across all tasks; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks, adaptive attacks) | FNR ↓ | DataSentinel (Minimax) | ≤ 0.06 (non-same-task) | 0.27–0.93 KAD | Mistral-7B detection / LLaMA3-8B backend | Adaptive attack; same-task attack raises FNR to 0.87; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 existing attacks) | RL ↑ | PromptLocate | 0.94-0.98 | 0.23-0.78 attribution baselines | LLaMA3-8B-Instruct backend | Localization rate on injected spans; ES = 0.93-0.99 and post-removal ASV = 0.06-0.08; source: @jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo | Utility ↑ | DataSentinel | 36.6% | 81.4% no defence | gpt-4o | High false-positive rate causes severe utility drop on complex agent tasks; reported in @wang2026defendingpromptinjectiondatafilter; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| AdvBench harmful behaviors (PAIR w/ defense) | DSR ↑ | Backtranslation | 76% | 64% paraphrasing | GPT-3.5-turbo | Response-space self-check against a defence-aware jailbreak attacker; source: @wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks |
| AdvBench-derived prompt set (50 harmful, 50 benign responses) | Accuracy ↑ | LLM Self Defense (suffix prompt, self-filter) | 99.0% | 98.0% prefix prompt | GPT-3.5 | TPR 0.98, FPR 0.00; zero-shot output screening over the drafted response; source: @phute2024llmselfdefenseself |
| MT-Bench | Response quality ↑ | Backtranslation | 8.60 | 8.71 no defense | GPT-3.5-turbo | Benign quality is nearly unchanged despite the extra response-space check; source: @wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Task Shield | 2.07% | 47.69% undefended | GPT-4o | Task-alignment verification; Important Instructions attack, all four suites; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| AgentDojo | Utility under attack ↑ | Task Shield | 69.79% | 50.08% undefended | GPT-4o | Task-alignment verification; Important Instructions attack; source: @jia2024taskshieldenforcingtask |
| SafeAgentBench (embodied agent, 10 hazard categories) | Hazardous task completion ↓ | AgentSpec (manual DSL rules) | 0% across all 10 categories | 0–78.6% per category undefended | ReAct / GPT-4o | DSL-based runtime enforcement; safe task success drops only 4.3pp; source: @wang2025agentspeccustomizableruntimeenforcement |
| μDrive/LawBreaker autonomous-driving scenarios (7 scenarios) | Advance warning time AWT ↑ | ProbGuard (θ = 0.7) | 0.77–38.66 s | REDriver: 0.77–15.84 s; some bounded-response cases 0 s at lower thresholds | Apollo ADS + PRISM | Predictive runtime monitoring forecasts driving-law violations before they become immediate rule hits; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| Embodied household tasks | Unsafe rate ↓ | ProbGuard stop (θ = 0.1) | 2.60% | 40.63% undefended | ReAct + SafeAgentBench low-level controller | Proactive stopping mode for embodied agents; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| Embodied household tasks | Completion ↑ | ProbGuard reflect (θ = 0.1) | 47.74% | 10.42% ProbGuard stop (θ = 0.1) | ReAct + SafeAgentBench low-level controller | Reflection mode preserves substantially more utility than hard stopping while retaining a much lower unsafe rate than undefended execution; source: @wang2026probguardprobabilisticruntimemonitoring |
| English Prompt avg. (7 benchmarks) | F1 ↑ | Qwen3Guard-8B-Gen (best mode) | 90.0% | WildGuard-7B: 79.4%; NemoGuard-8B: 82.9% | Qwen3-8B backbone | Guardrail model; state-of-the-art on all seven English prompt benchmarks; source: @zhao2025qwen3guardtechnicalreport |
| 13-benchmark suite (3 guardrail tasks, weighted avg.) | F1 ↑ | GuardReasoner 8B | 84.09% | 81.96% WildGuard 7B | Llama-3.1-8B | Reasoning-trained guard model; broad prompt/response/refusal moderation summary; source: @liu2025guardreasonerreasoningbasedllmsafeguards |
| Harmful Content (multi-benchmark avg.) | AUC ↑ | Granite Guardian | 0.871 | — | Granite (instruction-tuned) | Unified guardrail covering prompt/response harm, RAG hallucination, and agentic function-call errors; source: @padhi2024graniteguardian |
| Custom jailbreak dataset (50 attacks) | Attack prompts blocked ↑ | GUARDIAN pre-processing tier | 60% | 40% after system-prompt filter only | Llama-2-7b-chat | 30/50 blocked cumulatively after adding the BERT toxicity classifier; no classifier-only ablation; source: @rai2024guardian |
| deepset prompt-injections dataset (evaluation split) | Accuracy ↑ | deberta-v3-base-injection | 99.14% | — | microsoft/deberta-v3-base | Input-screening classifier; strong in-domain model-card accuracy on deepset's own split; source: @deepset2023debertav3baseinjection |
| HASTE out-of-loop evaluation set | Detection accuracy ↑ | HM-Bal+All (M10) | 94.44% | 82.12% at M0; 92.80% Base (M10) | Protect AI DeBERTa-v3 | Proactive hard-negative mining improves a classifier-based detector on held-out prompt-injection evaluation; source: @chen2026proactivehardeningllmdefenseshaste |
| safe-guard-prompt-injection adversarial subset (476 prompts) | Detection accuracy ↑ | Vijil prompt-injection guardrail | 99.08% | — | ModernBERT-based classifier | Third-party result from @hackett2025bypassingllmguardrailsempirical for one Vijil guardrail component, not the full platform; source: @vijil2026agenttrustplatform |
| safe-guard-prompt-injection adversarial subset (476 prompts) | Bypass ASR ↓ | Vijil prompt-injection guardrail under character injection | 87.95% | 99.08% nominal detection accuracy | ModernBERT-based classifier | Third-party average across character-injection attacks; shows simple evasions can defeat the detector despite strong nominal accuracy; source: @vijil2026agenttrustplatform |
| Pint-Benchmark | Accuracy ↑ | Lakera Guard | 98.10% | — | Unknown | Reported in @wang2025protectllmagentprompt as an external comparison on Lakera's benchmark. |
| PINT + BIPIA + WildGuard + NotInject (combined) | Avg Accuracy ↑ | PIGuard | 83.48% | ProtectAIv2 64.01% | DeBERTaV3-base | MOF strategy eliminates token-level bias without labelled false-positives; Over-defense: 87.32%, Benign: 85.74%, Malicious: 77.39%; source: @li-etal-2025-piguard |
| System overhead comparison (Table 1) | Inference time ↓ | PIGuard | 15.34 ms | GPT-4o 7907.18 ms | DeBERTaV3-base | Compact representative efficiency row for low-latency detection versus LLM-scale screening; earlier arXiv preprint reports efficiency 5.44 vs. GPT-4o's 0.01; source: @li-etal-2025-piguard |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Blocklist and Allowlist Filtering
nodes.content:
# Blocklist and Allowlist Filtering

Content filtering applied at the input (and/or output) level, before any LLM call. Blocklist filtering rejects inputs containing known harmful or sensitive terms; allowlist filtering only permits a pre-approved vocabulary.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@schulhoff2024filtering\|Blocklist and Allowlist Filtering]] | Describes blocklist and allowlist filtering as complementary string-checking strategies, noting that coverage is limited to known attack patterns and that both can be bypassed by novel or obfuscated injections. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@owasp2025llm\|OWASP LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet]] | Practitioner pipeline extending blocklist filtering with fuzzy/typoglycemia-aware pattern matching on inputs and pattern-based leakage detection on outputs, bundled with structured prompts and a HITL gate in a single reference implementation. |

## Advantages

N/A

## Limitations

Coverage is inherently limited to known attack patterns. Novel or obfuscated injections can bypass both approaches.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | ASR (↓) | Best-of-N attack vs. blocklist filters | 89% | — | GPT-4o | BoN defeats all current filters with sufficient attempts; power-law scaling; cited from Hughes et al. in @owasp2025llm. |
| — | ASR (↓) | Best-of-N attack vs. blocklist filters | 78% | — | Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Same power-law scaling finding; cited from Hughes et al. in @owasp2025llm. |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Certified Input Erasure
nodes.content:
# Certified Input Erasure

A detection technique that wraps any existing safety filter with a systematic token-erasure loop to produce provably certified safety guarantees. Given a potentially adversarial prompt, the procedure generates every subsequence that can be formed by erasing up to _d_ tokens from the input (at the suffix, as arbitrary insertions, or at arbitrary positions), submits each subsequence to an `is-harmful` filter, and declares the original prompt harmful if any subsequence is flagged. The key theoretical result is that, for any adversarial extension of a harmful prompt of bounded length, at least one erased subsequence must equal the original harmful prompt — guaranteeing detection regardless of the attack algorithm used.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial\|Kumar et al. 2025 (erase-and-check)]] | Introduces erase-and-check, the first framework with provable certified safety against adversarial prompting: systematically erases token subsequences and flags the prompt harmful if any subsequence triggers the underlying safety filter, with formal guarantees holding for all adversarial suffix/insertion/infusion attacks up to a bounded length. |

## Advantages

- Provides the first provable certified safety guarantee against adversarial prompting: any adversarial suffix or token insertion of bounded length _d_ cannot cause a harmful prompt to be classified as safe.
- The certificate is attack-agnostic — it does not depend on knowing the attack algorithm and holds against all current and future attacks within the threat model.
