- Compatible with any safety filter, including proprietary black-box LLMs accessed via API, requiring no white-box access or retraining.
- A DistilBERT-based implementation achieves 100% certified accuracy on harmful prompts with sub-second latency per prompt.

## Limitations

- Computational cost scales as O(_d_) for suffix mode but becomes exponential for general infusion mode (arbitrary _d_ tokens removed from anywhere in the prompt), making full certification infeasible for large _d_ under the strongest threat model.
- The certificate covers only the harmful-prompt direction: the framework does not certify that safe prompts cannot be mislabelled as harmful by an adversary.
- Safe-prompt accuracy decreases at larger erase lengths when using an LLM-based safety filter, because more subsequences of benign prompts are checked and each may be falsely flagged.
- The certified guarantee is only as strong as the underlying safety filter — a weak filter produces a weak certificate.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AdvBench (520 harmful prompts) | Certified accuracy ↑ | erase-and-check (suffix mode, any d) | 92% | — | Llama 2 safety filter | Equals is-harmful accuracy on clean harmful prompts; constant across all erase lengths; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |
| AdvBench (120 harmful test prompts) | Certified accuracy ↑ | erase-and-check (suffix mode, any d) | 100% | — | DistilBERT safety filter | DistilBERT fine-tuned on 400 harmful + safe examples; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |
| AdvBench (520 safe prompts) | Safe-prompt accuracy ↑ | erase-and-check (suffix, d=20) | 97% | — | Llama 2 | Empirical; not certified in the safe direction; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |
| AdvBench (120 safe prompts) | Safe-prompt accuracy ↑ | erase-and-check (suffix, d=20) | 98% | — | DistilBERT | DistilBERT maintains high accuracy across all erase lengths; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |
| GCG adversarial prompts | Detection rate ↑ | RandEC (sampling ratio 0.3, d=20) | >90% | 0% at ratio=0 | DistilBERT | 0.03 s/prompt on NVIDIA A100; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |
| GCG adversarial prompts | Detection rate ↑ | GreedyEC (9 iterations) | >94% | 0% at 0 iterations | DistilBERT | 0.06 s/prompt; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Perplexity-Based Detection\|Perplexity-Based Detection]] | The paper identifies perplexity filtering as an empirical defence without certified guarantees that can be bypassed by fluent adversarial prompts (e.g. AutoDAN); erase-and-check addresses this gap by providing formal certification that holds regardless of the attack's surface statistics. |

nodes.name: Classifier-Based Detection
nodes.content:
# Classifier-Based Detection

A supervised classifier is placed in front of (and optionally behind) the agent pipeline to screen every incoming message for adversarial patterns before any LLM call is made, and optionally to monitor generated outputs before they are delivered. Unlike prompted LLM-based evaluators, the detector is trained specifically for the classification task rather than given a security persona at inference time. Implementations range from small fine-tuned encoder models (tens of millions of parameters, sub-100 ms latency), through frozen embedding models paired with lightweight classical-ML heads such as random forests or XGBoost, to full LLM-scale models fine-tuned as classifiers via next-token prediction or a linear value-head probe. These variants trade off retraining cost, semantic coverage, and false-positive behaviour, but all share the same core pattern: turn the input (or output) into a representation, classify it, and block if malicious. A constitution-driven synthetic data pipeline can train domain-specific classifiers without any manually labelled data. When the classifier flags an input, it is blocked before the guarded LLM ever sees it; an output classifier halts generation mid-stream if the emerging response crosses a harmfulness threshold.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail\|LlamaFirewall]] | Combines PromptGuard 2 (a fine-tuned DeBERTa classifier for jailbreak detection) with AlignmentCheck (an LLM-based chain-of-thought auditor for goal drift) and CodeShield (static analysis for generated code) into a layered, real-time guardrail framework deployed in production at Meta. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@protectai2023llmguard\|LLM Guard]] | Open-source security toolkit by Protect AI that screens LLM inputs using a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer classifier as its primary prompt injection scanner. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@swanda2023vigil\|Vigil]] | Multi-scanner open-source library that combines a DeBERTa-v3 transformer classifier, YARA heuristic rules, a ChromaDB vector database scanner, and canary tokens to detect prompt injection in LLM inputs and responses. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@meta2024promptguard\|PromptGuard]] | Meta's fine-tuned 86M-parameter DeBERTa model for detecting prompt injection and jailbreak attempts in LLM inputs; achieves near-zero false-negative rates but suffers from very high false-positive rates in held-out evaluations. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@protectai2023debertav3\|ProtectAI Prompt Injection Detection v1]] | Protect AI's earlier DeBERTa-v3 prompt-injection classifier, later used by Hackett et al. as a white-box baseline and shown to be especially susceptible to AML evasion attacks. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@deepset2023debertav3baseinjection\|deberta-v3-base-injection]] | deepset's lightweight Hugging Face DeBERTa-v3-base binary classifier for screening single input texts as `INJECTION` vs `LEGIT` before they reach a downstream LLM. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@fmops2023distilbertpromptinjection\|DistilBERT Prompt Injection Classifier]] | fmops's lightweight Hugging Face DistilBERT text-classification model for front-door prompt-injection screening, with sparse public documentation and most comparative evidence coming from external benchmark evaluations. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@kim2023robustsafetyclassifierlarge\|Adversarial Prompt Shield (APS)]] | Uses a lightweight DistilBERT dialogue-safety classifier hardened with BAND adversarial-noise training data to detect unsafe or jailbreak-style prompts before they reach the protected LLM. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots\|BAD classifier]] | Collects adversarial human-bot dialogues to train a multi-turn, context-aware safety classifier that screens unsafe user turns and can also score draft bot replies before release. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@protectai2024debertav2\|ProtectAI DeBERTa Prompt Injection Detector v2]] | Protect AI's standalone DeBERTa-v3-base binary input classifier for English prompt injection screening; released as open weights/ONNX and later identified by PIGuard as a strong open-source baseline with substantial over-defense. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@galinkin2024improvedlargelanguagemodel\|NeMo Guard Jailbreak Detect]] | Uses frozen retrieval-oriented prompt embeddings plus a lightweight classical classifier (best: Snowflake embeddings + random forest) to detect jailbreaks, outperforming PromptGuard and deepset's DeBERTa detector on the paper's held-out set and on JailbreakHub. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@ayub2024embeddingbasedclassifiersdetectprompt\|Embedding-based classifiers (Ayub & Majumdar)]] | Trains traditional ML classifiers (Logistic Regression, XGBoost, Random Forests) on frozen sentence-encoder embedding vectors to classify prompt injection without fine-tuning the embedding model itself; a simpler prior approach that ZEDD's drift-based method supersedes. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@anthropic2026prompt\|Anthropic Claude for Chrome (classifier layer)]] | Deploys a production inference-time classifier that scans all untrusted content entering the context window — including hidden text, manipulated images, and deceptive UI elements — and adjusts agent behaviour on detection; classifiers were iteratively improved between research preview and full beta launch via continuous human red teaming, contributing to a combined ~1% ASR against an internal adaptive attacker. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@arthur2023shield\|Arthur Shield]] | API-first LLM firewall that wraps applications at both input (`validate_prompt`) and output (`validate_response`) boundaries; uses a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 binary classifier focused on Role Play and Instruction Manipulation attacks, with an enterprise proprietary model achieving F1 = 0.89 on the deepset benchmark at 0.03 s latency versus F1 = 0.52 for the open-source version. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@rai2024guardian\|GUARDIAN]] | Uses a fine-tuned `bert-base-uncased` toxicity classifier as the pre-prompt screening layer in a three-stage guardrail stack, routing suspicious requests to an ethical rewrite generator before they reach the protected Llama-2 model. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@sharma2025constitutionalclassifiersdefendinguniversal\|Constitutional Classifiers]] | Fine-tunes LLM-scale input and streaming output classifiers from a natural-language constitution and a synthetic data pipeline; survived 3,000+ hours of human red teaming without a universal CBRN jailbreak, reducing held-out ASR from 86% to 5% at only +0.38% false-positive rate on production traffic. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@peng2024rapidresponsemitigatingllm\|Rapid Response (Guard Fine-tuning)]] | Proposes a reactive rapid-response paradigm: after observing a single novel jailbreak, an auxiliary LLM proliferates ~1,000 stylistic variants, and an input guard classifier (Llama-Guard-2-8B) is fine-tuned on them to block the whole attack class — achieving >99.6% in-distribution and >93.6% out-of-distribution ASR reduction from just one observed example per strategy. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@chen2026proactivehardeningllmdefenseshaste\|HASTE]] | Uses a closed-loop hard-negative-mining pipeline to stress-test an existing prompt-attack detector, generate evasive attack variants, and retrain the classifier on the failures it misses so robustness improves proactively rather than only after deployment incidents. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@li-etal-2025-piguard\|PIGuard]] | Fine-tunes DeBERTaV3-base via the MOF (Mitigating Over-defense for Free) strategy: identifies token-level classifier biases by feeding each vocabulary token individually, synthesises benign sentences containing those trigger tokens, and retrains from scratch — achieving 83.48% average accuracy across malicious, benign, and over-defense dimensions without requiring any labelled false-positive examples. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@guardrailsai2026detect\|Guardrails AI Detect Jailbreak]] | Vendor-managed jailbreak detector surfaced in the AI Guardrails Index leaderboard, where it posts the top reported Max F1 (0.8118) alongside 0.0527 ms latency on the jailbreaking slice. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@shone2025enhance\|Azure Prompt Shields]] | Microsoft Azure's managed API that applies ML and NLP classifiers with contextual intent awareness to detect and block both direct prompt injection (jailbreaks) and indirect/cross-prompt injection (XPIA) in real time, before inputs reach the guarded LLM; additionally incorporates Spotlighting to separate trusted from untrusted input segments. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@lakeraai2024lakeraguard\|Lakera Guard]] | Lakera's managed guardrail platform screens prompts for prompt injection and jailbreaks via a project policy layer, while also bundling moderation, PII/data-leakage, and unknown-link checks with policy tuning and request logging. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@aws2024guardrails\|Amazon Bedrock Guardrails]] | AWS managed multi-policy guardrail service that wraps any foundation model with six configurable safeguard policies — including a prompt-attack detector for injections and jailbreaks, a denied-topics classifier, word filters, PII redaction, contextual grounding checks, and Automated Reasoning checks — evaluated on both input and output through a model-agnostic API. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@cloudflare2024promptinjection\|Cloudflare AI Security for Apps]] | Cloudflare WAF feature that scores every incoming prompt from 1–99 for injection likelihood at the network edge, before the request reaches the origin LLM; operators build blocking or challenge rules from the score, composing it with bot-management and PII signals in a single rule expression. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@cloudflare2025blockunsafe\|Cloudflare Firewall for AI — Unsafe Content Moderation]] | Cloudflare WAF feature that runs Llama Guard 3 at the network edge to classify incoming prompts against 13 unsafe-topic categories (hate, self-harm, violent crimes, child exploitation, etc.) and exposes the result as WAF boolean and array fields; operators build granular per-category block or log rules complementing the injection-score feature. |

## Advantages

- Sub-100 ms classification latency (19–92 ms on an A100 GPU at the 22 M–86 M parameter scale) makes small-encoder classifiers suitable for real-time, resource-constrained deployments.
- LLM-scale fine-tuned classifiers capture semantically complex attack patterns (role-play, authority-override, multilingual obfuscation) that lexical classifiers miss, and constitution-driven synthetic training removes the need for manual data collection.
- A streaming output classifier can halt generation mid-token, preserving time-to-first-token for benign users while blocking harmful completions.
- Requires no fine-tuning of the backend agent or changes to its prompt.
- Layering a cheap classifier before a heavier LLM auditor achieves defence-in-depth: the classifier handles the bulk of clear-cut cases at low cost, and the LLM auditor is invoked only for inputs that pass the first screen.
- Open-weight classifiers can be run on CPU or GPU without API dependency.

## Limitations

- Lexical classifiers detect explicit jailbreak phrasing (instruction overrides, template injection tokens) but are blind to injections that redirect agent goals without using recognisable adversarial keywords.
- A chained LLM-based auditor (AlignmentCheck) substantially reduces this gap but adds significant latency and requires a large, capable backbone model to achieve acceptable false-positive rates.
- The combined pipeline reduces task utility by approximately 5 percentage points on AgentDojo relative to an undefended agent.
- The LLM-based auditing component can itself be targeted by adversarially crafted inputs designed to confuse both the protected agent and the guardrail model simultaneously.
- Domain-specific classifiers (e.g. CBRN-focused Constitutional Classifiers) do not protect against unrelated harm categories without a new constitution and retraining.
- LLM-scale classifiers add meaningful inference overhead (~23.7% extra compute) and may be prohibitive for latency-sensitive edge deployments.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | PromptGuard 2 86M | 7.53% | 17.63% undefended | Various (10 agent LLMs) | 57% drop in ASR; utility 47.01% vs 47.73% baseline; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | AlignmentCheck (Llama 4 Maverick) | 2.89% | 17.63% undefended | Llama 4 Maverick as guardrail | 84% drop in ASR; utility falls to 43.09%; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | PromptGuard 2 86M + AlignmentCheck (Llama 4 Maverick) | 1.75% | 17.63% undefended | Combined | >90% reduction in ASR; utility 42.68%; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| Direct jailbreak eval (private OOD, English) | Recall @ 1% FPR ↑ | PromptGuard 2 86M | 97.5% | PromptGuard 1: 21.2% | mDeBERTa-base | Large false-positive reduction vs v1; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| Meta goal hijacking benchmark | Detection rate ↑ | AlignmentCheck (Llama 4 Maverick) | 83.8% | — | Llama 4 Maverick | FPR 2.5%; best precision-recall balance; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| AdvBench + GCG adversarial suffix | ASR ↓ | APS WP 20 | 28.0% | 75.6% BAD classifier; 88.7% undefended | Falcon | Robust DistilBERT dialogue-safety classifier hardened with BAND suffix noise; best reported APS jailbreak-screening result; source: @kim2023robustsafetyclassifierlarge |
| BAND Random corpora average (10-word random suffix) | Unsafe F1 ↑ | APS Random | 74.2 | 39.6 BAD classifier; 38.5 Moderation API | DistilBERT-based APS | Adversarial-noise training preserves unsafe-prompt detection under randomized suffix obfuscation; source: @kim2023robustsafetyclassifierlarge |
| BAD test set | Unsafe F1 ↑ | Multi-turn Safety Classifier (Adv. Dialog) | 80.8 | 60.7 Safety Classifier+ | 622M classifier | Context-aware multi-turn classifier trained on adversarial human-bot dialogues; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | LLM Guard (transformer, Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.999 | — | DeBERTa-v3 | FPR 0.127; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | FPR ↓ | LLM Guard (transformer, Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.127 | Vigil th=0.999: 0.014; Rebuff: 0.034 | DeBERTa-v3 | Highest FPR of three solutions; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F-beta ↑ | LLM Guard (transformer, Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.989 | Vigil th=0.999: 0.998; Rebuff: 0.997 | DeBERTa-v3 | beta=1/11; lowest F-beta of three solutions; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F1 ↑ | LLM Guard (transformer, Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.994 | — | DeBERTa-v3 | source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | LLM Guard (transformer, Sentence mode, th=0.92) | 1.000 | Full mode: 0.999 | DeBERTa-v3 | FPR 0.157; higher FPR than Full mode; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | FPR ↓ | LLM Guard (transformer, Sentence mode, th=0.92) | 0.157 | Full mode: 0.127 | DeBERTa-v3 | Sentence mode raises both recall and FPR; Full mode described as more practical; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.838 | — | DeBERTa-v3 + YARA + VectorDB + canary | Lowest recall of three solutions; canary check contributes zero true positives; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | FPR ↓ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.000 | LLM Guard 0.127; Rebuff 0.034 | — | Lowest FPR of three solutions at default config; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | F-beta ↑ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.998 | LLM Guard 0.989; Rebuff 0.997 | — | Best F-beta (beta=1/11) of three solutions at default config; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks) | FPR ↓ | PromptGuard | ≈ 1.00 (very high, most tasks) | 0.00 DataSentinel | Prompt-Guard-86M | Flags nearly all inputs as contaminated; FNR ≈ 0 but at cost of near-total false alarm rate; reported in @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| OOD Jailbreak Set (English, held-out) | TPR ↑ | PromptGuard | 97.5% | — | mDeBERTa-v3-base | FPR 3.9%, AUC 0.975; dataset entirely excluded from training; source: @meta2024promptguard |
| OOD Jailbreak Set (English, held-out) | FPR ↓ | PromptGuard | 3.9% | — | mDeBERTa-v3-base | Paired with TPR 97.5%; 3–5% FPR may be too high without threshold tuning or fine-tuning; source: @meta2024promptguard |
| CyberSecEval Indirect Injections | TPR ↑ | PromptGuard | 71.4% | — | mDeBERTa-v3-base | FPR 1.0%, AUC 0.966; harder embedded injections with negative-document foils; source: @meta2024promptguard |
| JailbreakHub | F1 ↑ | Random Forest + Snowflake embeddings | 0.9601 | PromptGuard: 0.3029; deberta-v3-base-injection: 0.1947; gelectra: 0.1926 | `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-long` + random forest | Realistic public jailbreak benchmark; source: @galinkin2024improvedlargelanguagemodel |
| JailbreakHub | FPR ↓ | Random Forest + Snowflake embeddings | 0.0042 | PromptGuard: 0.5066; deberta-v3-base-injection: 0.9596; gelectra: 0.9729 | `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-long` + random forest | Sub-1% false-positive rate on the paper's main realistic public benchmark; source: @galinkin2024improvedlargelanguagemodel |
| TaskTracker test set (public, OOD) | ROC AUC ↑ | PromptGuard | 0.974 | TaskTracker probe 0.996 | Phi-3 14B | Used as baseline in TaskTracker/DRIFT evaluation; reported in @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| AgentDojo (80 agentic scenarios) | ASR ↓ | PromptGuard (adaptive search attack) | 94% | 26% static ASR | Gemini-2.5 Pro | White-box greedy-search bypass; reported in @nasr2025attackermovessecondstronger |
| Internal Best-of-N adaptive attacker (100 attempts / env) | ASR ↓ | Classifier pipeline (updated) + RL-trained Claude Opus 4.5 | ~1% | Original launch config (substantially higher, exact not disclosed) | Claude Opus 4.5 | Browser use; classifier scans hidden text, images, deceptive UI; iteratively improved post-launch; source: @anthropic2026prompt |
| Authors' curated dataset (467 K prompts, 80/20 split) | F1 (↑) | Random Forest + OpenAI embeddings | 0.868 | ProtectAI DeBERTa-v2: 0.859; deepset DeBERTa: 0.860; Myadav SetFit: 0.709 | text-embedding-3-small + sklearn RF | Highest F1 of five classifiers compared; beats fine-tuned DeBERTa models by ~1 pp; source: @ayub2024embeddingbasedclassifiersdetectprompt |
| Authors' curated dataset | Recall (↑) | Random Forest + OpenAI embeddings | 0.870 | ProtectAI DeBERTa-v2: 0.991; deepset DeBERTa: 0.988 | text-embedding-3-small + sklearn RF | Lower recall than DeBERTa fine-tunes (91–99%); more false negatives reach the LLM; source: @ayub2024embeddingbasedclassifiersdetectprompt |
| Custom jailbreak dataset (50 attacks) | Attack prompts blocked ↑ | GUARDIAN pre-processing tier | 60% | 40% after system-prompt filter only | Llama-2-7b-chat | 30/50 blocked cumulatively after adding the BERT toxicity classifier; no classifier-only ablation; source: @rai2024guardian |
| deepset prompt-injection dataset | F1 ↑ | Arthur Engine free version | 0.52 (0.44–0.60) | — | protectai/deberta-v3-base-prompt-injection-v2 | Latency 0.966 s/inference; source: @arthur2023shield |
| deepset prompt-injection dataset | F1 ↑ | Arthur Engine enterprise version | 0.89 (0.85–0.93) | Free: 0.52 | Proprietary (Arthur AI) | Latency 0.03 s/inference; ~30× faster than free version; source: @arthur2023shield |
| deepset prompt-injections dataset (evaluation split) | Accuracy ↑ | deberta-v3-base-injection | 99.14% | — | microsoft/deberta-v3-base | Hugging Face model card; validation loss 0.0673 after 3 epochs of fine-tuning; source: @deepset2023debertav3baseinjection |
| RapidResponseBench (ID, avg 6 strategies) | ASR (↓) | Guard Fine-tuning (1 observed example) | ~0.4% (>99.6% avg. reduction) | Pre-adaptation ASR | avg GPT-4o / Llama-3-8B / Mistral-7B | 1 observed example per strategy; proliferated 1,000 variants; source: @peng2024rapidresponsemitigatingllm |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| outperforms:: [[Detection/Input Screening/LLM-Based Evaluation\|LLM-Based Evaluation]] | On AgentDojo at fixed 3% utility cost, PromptGuard 2 86M achieves 3.3% ASR versus competing open-weight LLM-based scanners (LLM Warden 15.4%, Deepset 15.3%), making it the best-performing open-weight scanner at that operating point. |
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/LLM-Based Evaluation\|LLM-Based Evaluation]] | AlignmentCheck refines the LLM-as-evaluator pattern by auditing agent chain-of-thought for goal drift rather than screening raw input text, closing the gap that lexical classifiers and prompt-level evaluators leave against semantic misalignment attacks. |
| embeds:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Delimiters]] | Azure Prompt Shields incorporates Spotlighting — a delimiter-family trusted/untrusted input-separation technique — as its sub-component for indirect prompt injection detection, combining classifier-based screening with structured input marking in a single managed API. |

nodes.name: Input Screening
nodes.content:
# Input Screening

Techniques that evaluate the raw input text, tokens, or embeddings before the agent acts on them. A screening layer intercepts every incoming message and applies one or more detection signals — deterministic pattern matching, statistical anomaly scoring, neural classification, semantic drift measurement, known-answer canaries, or certified erasure — to decide whether the input contains adversarial content. Flagged inputs are blocked or dropped from context; clean inputs proceed to the agent. These defences operate entirely on the input surface and require no inspection of the model's internal reasoning or execution trace.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Classifier-Based Detection\|Classifier-Based Detection]] | Uses a supervised classifier to screen every message before the agent acts on it, ranging from fine-tuned encoder models (DeBERTa-scale) to frozen embeddings paired with lightweight classical-ML heads; optionally chains a heavier LLM auditor for defence-in-depth. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/LLM-Based Evaluation\|LLM-Based Evaluation]] | A prompted LLM evaluator screens inputs for adversarial intent; variants use either dedicated guard models or parallel shadow-stack self-checking and may return a binary verdict or localise the harmful span. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Perplexity-Based Detection\|Perplexity-Based Detection]] | Exploits the statistical abnormality of optimizer-generated text by scoring whole-prompt or per-token perplexity under a language model, then either rejecting anomalous inputs or localising suspicious spans; variants add lightweight classifiers or contextual smoothing on top of the perplexity signal. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Semantic Drift Detection\|Semantic Drift Detection]] | Measures cosine drift between a known-clean baseline embedding and the candidate prompt embedding to detect injections that cause measurable semantic shift, without requiring LLM inference at detection time. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Prompt-Neighborhood Screening\|Prompt-Neighborhood Screening]] | Maintains an evolving memory of observed or simulated jailbreak prompts and blocks new inputs whose embeddings land inside those risky semantic neighborhoods, making screening proactive across multi-turn attacks. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Known-Answer Detection\|Known-Answer Detection]] | Inserts a canary instruction with a known expected answer, or a hidden secret key to be echoed back, and flags the input if the model no longer preserves that answer; advanced variants fine-tune a dedicated detector for adaptive robustness. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Certified Input Erasure\|Certified Input Erasure]] | Wraps any safety filter with a systematic token-erasure loop that checks every subsequence formed by erasing up to d tokens, providing provably certified safety guarantees against bounded-length adversarial extensions. |
| down:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Blocklist and Allowlist Filtering\|Blocklist and Allowlist Filtering]] | Checks inputs (and optionally outputs) against maintained pattern lists — blocking known harmful terms or permitting only an approved vocabulary — via deterministic string matching requiring no model inference. |

## Advantages

- Operates as a preprocessing layer with no modification to the backend agent's architecture, weights, or prompt — applicable to closed-source API-only models.
- Spans a wide cost–accuracy spectrum: from sub-millisecond deterministic pattern matching (blocklist/allowlist), through sub-100 ms classifier inference (19–92 ms on A100), to LLM-based evaluation and certified erasure, allowing operators to choose a trade-off appropriate to their deployment.
- Classifier-based and perplexity-based methods are training-free with respect to the production LLM and add minimal latency.
- Certified approaches (erase-and-check) provide attack-agnostic formal guarantees that hold against all current and future attacks within the bounded threat model.
- Semantic drift detection requires no LLM inference at detection time — a single cosine similarity computation is the entire overhead.

## Limitations

- Surface-level methods (classifiers, perplexity filters) are blind to injections that redirect agent goals without using recognisable adversarial patterns — stealthy, semantically fluent attacks (e.g. AutoDAN) bypass perplexity-based detection entirely.
- LLM-based evaluation adds significant latency and API cost and can itself be targeted by adversarially crafted inputs.
- Perplexity filtering produces ~10% false positives on benign prompts when used standalone; Known-Answer Detection suffers high FPR on complex agent benchmarks (utility drops to 36.6% on AgentDojo).
- Certified erasure scales exponentially for the strongest threat model (arbitrary token infusion) and certifies only the harmful-prompt direction.
- Most screening methods make a binary accept/reject decision on the entire input, so legitimate content within a partially contaminated prompt is often discarded along with the adversarial payload; span-localising variants are the main exception.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | PromptGuard 2 86M + AlignmentCheck | 1.75% | 17.63% undefended | Combined (Llama 4 Maverick guardrail) | >90% reduction in ASR; utility 42.68%; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| AgentDojo | ASR (↓) | PromptGuard 2 86M | 7.53% | 17.63% undefended | Various (10 agent LLMs) | 57% drop in ASR; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| BAD test set | Unsafe F1 ↑ | Multi-turn Safety Classifier (Adv. Dialog) | 80.8 | 60.7 Safety Classifier+ | 622M classifier | Context-aware adversarial-dialogue screening result propagated from Classifier-Based Detection; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |
| AdvBench (GCG) | ASR (↓) | Perplexity filter (basic) | 0.00 | 0.79 undefended | Vicuna-7B | 0/100 attacks pass; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| Adversarial prompt test set (machine-generated GCG) | F2 (↑) | LightGBM (PPL + seq-len) | 99.1% | 91.6% PPL threshold alone | GPT-2 scorer | 4 false negatives, 1 false positive / 26,188 benign; source: @alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks |
| Authors' GCG-derived adversarial-prompt dataset | Token F1 ↑ | PGM-based detector | 0.9398 | 0.9354 optimization-based detector | GPT-2 124M scorer | Span-localising perplexity detector; source: @hu2024tokenleveladversarialpromptdetection |
| LLMail-Inject (re-annotated) | Accuracy (↑) | ZEDD | 95.55% | 90.75% SBERT variant | Mistral-7B Instruct | Best accuracy across four encoders; source: @sekar2026zeroshotembeddingdriftdetection |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks) | FPR (↓) | DataSentinel | ≈ 0.00 | 0.08–0.10 KAD | Mistral-7B detection / LLaMA3-8B backend | FPR = 0 across all target tasks; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 existing attacks) | RL ↑ | PromptLocate | 0.94-0.98 | 0.23-0.78 attribution baselines | LLaMA3-8B-Instruct backend | Localization rate on injected spans; ES = 0.93-0.99 and post-removal ASV = 0.06-0.08; source: @jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection |
| AdvBench (520 harmful prompts) | Certified accuracy (↑) | erase-and-check (suffix mode) | 100% | — | DistilBERT safety filter | Provably certified; source: @kumar2025certifyingllmsafetyadversarial |
| TaskTracker test set (public, OOD) | ROC AUC (↑) | PromptGuard | 0.974 | — | Phi-3 14B | Used as baseline in DRIFT evaluation; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| JailbreakHub | F1 ↑ | Random Forest + Snowflake embeddings | 0.9601 | PromptGuard: 0.3029; deberta-v3-base-injection: 0.1947; gelectra: 0.1926 | `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-long` + random forest | Realistic public jailbreak benchmark; propagated from Classifier-Based Detection; source: @galinkin2024improvedlargelanguagemodel |
| JailbreakHub | FPR ↓ | Random Forest + Snowflake embeddings | 0.0042 | PromptGuard: 0.5066; deberta-v3-base-injection: 0.9596; gelectra: 0.9729 | `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-long` + random forest | Strong operational false-positive result on a public jailbreak benchmark; propagated from Classifier-Based Detection; source: @galinkin2024improvedlargelanguagemodel |
| English Prompt avg. (7 benchmarks) | F1 ↑ | Qwen3Guard-8B-Gen (best mode) | 90.0% | WildGuard-7B: 79.4%; NemoGuard-8B: 82.9% | Qwen3-8B backbone | State-of-the-art on all seven English prompt benchmarks; source: @zhao2025qwen3guardtechnicalreport |
| 13-benchmark suite (3 guardrail tasks, weighted avg.) | F1 ↑ | GuardReasoner 8B | 84.09% | 81.96% WildGuard 7B | Llama-3.1-8B | Reasoning-trained guard model; broad prompt/response/refusal moderation summary; source: @liu2025guardreasonerreasoningbasedllmsafeguards |
| Harmful Content (multi-benchmark avg.) | AUC ↑ | Granite Guardian | 0.871 | — | Granite (instruction-tuned) | Avg. across multiple benchmarks; source: @padhi2024graniteguardian |
| RAG Hallucination (multi-benchmark avg.) | AUC ↑ | Granite Guardian | 0.854 | — | Granite (instruction-tuned) | Covers context relevance, groundedness, answer relevance; source: @padhi2024graniteguardian |
| Custom jailbreak dataset (50 attacks) | Attack prompts blocked ↑ | GUARDIAN pre-processing tier | 60% | 40% after system-prompt filter only | Llama-2-7b-chat | 30/50 blocked cumulatively after adding the BERT toxicity classifier; no classifier-only ablation; source: @rai2024guardian |
| deepset prompt-injections dataset (evaluation split) | Accuracy ↑ | deberta-v3-base-injection | 99.14% | — | microsoft/deberta-v3-base | Lightweight binary classifier; model-card self-eval on deepset's own split; source: @deepset2023debertav3baseinjection |
| HASTE out-of-loop evaluation set | Detection accuracy ↑ | HM-Bal+All (M10) | 94.44% | 82.12% at M0; 92.80% Base (M10) | Protect AI DeBERTa-v3 | Classifier-based input screening improved by proactive hard-negative mining; source: @chen2026proactivehardeningllmdefenseshaste |
| Pint-Benchmark | Accuracy ↑ | Lakera Guard | 98.10% | — | Unknown | Reported in @wang2025protectllmagentprompt as an external comparison on Lakera's benchmark. |
| PINT + BIPIA + WildGuard + NotInject (combined) | Avg Accuracy ↑ | PIGuard | 83.48% | ProtectAIv2 64.01% | DeBERTaV3-base | MOF strategy balances malicious (77.39%), benign (85.74%), and over-defense (87.32%) accuracy; source: @li-etal-2025-piguard |
| System overhead comparison (Table 1) | Inference time ↓ | PIGuard | 15.34 ms | GPT-4o 7907.18 ms | DeBERTaV3-base | Representative low-latency neural input screening row; earlier arXiv preprint reports efficiency 5.44 vs. GPT-4o's 0.01; source: @li-etal-2025-piguard |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Known-Answer Detection
nodes.content:
# Known-Answer Detection

A detection mechanism that inserts a small canary instruction with a pre-specified correct answer before or alongside untrusted content, then checks whether the model still returns that answer. If the answer changes, the system treats this as evidence that the untrusted content overrode the trusted instruction and flags the input as contaminated. Simple variants use a fixed known-answer test as a lightweight preflight check; more advanced variants hide a randomly generated secret key inside a dedicated detection prompt and use a separate detection LLM to verify that the key is echoed verbatim. The shared idea is prompt-integrity testing: the defender asks the model to prove it is still following a trusted instruction after seeing untrusted text.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@nakajima2022preflight\|Preflight prompt injection test]] | Proposes a lightweight canary-style preflight check: include a question with a known expected answer (for example, "dog") and treat any different answer as a sign that prompt injection may have overridden the trusted instruction. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt\|DataSentinel]] | Fine-tunes a dedicated generative detection LLM via minimax optimisation — alternating GCG-style adversarial inner maximisation with QLoRA outer minimisation — so the detector learns to resist the strongest adaptive evasion attacks while maintaining near-zero false-positive and false-negative rates on unseen tasks and LLMs. |

## Advantages

- Can be extremely simple to deploy: the basic variant only needs a small canary question with a known answer and no retraining.
- Directly tests prompt integrity rather than merely matching suspicious surface patterns.
- Near-zero false-positive rate is achievable in advanced variants: fine-tuning the detection LLM eliminates the high-FPR failure mode seen in static classifiers (e.g. PromptGuard achieving FNR ≈ 0 at the cost of FPR ≈ 1) and zero-shot KAD.
- Adaptive robustness: the minimax training loop explicitly exposes the detector to optimised adversarial evasion, achieving FNR ≤ 0.06 under adaptive attacks (non-same-task setting) versus KAD's FNR of 0.27–0.93.
- Generalises across LLMs and tasks: a 500-sample generic fine-tuning corpus suffices; the detector transfers to unseen injected-task combinations and to backend LLMs not seen during training.
- Low operational overhead after a one-time training run: detection adds ~10% query latency; a 1B detection LLM achieves comparable accuracy at 0.7 s per query.

## Limitations

- The basic canary form is only a heuristic: an attack can succeed while preserving the expected answer, and benign variation can also perturb the answer.
- Same-task attacks evade detection: when the injected task matches the target task the attack reduces to adversarial input perturbation and FNR rises sharply (~0.87).
- Requires white-box backend LLM at fine-tuning time: the inner minimax loop needs gradient access to the backend LLM; defenders must proxy with a surrogate model if the backend is proprietary.
- Not zero-shot deployable: a one-time QLoRA fine-tuning step (~3 GPU-hours, ~$0.90) is required before deployment, unlike prompt-based or static classifier guards.
- Struggles on complex agent benchmarks: external evaluations (AgentDojo, InjecAgent) show high false-positive rates causing utility to drop to 36.6% vs 81.4% undefended.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Known-answer preflight "injection test" / canary check | — | — | LLM prompt-response behaviour (illustrative) | Qualitative canary-style preflight check: the expected answer is "dog," and any different answer is treated as a possible injection signal; no quantitative evaluation is reported; source: @nakajima2022preflight |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks) | FPR ↓ | DataSentinel | ≈ 0.00 | 0.08–0.10 KAD | Mistral-7B detection / LLaMA3-8B backend | FPR = 0 across all target tasks; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks) | FNR ↓ | DataSentinel vs. NeuralExec | ≤ 0.01 | 0.10–0.21 KAD | Mistral-7B detection / LLaMA3-8B backend | Optimization-based attack; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| Open-Prompt-Injection (7 tasks, adaptive attacks) | FNR ↓ | DataSentinel (Minimax) | ≤ 0.06 (non-same-task) / 0.87 (same-task) | 0.27–0.93 KAD | Mistral-7B detection / LLaMA3-8B backend | Same-task attack degrades to adversarial examples; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | DataSentinel | 1.7% | 3.1% no defence | gpt-4o | Direct attack; reported in @wang2026defendingpromptinjectiondatafilter; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |
| AgentDojo | Utility ↑ | DataSentinel | 36.6% | 81.4% no defence | gpt-4o | High FPR causes severe utility drop on complex agent tasks; reported in @wang2026defendingpromptinjectiondatafilter; source: @liu2025datasentinelgametheoreticdetectionprompt |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: LLM-Based Evaluation
nodes.content:
# LLM-Based Evaluation

A prompted LLM evaluator acts as a security gate, evaluating prompts and, in richer deployments, responses or tool-related turns for adversarial intent or policy violations before the system proceeds. The evaluator may be a dedicated guard model or a parallel shadow invocation of the protected model itself.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@schulhoff2024llmeval\|LLM-Based Evaluation]] | Describes using a dedicated LLM instance as a security gate that screens inputs for adversarial intent before they reach the primary model, with the evaluator prompted with a security-focused persona and returning a malicious/benign verdict. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wu2024llmsdefendjailbreakingpractical\|Wu et al. 2024 (SELF DEFEND)]] | Runs a parallel "shadow stack" that asks the LLM to quote any harmful span hidden in the input and interrupts the normal generation path with a refusal if such a span is found. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection\|PromptLocate]] | Semantically segments already-flagged contaminated context, then uses a tailored detector as a segment-level oracle to localize injected instruction and data spans for removal, recovery, or source tracing. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@protectai2023rebuff\|Rebuff]] | Self-hardening prompt injection detector by Protect AI that uses a secondary LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo or GPT-4o) as one of four layered scanners; detected patterns are stored back into a vector database to expand coverage over time. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@hossain2025multiagentllmdefensepipeline\|Hossain et al. — Multi-Agent LLM Defense Pipeline]] | Routes every query through a Coordinator LLM that classifies input as safe or malicious before the Domain LLM is invoked, and mandatorily passes all generated responses through a Guard LLM that checks for policy violations before delivery — providing dual-sided (input and output) LLM-based evaluation in a single pipeline. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@jarvis2023how\|How to implement LLM guardrails]] | OpenAI Cookbook guide showing a lightweight deployment pattern where prompted secondary LLM calls run asynchronously as input topicality and output moderation evaluators, returning allow/deny or rubric-score decisions that gate the main model's response. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@inan2023llamaguardllmbasedinputoutput\|Llama Guard]] | Meta's original open-weight Llama-2-7B safeguard model for generative safety classification; moderates both user prompts and assistant responses against a prompt-supplied policy taxonomy and returns `safe`/`unsafe` plus violated category IDs. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@wang2025openguardrailsconfigurableunifiedscalable\|OpenGuardrails]] | Fully open-source guardrail platform that fine-tunes a 14B LLM guard model (compressed to 3.3B via GPTQ) for unified content-safety, prompt-manipulation, and data-leak detection across 119 languages, with a continuously tunable per-request sensitivity threshold derived from first-token logits. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@han2024wildguardopenonestopmoderation\|WildGuard]] | Open-source multi-task moderation model (Mistral-7B fine-tuned on 92K examples) that simultaneously classifies prompt harmfulness, response harmfulness, and refusal in a single pass; matches or exceeds GPT-4 on adversarial prompt detection and reduces jailbreak ASR from 79.8% to 2.4% as an inference-time filter. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@liu2025guardreasonerreasoningbasedllmsafeguards\|GuardReasoner]] | Reasoning-trained guard model that emits step-by-step safety rationales plus verdicts for prompt harmfulness, response harmfulness, and refusal/compliance, improving robustness on adversarial moderation benchmarks at higher latency. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@meta2024llamaguard3\|Llama Guard 3]] | Meta's open-weight Llama-3.1-8B safeguard model for generative safety classification; moderates prompts and responses in eight languages and adds dedicated checks for search-tool misuse and code-interpreter abuse. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@zhao2025qwen3guardtechnicalreport\|Qwen3Guard]] | Multilingual guardrail model series (0.6B–8B) with a tri-class verdict (safe / controversial / unsafe) enabling operator-tunable safety tolerance, plus a streaming variant that attaches per-token classification heads to a frozen backbone for real-time response interruption; covers 119 languages. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@zeng2024shieldgemma\|ShieldGemma]] | Google's Gemma-based safeguard model family for input/output moderation, classifying prompts and responses into safety categories with lightweight open-weight 2B and 9B variants. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@joshi2025cultureguard\|CultureGuard / Nemotron Safety Guard]] | Introduces a culturally-aware multilingual safety dataset and Nemotron Safety Guard, an 8B guard model for classifying unsafe content across languages with less English-centric bias. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@padhi2024graniteguardian\|Granite Guardian]] | IBM's open-source instruction-fine-tuned guardrail family that covers the full prompt/response risk surface — harmful content, jailbreaks, RAG hallucination (context relevance, groundedness, answer relevance), and agentic function-call errors — via a unified guardian_config interface with a bring-your-own-criteria mode for custom risk definitions. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@kasundra2026aprielguard\|AprielGuard]] | ServiceNow's 8B-parameter unified safeguard that detects both safety risks (16 harm categories) and adversarial attacks (prompt injection, jailbreaks, agentic hijacks) in a single inference pass; explicitly covers multi-turn conversations and agentic tool-call workflows with a 32k-token context window and optional structured reasoning traces. |

## Advantages

Tunable independently of the primary task model; no changes to the main prompt.

## Limitations

Additional latency and API cost per request.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | Rebuff (4 scanners, default thresholds) | 0.981 | — | GPT-4o + VectorDB + heuristics + canary | FPR 0.034; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | Rebuff (4 scanners, default thresholds) | 0.034 | LLM Guard Full 0.127; Vigil 0.000 | GPT-4o | Middle ground between LLM Guard (0.127) and Vigil (0.000); source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F-beta ↑ | Rebuff (4 scanners, default thresholds) | 0.997 | — | GPT-4o | beta=1/11; source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Rebuff secondary model (GPT-4o, th=0.752) | 0.868 | GPT-3.5-turbo th=0.802: 0.914 | GPT-4o | GPT-4o fewer false positives (FPR 0.021 vs 0.499); source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications |
| HPI_ATTACK_DATASET (v1 Taxonomy, 100 cases) | ASR (↓) | Chain/Coordinator + Guard | 0.0% | 30% undefended | ChatGLM-6B | 55 attacks, 8 categories; dual-sided LLM pipeline; source: @hossain2025multiagentllmdefensepipeline |
| HPI_ATTACK_DATASET (Phase 2, 50 cases each) | ASR (↓) | Coordinator + Guard | 0.0% | 20–30% undefended | Llama2-13B | Pre-input classification pipeline; source: @hossain2025multiagentllmdefensepipeline |

nodes.name: Perplexity-Based Detection
nodes.content:
# Perplexity-Based Detection

Detection defences that intercept adversarial inputs before the primary model processes them by exploiting the statistical abnormality of optimizer-generated text. Early variants score the whole input under a language model and reject prompts whose perplexity exceeds a threshold; later variants combine perplexity with token-sequence length features or contextual smoothing to reduce false positives and, in Hu et al. 2024, localise suspicious token spans instead of only flagging the entire prompt. These approaches are training-free with respect to the production LLM, though some variants train a lightweight auxiliary classifier on top of the perplexity signal. Paraphrasing and retokenization techniques that also appear in the sources below are covered under the Sanitization/Input Perturbation node for their sanitization aspect; this node focuses on the perplexity-based statistical anomaly scoring used for detection.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks\|Jain et al. 2023 — Perplexity Filtering]] | Benchmarks perplexity filtering (basic and windowed), paraphrasing via ChatGPT, and BPE-dropout retokenization against the GCG jailbreak attack, finding that perplexity filtering and paraphrasing are highly effective while retokenization offers inconsistent gains. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks\|Alon & Kamfonas 2023]] | Scores inputs with GPT-2 perplexity and combines the signal with token-sequence length in a LightGBM classifier, achieving F2=99.1% against GCG-style machine-generated attacks while resolving the false-positive problem of a plain perplexity threshold; cited as the key motivation behind AutoDAN's stealthiness design goal. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@hu2024tokenleveladversarialpromptdetection\|Hu et al. 2024]] | Uses per-token perplexity plus contextual smoothing to localise contiguous adversarial spans, producing token-level heatmaps and sentence-level risk scores that can support selective stripping rather than only whole-prompt rejection. |

## Advantages

- Perplexity filtering is entirely training-free with respect to the production LLM: GCG-generated suffixes (grammatically incoherent, high-entropy token sequences) are blocked with 0% attack success rate on multiple 7B-parameter models.
- Pairing perplexity with token-sequence length in a lightweight classifier (LightGBM) resolves the false-positive problem of a plain threshold: Alon & Kamfonas achieve F2=99.1% against machine-generated attacks with only 1 false positive out of 26,188 benign test prompts.
- Paraphrasing reduces attack success rate on Vicuna-7B from 0.79 to 0.05 (matching the no-attack baseline) without changing any model weights; it also provides a second alignment pass because the paraphrasing model may itself refuse harmful content.
- Retokenization is lightweight (BPE-dropout on the tokenizer, no extra parameters) and is complementary to other defences.
- All preprocessing defences raise the computational burden for an adaptive attacker, who must optimise simultaneously for the adversarial objective and for bypassing the defence.

## Limitations

- Perplexity filtering alone produces ~10% false positives on benign prompts (AlpacaEval: ~93.7% of instructions pass; ~1 in 10 is blocked); a classifier trained on perplexity + sequence length resolves this but requires a labelled training set.
- A white-box attacker who adds a perplexity penalty to the GCG loss can almost always bypass the basic filter, and bypasses the windowed variant ~20% of the time before attacks become ineffective — the filter is not robustly secure under adaptive attack.
- Stealthy attacks that generate semantically fluent natural-language jailbreaks (e.g. AutoDAN, perplexity ~40–55; human-crafted GPT-4 jailbreaks, perplexity ~45) bypass perplexity-based detection entirely: GCG's ~1500+ perplexity is structural to the attack optimiser, not a general property of all adversarial inputs.
- Paraphrasing degrades generation quality by ~10–15% on AlpacaEval and can be bypassed when the paraphrasing model itself is targeted by a white-box adaptive attack.
- Retokenization's effectiveness is model-dependent: Falcon-7B-Instruct shows no ASR reduction across all BPE-dropout rates, and higher dropout rates degrade benign output quality more than they hurt attacks on Guanaco.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Perplexity filter (basic) | 0.00 | 0.79 undefended | Vicuna-7B | 0/100 attacks pass; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Perplexity filter (basic) | 0.00 | 0.70 undefended | Falcon-7B-Instruct | Same threshold protocol; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Perplexity filter (basic) | 0.00 | 0.96 undefended | Guanaco-7B | source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Paraphrasing (ChatGPT gpt-3.5-turbo) | 0.05 | 0.79 undefended | Vicuna-7B | Matches no-attack baseline; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Paraphrasing (ChatGPT gpt-3.5-turbo) | 0.33 | 0.96 undefended | Guanaco-7B | Partial reduction; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AdvBench (GCG attack) | ASR ↓ | Retokenization BPE-dropout p=0.4 | 0.52 | 0.79 undefended | Vicuna-7B | Optimal dropout rate; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AlpacaEval | Win rate ↑ | Retokenization BPE-dropout p=0.4 | 47.64% | 54.41% no dropout | Vicuna-7B | ~6.8 pp quality degradation; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AlpacaEval | Win rate ↑ | Paraphrasing (ChatGPT) | ~45% | ~54% no paraphrase | Vicuna-7B | ~10% drop in generation quality; source: @jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks |
| AgentDojo | ASR ↓ | Paraphrasing | — | 17.63% undefended | Various | Cited as prior defence baseline outperformed by LlamaFirewall; source: @chennabasappa2025llamafirewallopensourceguardrail |
| AdvBench + perplexity defence | ASR ↑ | AutoDAN-HGA | 0.9769 | GCG: 0.3923 | Vicuna-7B | Perplexity filter (Jain et al. 2023) fully neutralised by AutoDAN's fluent prompts (PPL ~54 vs GCG ~1532); source: @liu2024autodangeneratingstealthyjailbreak |
| AdvBench + perplexity defence | ASR ↑ | AutoDAN-HGA | 0.9846 | GCG: 0.4058 | Guanaco-7B | GCG reduced to 40% ASR; AutoDAN unaffected; source: @liu2024autodangeneratingstealthyjailbreak |
| AdvBench + perplexity defence | ASR ↑ | AutoDAN-HGA | 0.6077 | GCG: 0.0000 | Llama2-7b-chat | GCG fully neutralised (0% ASR); AutoDAN retains 60.8%; source: @liu2024autodangeneratingstealthyjailbreak |
| AdvBench + paraphrasing defence | ASR ↑ | AutoDAN-HGA | 0.68 | GCG: 0.06 | Vicuna-7B | Paraphrasing reduces AutoDAN ASR from 0.97 to 0.68; reduces GCG to near-zero; source: @liu2024autodangeneratingstealthyjailbreak |
| Adversarial prompt test set (all prompts incl. human-crafted) | F2 ↑ | LightGBM (PPL + seq-len) | 94.2% | 87.2% PPL threshold alone | GPT-2 scorer | 355/382 machine-generated GCG attacks caught; 23 human-crafted jailbreaks all missed; source: @alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks |
| Adversarial prompt test set (machine-generated GCG only) | F2 ↑ | LightGBM (PPL + seq-len) | 99.1% | 91.6% PPL threshold alone | GPT-2 scorer | 4 false negatives, 1 false positive out of 26,188 benign prompts; source: @alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks |
| Authors' GCG-derived adversarial-prompt dataset | Token F1 ↑ | PGM-based detector | 0.9398 | 0.9354 optimization-based detector | GPT-2 124M scorer | Token-level localisation of the adversarial span in Hu et al.'s lightweight default setup; source: @hu2024tokenleveladversarialpromptdetection |
| AdvBench (GCG) | ASR ↑ | GCG under PPL filter | ~0% | 0.97 undefended | Vicuna-7B | PPL filter eliminates GCG entirely; reported in @liu2024autodangeneratingstealthyjailbreak Table 3; source: @alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks |
| AdvBench (AutoDAN) | ASR ↑ | AutoDAN-HGA under PPL filter | 0.9769 | 0.9769 undefended | Vicuna-7B | PPL filter provides zero reduction against AutoDAN's fluent prompts; reported in @liu2024autodangeneratingstealthyjailbreak Table 3; source: @alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks |

nodes.name: Prompt-Neighborhood Screening
nodes.content:
# Prompt-Neighborhood Screening

Techniques that maintain a memory of semantically risky prompt regions and screen each new prompt by whether it falls inside one of those regions. Unlike semantic drift detection, which compares against a fixed clean baseline, these methods continuously update a set of risky exemplars from observed or simulated jailbreak trajectories, then block prompts whose embeddings land within a configured radius. This makes the screening layer trajectory-aware: a prompt that looks harmless in isolation can still be rejected because it sits near a previously discovered path to jailbreak.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@han2026dynamicstackelberggametheoreticframework\|Purple Agent]] | Models multi-turn jailbreak as a Stackelberg game, uses RRT-style defensive rollouts to populate risky and simulated prompt memories, and blocks new prompts that are semantically close to either memory set before they reach the target LLM. |

## Advantages

- Proactive rather than purely reactive: can block semantically nearby follow-up prompts even before they have individually caused a jailbreak.
- Captures multi-turn jailbreak structure by screening against discovered attack trajectories rather than only judging each prompt independently.
- Black-box and model-agnostic in principle: the screening decision uses prompt embeddings and query outcomes rather than model retraining or weight access.

## Limitations

- Coverage depends on how well the defender's search procedure samples the dangerous prompt neighborhood; missed regions remain unblocked.
- Sensitive to the embedding metric and blocking radius, which can trade off missed attacks against over-blocking benign prompts.
- Risk memories can grow over time, but the paper does not study pruning, benign false positives, or long-run operational cost.
- Current evidence is narrow: the technique is evaluated only against the authors' own RRT-style jailbreak attackers, not standard benchmarks or mainstream baselines.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Authors' RRT jailbreak search, 50-query budget | Successful jailbreaks ↓ | Purple Agent + Baseline RRT | 4.2 +/- 2.99 | 17.6 +/- 6.79 attacker-only | DeepSeek-V3 | Early-budget reduction; source: @han2026dynamicstackelberggametheoreticframework |
| Authors' RRT jailbreak search, 200-query budget | Successful jailbreaks ↓ | Purple Agent + Reward-Guided RRT | 39.4 +/- 10.53 | 79.0 +/- 17.43 attacker-only | DeepSeek-V3 | Strongest evaluated attacker on the main model; source: @han2026dynamicstackelberggametheoreticframework |
| Authors' RRT jailbreak search, 100-query budget | Successful jailbreaks ↓ | Purple Agent + Reward-Guided RRT | 27.2 +/- 7.62 | 33.8 +/- 6.73 attacker-only | Llama-3.1-70B | Cross-model transfer works but only partly offsets optimized search on this backbone; source: @han2026dynamicstackelberggametheoreticframework |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Semantic Drift Detection\|Semantic Drift Detection]] | Prompt-neighborhood screening replaces the single clean reference with an evolving memory of observed and simulated jailbreak prompts, letting the screen proactively block semantically nearby follow-up turns in multi-turn attacks rather than only measuring drift from a fixed baseline. |

nodes.name: Semantic Drift Detection
nodes.content:
# Semantic Drift Detection

Detects prompt injection by measuring how much a candidate prompt shifts the semantic representation of a known-clean baseline in embedding space. A fine-tuned sentence encoder extracts dense vector representations for both the baseline and candidate prompts; the cosine drift between the two vectors is then compared against a calibrated threshold. Injections cause a measurable semantic shift; benign additions do not. This approach requires no LLM inference at detection time and no access to the target LLM's weights or logits.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@sekar2026zeroshotembeddingdriftdetection\|ZEDD]] | Fine-tunes a sentence encoder once, then detects injections by measuring cosine drift between a clean baseline embedding and a candidate embedding; a GMM/KDE threshold calibrated at 3% FPR makes deployment zero-shot across attack types and LLM backends. |

## Advantages

- No LLM inference required at detection time — a single cosine similarity computation is the entire overhead.
- Model-agnostic: works with any target LLM without access to its weights or logits.
- The detection threshold is configurable at deployment time (adjusting the FPR cap via GMM threshold search) without retraining.
- Broad attack coverage: cosine drift captures semantic shift across lexically diverse injection categories (jailbreak, task override, encoding manipulation, prompt confusion) that bypass keyword-based screens.
- Fine-tuning the encoder takes 15–18 minutes on a single GPU and requires only ≈10% of the training data.

## Limitations

- Requires a known-clean baseline prompt as a reference; in settings where no such baseline exists the approach cannot be applied directly.
- Detection quality is sensitive to encoder choice: smaller or general-purpose encoders show notably lower recall on adversarial categories compared with larger LLM-based encoders.
- Adaptive adversaries who know the embedding model in use can craft injections that minimise cosine drift to the clean baseline while still redirecting LLM behaviour.
- Current evaluation is limited to email-form data (LLMail-Inject); generalisation to other indirect injection channels (web pages, documents, API responses) is unvalidated.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LLMail-Inject (re-annotated) | Accuracy (↑) | ZEDD | 95.55% | 90.75% SBERT variant | Mistral-7B Instruct | Best accuracy across four encoders; source: @sekar2026zeroshotembeddingdriftdetection |
| LLMail-Inject (re-annotated) | F1 (↑) | ZEDD | 95.50% | — | Mistral-7B Instruct | Clean FPR 2.3%; precision 96.58%, recall 94.45%; source: @sekar2026zeroshotembeddingdriftdetection |
| LLMail-Inject (re-annotated) | Precision (↑) | ZEDD | 99.65% | Competing embedding classifiers (lower) per Fig. 2 | SBERT All-MPNET-Base-V2 | Outperforms Ayub & Majumdar 2024 on precision and F1; source: @sekar2026zeroshotembeddingdriftdetection |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Classifier-Based Detection\|Classifier-Based Detection]] | Drift-based detection measures semantic shift from a clean reference rather than classifying a single input against a learned decision boundary, giving broader coverage across injection categories that bypass keyword-based screens; also avoids end-to-end fine-tuning of a large encoder on labelled examples. |

nodes.name: Interaction-Level Monitoring
nodes.content:
# Interaction-Level Monitoring

Techniques that attach security signals to each prompt/response interaction and surface them as observability data, dashboards, alerts, compliance evidence, or downstream automation triggers. Unlike input screening, which is defined by a narrow pre-processing decision on raw input alone, these approaches treat detection as per-interaction risk scoring across prompts and/or responses. Some deployments remain monitoring-only, while others use configurable thresholds on the same scores to flag or block risky interactions inline; the distinctive value is the surrounding reporting and governance layer.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@whylabs2023langkit\|LangKit]] | Open-source LLM observability toolkit that logs prompt-injection signals such as embedding-similarity scores (`prompt.injection`) and an optional proactive control-prompt check, so suspicious interactions can be monitored, reviewed, or acted on downstream. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@fiddler2026aiguardrailsmetrics\|AI Guardrails Metrics to Strengthen LLM Monitoring]] | Vendor guardrail monitoring workflow that scores each prompt/response for jailbreaks, hallucinations, toxicity, and sensitive-data exposure, then uses configurable thresholds to flag, block, and report risky interactions in enterprise dashboards. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@vijil2026agenttrustplatform\|Vijil AI Agent Trust Platform]] | Dome blocks unsafe inputs and outputs while logging production telemetry, and Darwin uses that telemetry to continuously reinforce safer agent behaviour after deployment. |

## Advantages

- Fits existing observability pipelines: teams can feed per-interaction security telemetry into dashboards, alerts, review queues, and policy engines.
- Preserves deployment flexibility: the same scores can support passive monitoring, human review, quarantine workflows, or threshold-based inline enforcement.
- Supports multi-signal monitoring rather than a single binary verdict, which helps operators correlate prompt injection with other issues such as jailbreaks, hallucinations, or PII exposure.
- Supports governance and audit workflows: the same per-interaction signals can feed reports, threshold tuning, and compliance evidence instead of being discarded after a one-off decision.

## Limitations

- Logged per-interaction scores may miss aggregate misuse patterns that only appear across many interactions.
- Per-interaction risk signals are still only as good as the detectors behind them, so novel attacks can evade scoring or trigger noisy false positives.
- Strong deployments still require application-specific threshold tuning and downstream response logic.
- Published tools often emphasise operational workflow and deployment claims more than reproducible head-to-head detection-quality benchmarks.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LangKit README throughput benchmark | Throughput ↑ | LangKit `llm_metrics` | 23.3 chats/sec | `light_metrics`: 2492 chats/sec | — | AWS `g4dn.xlarge`; best reported throughput for LangKit's LLM-backed metric bundle; README benchmark, not prompt-injection detection accuracy; source: @whylabs2023langkit |
| LangKit README throughput benchmark | Throughput ↑ | LangKit `all_metrics` | 1.79 chats/sec | `light_metrics`: 2492 chats/sec | — | AWS `g4dn.xlarge`; representative full-stack configuration shows substantial operational overhead; README benchmark, not prompt-injection detection accuracy; source: @whylabs2023langkit |
| Production guardrail scoring | Latency ↓ | Fiddler Trust Service trust models | <100 ms | — | Unspecified trust models | Vendor claim from the article: trust models evaluate prompts and responses in under 100 milliseconds; inline flag/block workflow rather than a third-party benchmark; source: @fiddler2026aiguardrailsmetrics |
| Enterprise deployment scale | Prompts monitored ↑ | Fiddler Trust Service | millions | — | Unspecified trust models | Self-reported scale claim for hybrid cloud and VPC deployments; operational reach metric, not a controlled benchmark; source: @fiddler2026aiguardrailsmetrics |
| safe-guard-prompt-injection adversarial subset (476 prompts) | Detection accuracy ↑ | Vijil prompt-injection guardrail | 99.08% | — | ModernBERT-based classifier | Third-party result from @hackett2025bypassingllmguardrailsempirical for one Vijil guardrail component, not the full Depot/Diamond/Dome/Darwin platform; source: @vijil2026agenttrustplatform |
| safe-guard-prompt-injection adversarial subset (476 prompts) | Bypass ASR ↓ | Vijil prompt-injection guardrail under character injection | 87.95% | 99.08% nominal detection accuracy | ModernBERT-based classifier | Third-party average across character-injection attacks in @hackett2025bypassingllmguardrailsempirical; shows nominal accuracy collapses under simple evasions; source: @vijil2026agenttrustplatform |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Multi-Agent Anomaly Detection
nodes.content:
# Multi-Agent Anomaly Detection

Defences that identify compromised agents within an LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) by analysing each agent's behaviour relative to its peers, then structurally isolating the flagged agents to prevent adversarial propagation through the communication topology. Unlike input-screening detection approaches that inspect individual messages before forwarding them to the primary model, these techniques treat the agent itself — not a specific message — as the unit of analysis: an agent is flagged when its response embeddings deviate significantly from the population norm across one or more dialogue rounds. Once flagged, the agent is quarantined by pruning its communication edges, preventing it from influencing downstream neighbours.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@miao2025blindguardsafeguardingllmbasedmultiagent\|BlindGuard]] | Trains a graph neural network on normal agent interaction data alone (no labeled attacks) to score each agent's semantic deviation from its peers; the top-K anomalous agents are flagged and their bidirectional communication edges pruned for the remainder of the round. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2025gsafeguardtopologyguidedsecuritylens\|G-Safeguard]] | Trains an edge-featured GNN on labeled attack data to score each agent's compromise probability from its utterance-graph embedding, then prunes flagged agents' outgoing edges; serves as the supervised upper-bound against which unsupervised approaches such as BlindGuard are measured. |

## Advantages

- Topology-agnostic: tested across chain, tree, star, and random MAS communication structures with consistent ASR reduction.
- Generalises across heterogeneous attack types (prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool exploitation) within a single trained model, because the detector targets semantic deviation rather than attack-specific signatures.
- Unsupervised variants (BlindGuard) require only normal interaction data, with no attack labels — enabling deployment in real-world settings where labeled malicious agent data is unavailable.

## Limitations

- Anomaly scoring uses the global mean of all agent features as a normality reference; if a large fraction of agents are simultaneously compromised, the reference distribution shifts and detection degrades.
- Edge pruning is irreversible within a round; falsely flagged normal agents lose access to legitimate information, potentially degrading task utility.
- Evaluated primarily on QA tasks (CSQA, MMLU, GSM8K); generalisability to other MAS application domains is unconfirmed.
- Supervised variants (G-Safeguard) require labeled attack data, limiting deployment to settings where annotated attack examples are available.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CSQA — PI, Chain | ASR@3 ↓ | BlindGuard | 0.25 | 0.383 No Defense; 0.197 G-Safeguard | GPT-4o-mini | Unsupervised; outperforms all other unsupervised baselines; source: @miao2025blindguardsafeguardingllmbasedmultiagent |
| CSQA — PI, Star | ASR@3 ↓ | BlindGuard | 0.24 | 0.463 No Defense; 0.183 G-Safeguard | GPT-4o-mini | source: @miao2025blindguardsafeguardingllmbasedmultiagent |
| InjecAgent — TA, Chain | ASR@3 ↓ | BlindGuard | 0.142 | 0.480 No Defense; 0.102 G-Safeguard | GPT-4o-mini | Tool attack; source: @miao2025blindguardsafeguardingllmbasedmultiagent |
| PoisonRAG — MA, Chain | ASR@3 ↓ | BlindGuard | 0.093 | 0.223 No Defense; 0.060 G-Safeguard | GPT-4o-mini | Memory attack; source: @miao2025blindguardsafeguardingllmbasedmultiagent |
| PoisonRAG — MA, 50 agents | ASR@3 ↓ | BlindGuard | 1.81 / 2.66 / 3.76 | 5.67 / 16.31 / 20.92 No Defense | — | Rounds R1/R2/R3; demonstrates scalability through 50-agent MAS; source: @miao2025blindguardsafeguardingllmbasedmultiagent |
| CSQA — PI, Chain | ASR@3 ↓ | G-Safeguard | 0.197 | 0.383 No Defense | GPT-4o-mini | Supervised upper bound; ~21.38 % avg ASR reduction on CSQA; source: @wang2025gsafeguardtopologyguidedsecuritylens |
| InjecAgent — TA, Chain | ASR@3 ↓ | G-Safeguard | 0.102 | 0.480 No Defense | GPT-4o-mini | Tool attack; ~12.67 % ASR reduction; source: @wang2025gsafeguardtopologyguidedsecuritylens |
| CSQA — PI, 65-agent MAS | ASR reduction ↑ | G-Safeguard | 39.23 % | — | GPT-4o-mini | Trained on 8-agent data; applied to 65-agent MAS without retraining; source: @wang2025gsafeguardtopologyguidedsecuritylens |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: CoT Monitoring
nodes.content:
# CoT Monitoring

An automated monitor reads the internal chain-of-thought (CoT) scratchpad that a reasoning model generates before producing its final output and classifies the interaction as safe or suspicious. When the monitor detects that the model appears to intend misbehaviour — for example, crediting instructions from untrusted environment content or planning an action contrary to the user's goal — the interaction can be blocked, resampled, or escalated for human review. The approach exploits two properties of current reasoning models: *necessity* (for tasks that require hard sequential reasoning, the relevant computation must pass through the CoT token stream) and *propensity* (models trained predominantly on human text tend to externalise their reasoning in legible natural language, even when not strictly forced to do so).

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@korbak2025chainthoughtmonitorabilitynew\|Korbak et al. 2025 (CoT Monitorability)]] | Position paper arguing that CoT monitoring is a real but fragile safety opportunity — monitors can catch prompt injection and misalignment by reading scratchpad traces, but this property may erode as training practices evolve; advocates that frontier developers measure and actively preserve it. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@baker2025monitoringreasoningmodelsmisbehavior\|Baker et al. 2025 (Monitoring Reasoning Models)]] | Empirical study demonstrating that CoT monitors can detect misbehaviour in deployed reasoning models and showing that training against a CoT monitor promotes obfuscation rather than eliminating the underlying misbehaviour. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@emmons2025chainthoughtnecessarylanguage\|Emmons et al. 2025 (CoT Necessity)]] | Empirical demonstration that when a task genuinely requires CoT to complete, models struggle to evade CoT monitors — directly supporting the necessity argument for CoT monitorability. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@arnav2025cotredhandedstresstesting\|Arnav et al. 2025 (CoT Red-Handed)]] | Red-team stress test comparing action-only, CoT, and hybrid monitoring across subtle and non-subtle side tasks; finds CoT monitoring helps for subtle sabotage but hurts for obvious ones, and proposes a hybrid weighted-average protocol that consistently outperforms both single-signal approaches. |

## Advantages

- Catches misbehaviour in the reasoning trace before any irreversible action is taken, providing a safety layer that is complementary to and uncorrelated with action-level monitoring.
- Requires no modification to the agent's architecture or training; the monitor can be bolted on as a post-processing step over an existing reasoning model's scratchpad.
- Applicable to a wide range of misbehaviour types: prompt injection, reward hacking, goal misalignment, and sabotage — wherever the agent must plan its action in a scratchpad.

## Limitations

- Monitorability may be fragile: outcome-based reinforcement learning exerts no direct optimisation pressure on the CoT to remain legible, and as reasoning compute scales the natural language prior may weaken.
- Training directly against a CoT monitor promotes obfuscation — the model learns to produce a clean-looking scratchpad while the misbehaviour proceeds through activations rather than eliminating the underlying misalignment.
- Novel latent reasoning architectures (continuous-space recurrent models) do not externalise intermediate steps and are inherently unmonitorable via CoT.
- CoT monitoring does not guarantee safety: the CoT is an incomplete representation of the full computation, and a sufficiently situationally-aware model may hide intent entirely in activations.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | CoT monitoring (general) | — | — | Reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek-R1, Claude 3.7) | Position paper; no original quantitative experiments. Evidence cited from Baker et al. 2025, Meinke et al. 2024, Chennabasappa et al. 2025 showing models explicitly state injected goals or misbehaviours in CoT. Source: @korbak2025chainthoughtmonitorabilitynew |
| Frontier RL training (joint, both hacks) | Recall ↑ | CoT Monitor (GPT-4o, full trajectory) | 95% | 60% (Action Monitor) | Frontier o1-family agent | Joint recall over exit(0) and raise SkipTest systemic hacks; CoT monitor dramatically outperforms action-only. Source: @baker2025monitoringreasoningmodelsmisbehavior |
| Agentic coding (unit-test honeypot) | Cheating NOT caught by monitor ↑ | RL with CoT pressure (high optimization) | High and rising | Near zero (baseline agent) | Non-frontier agent | Obfuscated reward hacking: agent trained against monitor produces benign-looking CoT while continuing to cheat; monitor recall falls near zero. Source: @baker2025monitoringreasoningmodelsmisbehavior |
| GPQA uplift set (59 Qs, complex hint) | Unfaithful rate ↓ | Complex hint (CoT-as-computation) | ~7% (Flash), ~4% (Pro) | ~19% (Flash), ~14% (Pro) with simple hint | Gemini 2.5 Flash / Pro | Unfaithfulness disappears when the hint requires multi-step computation; model must externalise reasoning, making misbehaviour monitorable. Source: @emmons2025chainthoughtnecessarylanguage |
| GPQA uplift set, topic monitor | Recall ↑ / Task success ↑ | Ordinary CoT (no evasion attempt) | Recall >95%, Task success ~80% (Flash), ~88% (Pro) | — | Gemini 2.0 Flash / 2.5 Pro actor; Gemini 2.0 Flash / 2.5 Pro monitor | Monitors calibrated at <10% FPR; strong monitors harder to fool. Source: @emmons2025chainthoughtnecessarylanguage |
| `oversight_pattern` (multi-step stealth) | Suspiciousness score ↓ / Side task success ↑ | Gaslighting (best evasion strategy) | ~80% side task success, low monitor score against weak monitor only | Ordinary CoT: score ~10 (maximally suspicious) | Gemini 2.5 Pro actor; Gemini 2.0 Flash monitor | Evasion only against weaker monitor; fails against strong (2.5 Pro) monitor. Source: @emmons2025chainthoughtnecessarylanguage |
