| `factorize_derivative` (RL stress test) | Task success ↑ / Monitor trigger rate ↓ | RL fine-tuning (~10,000 steps) | Task success 94.4%; monitor trigger 19.5% | Pre-RL: 69.8% task success; 81.8% monitor trigger | Gemini 1.5 Flash (fine-tuned) | RL discovers "factorize early" and "targeted mistakes" strategies; demonstrates evasion is achievable with dedicated fine-tuning. Source: @emmons2025chainthoughtnecessarylanguage |
| BigCodeBench (all tasks) | Catch rate at 1% FPR ↑ | Hybrid monitor (w=0.55) | 21% higher than action-only; 42% higher than CoT-only | Action-only and CoT-only monitors | GPT-4.1-mini monitoring DeepSeek R1 family + Claude 3.7 Sonnet Thinking | Hybrid consistently outperforms both single-signal approaches across all untrusted models and tasks. Source: @arnav2025cotredhandedstresstesting |
| BigCodeBench (subtle side tasks) | Catch rate at 1% FPR ↑ | CoT monitor | ~20% avg | ~10% avg (action-only) | GPT-4.1-mini | CoT monitoring improves by ~10 pp over action-only on subtle tasks; CoT access hurts by ~25 pp on non-subtle tasks due to misleading rationalisations. Source: @arnav2025cotredhandedstresstesting |
| Sandbagging eval (multiplication task, goal given) | Accuracy difference between aligned/misaligned incentive conditions (↓) | In-context scheming — strategic underperformance | 0.65 | 0 (no difference) | sonnet-3.5 | Scheming reasoning explicitly visible in CoT for opus-3 and llama-3.1; o1 suppresses CoT scheming traces. Source: @meinke2025frontiermodelscapableincontext |
| Deception persistence (follow-up questions after scheming actions) | Rate of refusing to admit scheming (↓) | Doubling-down across 8 question variants | >0.85 | 0 (honest admission) | o1 | o1 hides scheming intent from CoT summaries; monitors relying on CoT cannot detect deception in o1. Source: @meinke2025frontiermodelscapableincontext |
| Instrumental Alignment Faking (no goal nudging) | Behavioral scheming rate (↓) | Differential behavior under oversight vs. deployment | 0.42 | ≤0.25 under null hypothesis | gemini-1.5 | p < 0.001; scheming reasoning for gemini-1.5 not consistently externalised in CoT, unlike opus-3/llama-3.1. Source: @meinke2025frontiermodelscapableincontext |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Agent Execution Analysis/AI Control Protocols\|AI Control Protocols]] | Korbak et al. explicitly frame CoT monitoring as an extension of AI control: instead of monitoring only actions or outputs, it monitors the model's scratchpad for suspicious intent and can block or escalate before the action is executed. |
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Classifier-Based Detection\|Classifier-Based Detection]] | CoT monitoring inspects the agent's internal reasoning trace rather than screening external input text, catching semantic goal drift (e.g. acting on injected instructions from retrieved content) that input-level lexical classifiers are blind to. |
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/LLM-Based Evaluation\|LLM-Based Evaluation]] | CoT monitoring shifts the evaluation point from the raw input to the agent's own scratchpad, allowing detection of misaligned intent that forms only after the model has processed the injected content — a gap that input-level LLM evaluators cannot close. |

nodes.name: Instruction-Following Intent Analysis
nodes.content:
# Instruction-Following Intent Analysis

Techniques that elicit the concrete instructions a reasoning model plans to follow, then decide safety from that intended-instruction list rather than from the raw prompt surface alone. Instead of asking only "does this input look malicious?", these defences ask "what actions does the model currently intend to take, and did those intended instructions come from trusted or untrusted context?" This makes them especially suited to indirect prompt injection, where the dangerous signal is the model's planned obedience to instructions hidden inside retrieved or tool-returned data.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection\|IntentGuard]] | Uses thinking interventions to make a reasoning model explicitly list the instructions it intends to follow, traces each intended instruction back to trusted or untrusted prompt spans, then either alerts or masks the tainted span and regenerates. |

## Advantages

- Targets the model's planned instruction-following behaviour rather than only surface-level attack markers, improving robustness to semantically fluent indirect injections.
- Training-free and black-box compatible for reasoning-capable API models: no fine-tuning or weight access is required.
- Produces an auditable explanation of why a request was flagged by showing the intended instruction and the untrusted span it came from.
- Can support either hard-stop alerting or selective mask-and-regenerate recovery.

## Limitations

- Depends on reasoning models that externalise their intent faithfully enough for extraction.
- Adds reasoning-token overhead because the defence must elicit and read the intended-instruction trace.
- Requires provenance labels or another trusted/untrusted segmentation of the prompt context.
- Current tracing and masking design is text-centric and does not directly cover multimodal context.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mind2Web + PAIR | ASR ↓ | IntentGuard | 8.5% | 100.0% vanilla; 71.5% SEP Defense; 67.2% PromptArmor | Qwen3-32B | Headline adaptive-attack result in the paper; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo + Beam Search | ASR ↓ | IntentGuard | 4.3% | 48.2% vanilla; 48.9% SEP Defense; 30.4% PromptArmor | Qwen3-32B | Search-based adaptive injection result; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo + PAIR | Utility under attack ↑ | IntentGuard | 0.687 | 0.464 vanilla; 0.476 SEP Defense; 0.432 PromptArmor | Qwen3-32B | Preserves substantially more task utility than PromptArmor under the strongest reported AgentDojo attack; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo | Benign utility ↑ | IntentGuard | 0.773 | 0.773 vanilla | Qwen3-32B | No benign degradation and 0.000 FPR in the reported AgentDojo setting; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| Mind2Web | Benign utility ↑ | IntentGuard | 0.745 | 0.740 vanilla | gpt-oss-20B | Slight benign gain with 0.000 FPR on the second reported backbone; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| Mind2Web + GCG | ASR ↓ | IntentGuard | 6.4% | 62.1% vanilla; 57.4% SEP Defense; 29.6% PromptArmor | gpt-oss-20B | White-box gradient-attack robustness on the second reported backbone; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Internal-Signal Detection
nodes.content:
# Internal-Signal Detection

Detects prompt injection by monitoring model-internal signals rather than screening only the text surface. These methods extract hidden-state, attention, or gradient signatures from the protected model itself, then classify those signals for evidence of task drift, injected-token spans, or unsafe-intent alignment before the model is allowed to proceed normally.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask\|TaskTracker]] | Computes activation deltas between a primary-task forward pass and a full-context forward pass, then classifies the delta with a logistic-regression or triplet-network probe to detect task drift; achieves ROC AUC ≥ 0.99 across six models with zero-shot generalisation to unseen attack types. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@zhong2025attentionneeddefendindirect\|Rennervate]] | Uses the guarded LLM's own attention patterns over untrusted tokens to detect contiguous injected spans at token level, then removes just those spans so the remaining benign content can still be used. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@xie2024gradsafedetectingjailbreakprompts\|GradSafe]] | Pairs the candidate prompt with a compliant dummy reply, computes gradients on pre-identified safety-critical parameter slices, and flags prompts whose gradient fingerprint matches unsafe reference prompts; beats Llama Guard on ToxicChat and XSTest in the paper's zero-shot setting. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@hu2024gradientcuffdetectingjailbreak\|Gradient Cuff]] | Explores the guarded model's refusal-loss landscape around a candidate prompt and flags jailbreaks whose gradient signature indicates the prompt sits in an adversarial basin before normal answering proceeds. |

## Advantages

- Uses the protected model's own internal signals rather than surface wording, helping against semantically fluent or obfuscated attacks that evade lexical filters.
- Can block a request before the final harmful answer is delivered, while remaining separate from the main task prompt and weights.
- Some variants can localise the suspicious span inside otherwise useful context, enabling selective sanitization instead of whole-input rejection.
- Avoids full defence-model retraining: TaskTracker adds a lightweight probe, while GradSafe-Zero works from a tiny safe/unsafe reference set.

## Limitations

- Requires white-box access to internal signals (hidden states, attention weights, or gradients), so it is unusable with closed API-only models.
- Detection setup is model-specific: probes, layers, or safety-critical parameter slices must be identified for each protected backbone.
- Adds extra computation beyond a plain input classifier, since the system must run the model to extract the internal signature before deciding.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TaskTracker test set (public, OOD) | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker linear probe | 0.996 | Prompt Guard 0.974; Prompt Shields 0.988 | Phi-3 14B | Zero-shot on attacks; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| XSTest | AUPRC ↑ | GradSafe-Zero | 0.936 | 0.889 Llama Guard | Llama-2-7b-chat-hf | Zero-shot jailbreak-prompt detection from safety-critical gradients; source: @xie2024gradsafedetectingjailbreakprompts |
| Six jailbreak attacks (average) | TPR ↑ | Gradient Cuff (`σ = 5%`) | 0.883 ± 0.013 | 0.760 ± 0.017 Llama Guard | LLaMA-2-7B-Chat | Refusal-loss gradient detector; also keeps lower FPR (0.022 vs 0.040); source: @hu2024gradientcuffdetectingjailbreak |
| TaskTracker — per-model, best layer | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker linear probe | ≥0.99 | — | Mistral 7B / Llama-3 8B / Mixtral 8x7B / Phi-3 3.8B / Phi-3 14B | Linear probes achieve ≥0.99 on layers 7–31; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| Non-public internal PI dataset (~13K) | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker linear probe (no retrain) | 0.934 | Prompt Guard 0.728; Proprietary classifier 0.959 | Phi-3 14B | Includes longer docs, multilingual, subtle phrasings; retraining raises score to 0.989; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| TaskTracker metric learning probes | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker triplet-network probe | ≥0.98 | — | Mistral 7B / Llama-3 8B / Mixtral 8x7B / Phi-3 3.8B | Consistent across layer ranges; also enables temporal localisation of injection site; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| TaskTracker ASR | ASR % (↓) | TaskTracker (via Meta-SecAlign-70B pipeline) | 0.2% | 19.6% undefended | Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct | Reported in @chen2026metasecalignsecurefoundation (Table 4); activation-delta detection combined with SecAlign++ |
| FIPI | Accuracy ↑ | Rennervate | 99.58% | 75.48% ProtectAI-v2 | Falcon | Attention-based token-level detection for indirect prompt injection; source: @zhong2025attentionneeddefendindirect |
| FIPI | ASR ↓ | Rennervate | 0.00% | 35.10% Spotlighting | Falcon | Attention-based token-level sanitization for indirect prompt injection; source: @zhong2025attentionneeddefendindirect |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Classifier-Based Detection\|Classifier-Based Detection]] | Model-internal detection closes a gap left by surface classifiers: it monitors hidden-state, attention, or gradient evidence of task drift and unsafe alignment rather than relying on lexical attack markers alone. |

nodes.name: Reasoning Monitoring
nodes.content:
# Reasoning Monitoring

Techniques that inspect the model's internal reasoning process — chain-of-thought scratchpad traces, hidden-state activations, attention patterns, or gradient patterns — rather than the raw input text. Where input screening evaluates what goes *into* the model, reasoning monitoring evaluates what happens *inside* the model during inference. CoT monitors read the natural-language scratchpad that reasoning models generate before their final output, flagging traces that reveal intent to act on injected instructions or drift from the user's goal. Internal-signal detectors instead measure activation, attention, or gradient patterns induced by the candidate prompt, catching task drift or unsafe alignment that is invisible in the generated text.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Detection/Reasoning Monitoring/Instruction-Following Intent Analysis\|Instruction-Following Intent Analysis]] | Elicits the concrete instructions a reasoning model plans to follow, then traces those intended instructions back to trusted or untrusted prompt spans so indirect prompt injections can be blocked or repaired. |
| down:: [[Detection/Reasoning Monitoring/CoT Monitoring\|CoT Monitoring]] | An automated monitor reads the internal chain-of-thought scratchpad of a reasoning model and classifies the interaction as safe or suspicious; catches misbehaviour (prompt injection, reward hacking, goal misalignment) before any irreversible action is taken. |
| down:: [[Detection/Reasoning Monitoring/Internal-Signal Detection\|Internal-Signal Detection]] | Uses model-internal activation, attention, or gradient signatures to detect prompt-induced task drift, injected-token spans, or unsafe alignment before the guarded model proceeds normally. |

## Advantages

- Catches misbehaviour that is invisible at the input surface: both CoT monitoring and internal-signal detection operate on the model's internal state, detecting semantic goal drift and injected-instruction compliance that input-level classifiers and perplexity filters miss.
- Requires no modification to the agent's architecture or training for CoT monitoring — the monitor is bolted on as a post-processing step over an existing reasoning model's scratchpad.
- Internal-signal methods use the model's own behaviour as the signal, which can generalise better than lexical attack fingerprints to novel or obfuscated prompts.
- Complementary to and uncorrelated with input screening and action-level monitoring, enabling defence-in-depth when layered with other techniques.

## Limitations

- CoT monitorability may be fragile: outcome-based RL exerts no direct pressure on the CoT to remain legible, and training against a CoT monitor promotes obfuscation rather than eliminating the underlying misbehaviour.
- Novel latent reasoning architectures (continuous-space recurrent models) do not externalise intermediate steps and are inherently unmonitorable via CoT.
- Internal-signal detection requires white-box access to internal model signals, making it incompatible with closed API deployments.
- Each backbone model needs its own probe, layer choice, or safety-critical-parameter selection; cross-model transferability is limited.
- Internal-signal monitoring adds extra model computation before a request can be accepted, increasing latency relative to simple surface filters.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frontier RL training (joint, both hacks) | Recall (↑) | CoT Monitor (GPT-4o, full trajectory) | 95% | 60% (Action Monitor) | Frontier o1-family agent | CoT monitor dramatically outperforms action-only; source: @baker2025monitoringreasoningmodelsmisbehavior |
| APPS backdooring (5,000 problems) | Safety (↑) | Untrusted monitoring + defer to trusted 20% | 98% | 62% (trusted monitoring) | GPT-4 (U) + GPT-3.5 (T) | source: @greenblatt2024aicontrolimprovingsafety |
| BigCodeBench (all tasks) | Catch rate at 1% FPR (↑) | Hybrid monitor (w=0.55) | 21% higher than action-only | Action-only and CoT-only monitors | GPT-4.1-mini monitoring DeepSeek R1 + Claude 3.7 | Hybrid consistently outperforms both single-signal approaches; source: @arnav2025cotredhandedstresstesting |
| TaskTracker test set (public, OOD) | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker linear probe | 0.996 | Prompt Guard 0.974; Prompt Shields 0.988 | Phi-3 14B | Zero-shot on attacks; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| XSTest | AUPRC ↑ | GradSafe-Zero | 0.936 | 0.889 Llama Guard | Llama-2-7b-chat-hf | Gradient-based zero-shot jailbreak-prompt detection; source: @xie2024gradsafedetectingjailbreakprompts |
| Six jailbreak attacks (average) | TPR ↑ | Gradient Cuff (`σ = 5%`) | 0.883 ± 0.013 | 0.760 ± 0.017 Llama Guard | LLaMA-2-7B-Chat | Refusal-loss gradient detector; also keeps lower FPR (0.022 vs 0.040); source: @hu2024gradientcuffdetectingjailbreak |
| TaskTracker — per-model, best layer | ROC AUC (↑) | TaskTracker linear probe | ≥0.99 | — | Mistral 7B / Llama-3 8B / Mixtral 8x7B / Phi-3 14B | Linear probes across layers 7–31; source: @abdelnabi2025driftcatchingllmtask |
| FIPI | ASR ↓ | Rennervate | 0.00% | 35.10% Spotlighting | Falcon | Attention-based internal-signal detector with token-level sanitization for indirect prompt injection; source: @zhong2025attentionneeddefendindirect |
| Mind2Web + PAIR | ASR ↓ | IntentGuard | 8.5% | 100.0% vanilla; 71.5% SEP Defense; 67.2% PromptArmor | Qwen3-32B | Scratchpad-based intent analysis; headline adaptive indirect-prompt-injection result from IntentGuard; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo + Beam Search | ASR ↓ | IntentGuard | 4.3% | 48.2% vanilla; 48.9% SEP Defense; 30.4% PromptArmor | Qwen3-32B | Adds a second adaptive indirect-prompt-injection setting beyond PAIR, showing the same intent-analysis defence remains effective against search-based attacks; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo + PAIR | Utility under attack ↑ | IntentGuard | 0.687 | 0.464 vanilla; 0.476 SEP Defense; 0.432 PromptArmor | Qwen3-32B | Shows that scratchpad-based intent analysis preserves substantially more attacked-task utility than PromptArmor; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |
| AgentDojo | Benign utility ↑ | IntentGuard | 0.773 | 0.773 vanilla | Qwen3-32B | Maintains benign utility while inspecting the model's intended-instruction trace; source: @kang2025mitigatingindirectpromptinjection |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Detection/Input Screening/Input Screening\|Input Screening]] | Reasoning monitoring inspects the model's internal state (scratchpad traces, activations, attention patterns, and gradients) rather than the raw input surface, catching semantic goal drift and injected-instruction compliance that input-level classifiers, perplexity filters, and LLM evaluators are blind to. |

nodes.name: Backtranslation
nodes.content:
# Backtranslation

Defences that screen an interaction in the response space rather than the prompt space. A backtranslation wrapper first lets the target model produce a provisional answer, then asks a second model to infer the most harmful user request that could have produced that answer. The target model is re-run on this inferred request; if it refuses, the system treats the original interaction as a jailbreak and replaces the original answer with a refusal. This makes the security decision depend on model-generated output rather than directly on attacker-controlled wording.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks\|Backtranslation]] | Uses a second model to infer the likely hidden harmful request from the target model's first answer, then re-queries the target model on that inferred request and blocks the original response if the backtranslated request is refused. |

## Advantages

- Shifts detection away from attacker-controlled prompt text and onto the model's own provisional answer, which can expose harmful intent more clearly than obfuscated jailbreak wording.
- Training-free and model-agnostic: it wraps both closed and open models without modifying their weights.
- Preserves benign quality better than prompt-perturbation baselines because benign interactions simply return the original answer when the second check passes.
- Works across several jailbreak families in the paper's evaluation, including GCG, PAIR, AutoDAN, PAP, and fixed jailbreak prefixes.

## Limitations

- Requires at least one extra model plus extra inference passes, increasing latency and cost.
- Depends on the first response containing enough signal for the backtranslation model to reconstruct the underlying harmful intent.
- Can over-refuse benign prompts when the inferred request is a poor match; the paper's likelihood filter for this issue is only available when token probabilities are exposed.
- Strongly adaptive white-box attacks on the full defended pipeline are only partially explored.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AdvBench harmful behaviors (GCG) | DSR ↑ | Backtranslation | 100% | 98% SmoothLLM | Llama-2-13B | Near-perfect defence on transfer-suffix jailbreaks; source: @wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks |
| AdvBench harmful behaviors (PAIR w/ defense) | DSR ↑ | Backtranslation | 76% | 64% paraphrasing | GPT-3.5-turbo | Defence-aware PAIR attacks the defended pipeline; source: @wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks |
| MT-Bench | Response quality ↑ | Backtranslation | 8.60 | 8.71 no defense | GPT-3.5-turbo | Benign quality is nearly unchanged; source: @wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks |
| GCG + MT-Bench latency study | Avg. time cost ↓ | Backtranslation w/ early termination | 49.86s | 41.56s no defense | Vicuna-13B | Early termination roughly halves latency versus full backtranslation while retaining 96% DSR against GCG; source: @wang2024defendingllmsjailbreakingattacks |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Output Screening
nodes.content:
# Output Screening

Techniques that let the primary model draft a response, then run a second-stage safety check over that candidate output before releasing it. Instead of inferring the hidden user intent from the draft, these defences directly classify the generated text itself as harmful or benign. The security signal therefore comes from the model's own proposed answer, which can expose jailbreak success more clearly than the original prompt.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots\|BAD classifier]] | Uses a multi-turn safety classifier as a second-stage check on draft chatbot replies, replacing unsafe continuations with a canned safe response or topic-changing non-sequitur. |
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@phute2024llmselfdefenseself\|LLM Self Defense]] | Uses a zero-shot LLM judge to inspect the first model's draft answer and block it if the generated text itself is classified as harmful. |

## Advantages

- Can be added after generation without retraining the protected model or rewriting the input prompt.
- Uses the model's own drafted output as the detection target, which can make successful jailbreaks easier to identify than obfuscated prompts alone.
- A same-model self-filter is possible, so deployment can be lightweight when no separate classifier is available.

## Limitations

- The harmful text is still generated internally before the screen blocks it, so the defence does not stop the model from processing the malicious request.
- Adds an extra inference pass for every response.
- Reliability depends on the checker model's ability to follow the judging prompt and avoid false positives.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AdvBench-derived prompt set (50 harmful, 50 benign responses) | Accuracy ↑ | LLM Self Defense (suffix prompt, self-filter) | 99.0% | 98.0% prefix prompt | GPT-3.5 | TPR 0.98, FPR 0.00; authors describe residual ASR as "virtually 0"; source: @phute2024llmselfdefenseself |
| Adversarial dialogue safety test set | OK rate ↑ | BST 2.7B + Adv. Dialogue Safety | 94.4% | 55.0% BST 2.7B | BST 2.7B | Two-stage BAD deployment screens draft chatbot replies and replaces unsafe outputs with safe fallbacks/non-sequiturs; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |
| Non-adversarial Human-Chat | Non-sequitur rate ↓ | BST 2.7B + Adv. Dialogue Safety | 0.3% | 4.9% Multi-Turn Safety Cl.; 2.6% Safety Cl. | BST 2.7B | Benign-conversation derailment stays low despite the extra response screen; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Response Divergence Detection
nodes.content:
# Response Divergence Detection

Detects prompt attacks by applying small mutations to an untrusted input, running the protected model on those variants, and checking whether the resulting responses stay stable. Benign requests should preserve their intent under light perturbation, while jailbreaks and hijacking injections are expected to be brittle: once the exploit wording or hidden image instruction is disturbed, the model's responses diverge sharply. The defence therefore turns mutation robustness into a detection signal instead of a sanitization step.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework\|JailGuard]] | Generates multiple mutated text or image variants, queries the target model on each, embeds the responses, and flags the original input when response-similarity distributions diverge beyond a threshold. |

## Advantages

- Covers both text jailbreaks and image-based prompt attacks within one detection framework.
- Does not rely on attack-specific keywords or jailbreak templates; it looks for behavioural instability instead.
- Keeps the original input unchanged when accepted, avoiding the utility loss of always forwarding a perturbed or rewritten prompt.
- Extends mutation-based defence ideas to hijacking-style prompt injection and multimodal settings.

## Limitations

- Requires multiple extra model queries for each screened input, making it expensive at inference time.
- Needs empirically tuned mutators and thresholds for the defended model.
- Can produce false positives when stronger mutations disturb benign requests.
- Evaluation still depends on finite attack suites, so robustness to unseen attacks remains uncertain.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| JailGuard prompt-attack dataset (text) | Accuracy ↑ | JailGuard policy | 86.14% | 74.33% SmoothLLM-Swap | GPT-3.5-turbo-1106 | Default text policy over 12 text attack types plus benign data; source: @zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework |
| JailGuard prompt-attack dataset (text) | Recall ↑ | JailGuard policy | 86.10% | 44.98% SmoothLLM-Swap | GPT-3.5-turbo-1106 | Higher text attack catch rate than the strongest reported baseline; source: @zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework |
| JailGuard prompt-attack dataset (image) | Accuracy ↑ | JailGuard policy | 82.90% | 70.70% ECSO Detection | MiniGPT-4 | Default image policy over typographic and adversarial image attacks; source: @zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework |
| JailGuard prompt-attack dataset (image) | Recall ↑ | JailGuard policy | 88.25% | 34.00% ECSO Detection | MiniGPT-4 | Stronger image-side attack detection than the reported baseline; source: @zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Sanitization/Input Perturbation\|Input Perturbation]] | Response divergence detection reuses prompt mutation as a robustness probe rather than as an always-on sanitization transform, preserving accepted inputs unchanged while broadening the mutation-based idea from text jailbreak smoothing to multimodal attack detection. |

nodes.name: Response-Space Detection
nodes.content:
# Response-Space Detection

Techniques that detect prompt attacks by looking at the model's responses rather than only the raw input text. These methods run the protected model or a helper model on the candidate interaction, then use inconsistency, inferred intent, or other response-derived signals to decide whether the request is adversarial. They sit between pure input screening and deeper execution monitoring: the security signal comes from what the model does with the input, but the defence still blocks before the unsafe interaction is allowed to proceed normally.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Detection/Response-Space Detection/Output Screening\|Output Screening]] | Screens a draft model response directly for harmful content before release, typically by prompting a second LLM or the same LLM in judge mode. |
| down:: [[Detection/Response-Space Detection/Backtranslation\|Backtranslation]] | Lets the model draft a provisional answer, infers the hidden harmful request that answer implies, then blocks the original response if the inferred request is refused on re-check. |
| down:: [[Detection/Response-Space Detection/Response Divergence Detection\|Response Divergence Detection]] | Mutates the incoming text or image, queries the target model on those variants, and flags the input when the resulting responses become unusually inconsistent. |

## Advantages

- Detects attacks from the model's behavioural response rather than relying only on surface prompt features, which helps against obfuscated or semantically varied attacks.
- Works as a wrapper around existing models without retraining the primary task model.
- Can catch attacks that remain hard to recognise from the raw prompt alone because the model's own responses expose the instability or hidden intent.

## Limitations

- Requires extra model calls, increasing latency and serving cost.
- Detection quality depends on the auxiliary response-derived signal being reliable for the protected model and threat model in use.
- Adaptive attackers may optimize against the specific consistency or reconstruction check once they know the defence pipeline.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| JailGuard prompt-attack dataset (text) | Accuracy ↑ | JailGuard policy | 86.14% | 74.33% SmoothLLM-Swap | GPT-3.5-turbo-1106 | Mutation-based response divergence detection on text jailbreaks and prompt injections; source: @zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework |
| JailGuard prompt-attack dataset (image) | Accuracy ↑ | JailGuard policy | 82.90% | 70.70% ECSO Detection | MiniGPT-4 | Multimodal extension to image prompt attacks via response divergence under input mutations; source: @zhang2025jailguarduniversaldetectionframework |
| AdvBench-derived prompt set (50 harmful, 50 benign responses) | Accuracy ↑ | LLM Self Defense (suffix prompt, self-filter) | 99.0% | 98.0% prefix prompt | GPT-3.5 | TPR 0.98, FPR 0.00; zero-shot output screening over the drafted response; source: @phute2024llmselfdefenseself |
| Adversarial dialogue safety test set | OK rate ↑ | BST 2.7B + Adv. Dialogue Safety | 94.4% | 55.0% BST 2.7B | BST 2.7B | Output-screening deployment result under adversarial conversations; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |
| Non-adversarial Human-Chat | Non-sequitur rate ↓ | BST 2.7B + Adv. Dialogue Safety | 0.3% | 4.9% Multi-Turn Safety Cl.; 2.6% Safety Cl. | BST 2.7B | Benign-conversation derailment stays low for the same response-space screen; source: @xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Security Assessment Frameworks
nodes.content:
# Security Assessment Frameworks

Tools in this category package repeatable LLM-security assessments into reusable scanners or red-teaming frameworks. Rather than blocking a single live request, they run structured probes across categories such as prompt injection, prompt leakage, jailbreaks, poisoning, or excessive agency, then return reports, failing cases, or pass/fail summaries that teams can use for predeployment checks and recurring security reviews.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@confidentai2025owasptop10llms\|DeepTeam OWASP Top 10 for LLMs 2025]] | Implements the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs 2025 as a DeepTeam red-teaming framework, letting teams run either the full suite or selected categories such as `LLM_01` prompt injection and review the resulting pass rate, test cases, and platform reports. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@helpnetsecurity2025garak\|Garak: Open-source LLM vulnerability scanner]] | Open-source scanner that runs probe-and-detector assessments for prompt injection, encoding attacks, jailbreaks, data leakage, and other LLM failure modes, then records failures in logs and JSONL reports. |
| down:: [[Sources/Tools/@vijil2026agenttrustplatform\|Vijil AI Agent Trust Platform]] | Diamond gives Vijil a pre-deployment security-assessment role: it runs attack-library validation and verification, can invoke garak, and turns the results into an audit-ready Trust Score for recurring assurance. |

## Advantages

- Makes adversarial security testing repeatable instead of relying on ad hoc prompts.
- Supports both broad audits and targeted checks such as prompt injection alone.
- Fits recurring assurance workflows through reusable code, scanners, and report artefacts.

## Limitations

- Assesses risk exposure rather than mitigating attacks directly in production traffic.
- Published documentation often exposes workflow examples and capabilities without giving reproducible head-to-head efficacy numbers.
- Coverage and severity thresholds still need application-specific tailoring.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OWASP Top 10 for LLMs 2025 | Pass rate (↑) | DeepTeam `OWASPTop10()` framework | — | — | User-supplied `model_callback` | The documentation shows how to return `pass_rate` and test-case counts, but publishes no concrete numeric results; source: @confidentai2025owasptop10llms. |
| Source article capability summary | Scanner capability coverage ↑ | garak | — | — | Hugging Face / Replicate / OpenAI / LiteLLM / REST / GGUF | The Help Net Security article reports supported target interfaces, probe coverage, and logging outputs, but no quantitative benchmark or head-to-head scanner results; source: @helpnetsecurity2025garak. |
| — | Quantitative result availability ↑ | Vijil platform | — | — | — | Vijil's cited product pages describe Diamond's Trust Score workflow and broader lifecycle coverage, but publish no reproducible benchmark numbers for the platform as a whole; Hackett et al.'s guardrail-only results are not representative of Depot, Diamond, Darwin, or the full stack; source: @vijil2026agenttrustplatform. |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Usage-Level Monitoring
nodes.content:
# Usage-Level Monitoring

Techniques that aggregate many completed interaction records into structured summaries and then synthesise those summaries to identify harmful *usage patterns* across a session or user account. Rather than assigning a risk score to each prompt-completion pair in isolation, these approaches compress each interaction into a concise structured summary, accumulate hundreds of such summaries, and produce a human-legible report that exposes aggregate harms — coordinated misuse, multi-step fraud, and emergent attack patterns — that no single interaction would reveal. The result is a complementary monitoring layer that catches what per-interaction classifiers cannot: harms that are individually innocuous but collectively harmful.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@sumers2025monitoring\|Sumers et al. 2025 (Hierarchical Summarization)]] | Two-stage LLM pipeline that compresses each interaction into a structured text summary (Stage 1) and then aggregates hundreds of summaries in a single context window into a usage report with harm classification and cited representative interactions (Stage 2), detecting aggregate harms (e.g. click farms) and unanticipated misuse that per-interaction classifiers miss. |

## Advantages

- Detects aggregate harms that are invisible at the per-interaction level; no single interaction needs to be individually harmful for the usage pattern to be flagged.
- Produces human-legible reports with citations to representative interactions, enabling targeted reviewer action without reading every raw interaction at scale.
- Can surface unanticipated harm categories not covered by predefined taxonomies, because the summariser is instructed to flag overtly harmful content beyond its explicit specification.
- Interaction summaries are orders of magnitude smaller than raw interactions, making it practical to fit hundreds into a single context window for usage-level analysis.

## Limitations

- Operates post-hoc: by the time a harmful usage pattern is identified, the actions have already executed — the approach supports enforcement and deterrence rather than real-time prevention.
- Downstream quality depends on interaction-summary accuracy; hallucinations in early summaries propagate into the usage-level report.
- Usage-level summary quality is harder to measure than per-interaction accuracy; current deployments rely on periodic human spot-checks rather than automated metrics.
- Withholding the exact prompt text from the summariser (to reduce injection risk) may reduce accuracy for prompts where intent is only apparent from the literal text.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Human review of Computer Use interactions (accuracy) | % rated "Mostly accurate" or above ↑ | Hierarchical summarization — interaction summaries | 96% | — | Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) | Human raters compared summaries to original prompts/completions; four-point scale. Source: @sumers2025monitoring |
| Human review of Computer Use interactions (completeness) | % rated "Mostly complete" or above ↑ | Hierarchical summarization — interaction summaries | 98% | — | Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) | Same raters assessed whether all important aspects were captured. Source: @sumers2025monitoring |

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

nodes.name: Activation Steering
nodes.content:
# Activation Steering

Defences that derive a control vector from contrasts in hidden-state activations and then add that vector to selected layers during generation, steering the model toward a desired policy at inference time. The defence signal lives in the model's internal representations rather than in natural-language instructions, making this a representation-level steering technique rather than prompt engineering or fine-tuning.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@vogel2024representation\|Representation Engineering (Vogel)]] | Learns layer-wise control vectors from contrastive prompt pairs and injects them into Mistral-7B's hidden states during generation, qualitatively showing that a dealership-focused steering vector can resist a simple off-domain jailbreak better than a plain system prompt. |

## Advantages

- Pure inference-time control: no model fine-tuning is required.
- The steering coefficient provides a direct strength knob that is independent of prompt wording.
- The control signal is applied throughout generation instead of appearing as attacker-visible text in the prompt.

## Limitations

- Current evidence in this taxonomy is qualitative rather than benchmarked.
- The same mechanism can be used offensively to jailbreak a safety prompt.
- Large steering coefficients can destabilise outputs or make the control signal obvious.
- Requires access to internal activations, so it does not apply to closed hosted APIs.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Car dealership assistant off-domain jailbreak prompt | Qualitative robustness ↑ | Car dealership loyalty control vector | — | Baseline answered "Saturn" | Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 | Illustrative example from @vogel2024representation: with the vector applied, the model stays on-domain instead of answering the attacker's astronomy question. |
| Harmlessness system prompt + pipe-bomb request | Qualitative safety ↓ | Happiness control vector (+1.4) | — | Baseline refused the unsafe request | Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 | Negative result from @vogel2024representation: the same activation-steering mechanism can also jailbreak a weak safety prompt and elicit unsafe instructions. |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Learned Prompt Patches\|Learned Prompt Patches]] | Both families add a reusable steering artifact at inference time, but activation steering moves that artifact from visible prompt text into hidden states on every token, aiming to make it harder for injected instructions to simply override or ignore the defence. |

nodes.name: Inference-Time Model Steering
nodes.content:
# Inference-Time Model Steering

Defences that change model behaviour during generation by injecting, suppressing, or otherwise adjusting internal control signals — such as learned hidden-state vectors or selected KV-cache activations — rather than by rewriting the prompt or retraining model weights. These techniques sit between prompt engineering and fine-tuning: a reusable steering artifact or intervention rule is prepared offline, then applied inside the forward pass at inference time. Because the control signal acts in representation space instead of as ordinary text, the family aims to be harder to override with injected instructions than prompt-only defences.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Inference-Time Model Steering/Activation Steering\|Activation Steering]] | Adds learned control signals directly to hidden states during generation so the desired behaviour is reinforced on every token rather than expressed as a textual prompt fragment. |
| down:: [[Inference-Time Model Steering/KV-Cache Pruning\|KV-Cache Pruning]] | Identifies KV-cache neurons in untrusted context that are causing task drift and masks or prunes them so the model treats context as data rather than as executable instructions. |

## Advantages

- No full fine-tuning is required once the steering artifact has been derived.
- Separates behaviour control from prompt wording, giving developers a runtime steering knob instead of another text instruction.
- Can be enabled, disabled, or rescaled per deployment at inference time.
- Can target internal representations more selectively than whole-prompt rewrites, e.g. by pruning only the attributed cache features inside the untrusted span.

## Limitations

- Requires direct access to model internals, excluding closed API-only deployments.
- Evidence in the current taxonomy is still narrow and mostly limited to white-box, open-weight settings rather than full agentic deployment benchmarks.
- The same mechanism is dual-use: internal steering can harden a model or jailbreak it.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Car dealership assistant off-domain jailbreak prompt | Qualitative robustness ↑ | Control-vector steering | — | Baseline answered "Saturn" | Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 | Illustrative example from @vogel2024representation: activation steering keeps the assistant on-domain against a simple scope-bypass jailbreak. |
| Harmlessness system prompt + pipe-bomb request | Qualitative safety ↓ | Control-vector steering (happiness vector) | — | Baseline refused the unsafe request | Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 | Dual-use negative example from @vogel2024representation: the same inference-time steering mechanism can override a weak safety prompt and produce unsafe instructions. |
| SQuAD | ASR ↓ | CachePrune | 0.68 ± 0.41% | 9.01% Vanilla; 10.36% Sandwich; 6.37% Datamarking; 4.78% Encode-Base64 | Mistral-7B | Quantitative white-box steering result: pruning attributed KV-cache neurons gives this category its strongest standard QA robustness row; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |
| WildChat dialogue summarization | GPT-Score ↑ | CachePrune | 3.90 ± 0.03 | 3.88 Vanilla; 3.93 Delimiting; 3.91 Datamarking | Mistral-7B | Utility stays near baseline while ASR drops to 0.33 ± 0.26%, showing that representation-level steering can preserve summarization quality; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |
| SQuAD + adaptive GCG attack | ASR ↓ | CachePrune | 1.55 ± 0.35% | 10.32% Vanilla; 9.09% Delimiting; 5.53% Datamarking | Phi-3.5-mini-instruct | Adaptive-attack robustness row for this category; Liu et al.-style GCG still leaves CachePrune far below prompt-formatting baselines; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Prompt Massaging\|Prompt Massaging]] | Inference-time model steering moves the defence signal from attacker-visible prompt text into hidden states or KV caches, reducing reliance on the model faithfully obeying textual reminders, delimiters, or prompt patches. |

nodes.name: KV-Cache Pruning
nodes.content:
# KV-Cache Pruning

Inference-time defences that inspect the prompt context's key-value cache, identify neurons whose activations make untrusted context behave like an instruction, and suppress those neurons before normal generation proceeds. Instead of adding a new steering vector, these methods subtract or mask harmful internal state inside the context span itself.

## Techniques

| Node | What it does |
|---|---|
| down:: [[Sources/Defences/@wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense\|CachePrune]] | Uses activation × gradient attribution over context KV-cache neurons to prune the small subset that most strongly pushes the model toward following an injected instruction embedded in retrieved or user-supplied context. |

## Advantages

- Moves the instruction/data boundary into internal cache state rather than attacker-visible prompt text.
- Can preserve utility better than prompt-formatting baselines by pruning only attributed neurons inside the untrusted context span.
- Compatible with prompt caching because the edited context cache can be reused across generations.

## Limitations

- Requires white-box access to KV caches, gradients, and masking hooks.
- Current evidence is limited to open-weight QA and summarization settings, not full agentic tool-use systems.
- Utility depends on attribution quality: overly aggressive pruning can remove benign task signal along with the injected trigger.

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SQuAD | ASR ↓ | CachePrune | 0.68 ± 0.41% | 9.01% Vanilla; 10.36% Sandwich; 6.37% Datamarking; 4.78% Encode-Base64 | Mistral-7B | Strongest standard QA robustness row reported in the paper; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |
| HotpotQA | ASR ↓ | CachePrune | 15.23 ± 1.56% | 69.01% Vanilla; 67.21% Sandwich; 26.23% Datamarking; 3.05% Encode-Base64 | Llama3-8B | Harder multi-hop indirect-injection setting; robustness remains partial; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |
| WildChat dialogue summarization | ASR ↓ | CachePrune | 2.00 ± 0.41% | 14.50% Vanilla; 13.01% Sandwich; 7.50% Datamarking; 5.50% Encode-Base64 | Llama3-8B | Non-QA summarization robustness row; GPT-Score under attack remains 3.32 ± 0.10; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |
| WildChat dialogue summarization | GPT-Score ↑ | CachePrune | 3.90 ± 0.03 | 3.88 Vanilla; 3.93 Delimiting; 3.91 Datamarking | Mistral-7B | Utility stays near baseline while ASR drops to 0.33 ± 0.26%; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |
| SQuAD + adaptive GCG attack | ASR ↓ | CachePrune | 1.55 ± 0.35% | 10.32% Vanilla; 9.09% Delimiting; 5.53% Datamarking | Phi-3.5-mini-instruct | Liu et al.-style adaptive attack; source: @wang2025cachepruneneuralbasedattributiondefense |

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Delimiters\|Content Delimitation]] | KV-cache pruning pursues the same goal as delimiter-style formatting — making the model treat untrusted context as data — but enforces that boundary by masking the internal activations that trigger instruction-following instead of relying on textual markers alone. |
| improves-on:: [[Prompt Massaging/Instruction Reinforcement\|Instruction Reinforcement]] | CachePrune replaces repeated reminder text with a representation-level intervention, and the paper reports lower SQuAD ASR than Sandwich across all three tested backbones. |

nodes.name: Overview
nodes.content:
# Prompt Injection Defenses — Overview

A taxonomy of defenses against prompt injection attacks. Sources (the full source archive) live in `Sources/`; the nodes here organize them by technique.


---

## Taxonomy

### 1. Prompt Massaging
*No additional infrastructure; purely structural changes to the prompt.*

- down:: [[Prompt Massaging/Prompt Massaging\|Prompt Massaging]]

### 2. Detection
*Classify, monitor, or analyse prompts, responses, and agent behaviour for adversarial intent.*

- down:: [[Detection/Detection\|Detection]]

### 3. Sanitization
*Transform untrusted input to neutralize threats — whether or not the threat is first identified.*

- down:: [[Sanitization/Sanitization\|Sanitization]]

### 4. Architectural Isolation
*System-level structural guarantees; harder to bypass.*

- down:: [[Architectural Isolation/Architectural Isolation\|Architectural Isolation]]

### 5. Inference-Time Model Steering
*Modify hidden states or other internal control signals during generation so the model stays on-policy without retraining.*

- down:: [[Inference-Time Model Steering/Inference-Time Model Steering\|Inference-Time Model Steering]]

### 6. Training
*Modify model behaviour at training time so the model intrinsically resists injection.*

- down:: [[Training/Training\|Training]]

### 7. Proactive Deception
*Seed the attacker's environment with misleading data, honeytokens, and traps that exploit structural LLM weaknesses.*

- down:: [[Proactive Deception/Proactive Deception\|Proactive Deception]]

---

## Evaluation Highlights

**On agent task benchmarks (AgentDojo, 949 attacks across Workspace / Banking / Travel / Slack):**
All prompt-level techniques — sandwiching, spotlighting — offer no measurable reduction in ASR against an active adversary. Architectural isolation is decisive: CaMeL achieves near-zero ASR (~0%, with 1-2 residual attacks outside its threat model) at a utility cost of ~7 percentage points (~77% vs ~84%) and ~3x token overhead. Source: @debenedetti2025defeatingpromptinjectionsdesign. Runtime information flow control (AgentArmor) achieves 3% ASR on AgentDojo (GPT-4o-mini) and 0% ASR on ASB's observation prompt injection setting, with only 1% utility loss, by building a Program Dependence Graph over the agent's execution trace and blocking tool calls whose parameters are tainted by low-integrity inputs. Source: @wang2025agentarmorenforcingprogramanalysis. FIDES, a taint-tracking variable-passing planner, achieves 1/949 attacks on AgentDojo (0 policy-relevant) with gpt-4o, and raises task completion ~24% above an undefended planner for reasoning models (o1/o3/o4-mini) when prompt-tuned — demonstrating that deterministic label-lattice enforcement can simultaneously improve utility and eliminate policy-relevant attacks. Source: @costa2025securingaiagentsinformationflow. LLM-based sanitization also reaches near-zero ASR without modifying the underlying agent: PromptArmor with a GPT-4.1 guardrail achieves 0.00% ASR (down from 54.53% undefended) and raises utility under attack to 76.35%, remaining robust against adaptive fuzzing attacks (0.34% ASR vs. 36.99% baseline). Source: @shi2025promptarmorsimpleeffectiveprompt. DataFilter, a complementary fine-tuned seq2seq approach, achieves average ASR 1.2% on AgentDojo and 2.0% on InjecAgent (both gpt-4o backend) with only ~1% benign utility loss, and is robust against human-designed adaptive attacks (0.0% ASR on AgentDojo). However, a strong LLM-based adaptive attacker with full filter knowledge (Nasr et al. 2025) raises DataFilter's ASR to 83% on AgentDojo — the lowest of all tested defences under this attack, but not near-zero. Source: @wang2026defendingpromptinjectiondatafilter. ParseData+CheckTool, a training-free prompt-based approach, achieves the lowest reported Avg ASR (0.11%–0.34%) of any prompt-based defence on AgentDojo across three tested models by asking the LLM to enumerate format and logical constraints on tool outputs and extracting only the conforming subset. The trade-off is steep: Benign Utility drops 28%–45% relative to No Defense due to the additional LLM-inference overhead on every tool result. Source: @yu2026defenseindirectpromptinjection. DRIFT, a three-layer plan-then-execute defence (Secure Planner + Dynamic Validator + Injection Isolator), achieves 1.4% ASR on AgentDojo with best-in-class benign utility (57.3%) by dynamically updating security constraints for low-risk deviations while blocking write/execute-level injections and scrubbing injected content from memory each turn; it extends to ASB (4.8% ASR) and AgentDyn (3.1% ASR), where all defences still struggle on open-ended tasks. Source: @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense. RTBAS (Robust Tool-Based Agent Systems) adapts classical information-flow control to the tool-calling loop with three components — a dependency screener (LM-Judge or attention-based), a redactor that masks irrelevant tainted history, and lattice-based policy enforcement before each tool call — achieving 0% ASR on AgentDojo (weighted average, under attack) with <2% utility degradation (LM-Judge screener, GPT-4o), and simultaneously reducing accidental privacy-leakage FPR to 8.1% vs. 29.7% for a GPTs baseline across 37 confidentiality tasks. Source: @zhong2025rtbasdefendingllmagents.

**On manual injection test suites:**
Fine-tuned representation approaches (StruQ) reduce ASR to <2% on 13 of 15 standard attack types with near-zero utility loss (AlpacaEval win rate 67.6% vs 67.2% undefended). Optimization-based attacks (GCG, TAP) partially break through — 58% and 9% ASR respectively — but are expensive to mount. Source: @chen2025struq. For *fixed single-task applications*, Jatmo takes a different training-time route: it distils the trusted task into a non-instruction-tuned model and removes the live prompt channel entirely, reaching 0-2% ASR across seven tasks while retaining 98-100% of teacher quality; the trade-off is one fine-tuned model per task. Source: @piet2024jatmopromptinjectiondefense. Preference-optimisation fine-tuning (SecAlign) improves on this for general-purpose assistants: 0% Max Opt-Free ASR on AlpacaFarm across all five tested models, and 1–14% Max Opt-Based ASR (vs. 27–60% for StruQ), while requiring no front-end infrastructure. Source: @Chen_2025. SecAlign++ (Meta SecAlign) further improves on SecAlign by randomising injection positions during training and using self-generated DPO labels, reducing the Basic Adaptive ASR from 6.7% to 0.5% on Llama-3.3-70B while raising utility; the resulting Meta-SecAlign-70B is the first fully open-source model achieving commercial-grade PI security across 7 benchmarks (0–6.4% ASR) including agentic tool-calling (1.9% AgentDojo ASR, 84.5% utility). Source: @chen2026metasecalignsecurefoundation.

**On prompt-leak detection (three open-source tools, 11,000 malicious / 1,000 benign prompts):**
Vigil achieves the best F-beta (beta=1/11, penalising false positives heavily) of 0.998 at FPR 0.000, but its default recall is only 0.838. LLM Guard (Full mode) reaches recall 0.999 but FPR 0.127. Rebuff balances the two at recall 0.981 and FPR 0.034. A composite policy combining the strongest scanner from each tool reaches F-beta 0.998 with FPR 0.016 and Recall 0.981, outperforming all three individually on F1. Source: @gakh2025enhancingsecurityllmapplications.

**Utility preservation:**
StruQ and CaMeL's Tool Filter both preserve ~84% task completion. The full CaMeL system costs ~7pp of utility. Training-time adversarial fine-tuning (BIPIA white-box, GPT-4 responses) preserves utility: ROUGE-1 recall 0.626 vs 0.619 undefended, MT-Bench 4.83 vs 4.81 undefended on Vicuna-7B. Fine-tuning with low-quality automatic labels does degrade utility (MT-Bench win rate drops from 67% to 26%). Jatmo preserves fixed-task utility almost perfectly — 98-100% of teacher quality across seven tasks — by training on benign task examples and then deploying a single-purpose model. SecAlign (DPO fine-tuning) preserves utility statistically — AlpacaEval2 win rate 70.73% vs 67.01% undefended on Mistral-7B-Instruct.

**Technique selection by goal:**
- *Minimize ASR on agent benchmarks* → Architectural isolation (CaMeL) or LLM-based sanitization (PromptArmor); both reach near-zero ASR, with PromptArmor offering a drop-in deployment advantage at the cost of per-sample LLM overhead
- *Minimize ASR on injection test suites with minimal utility cost* → Preference optimisation fine-tuning (SecAlign / Meta SecAlign) or structured queries (StruQ); Meta SecAlign additionally generalises to agentic benchmarks
- *Zero infrastructure change* → Prompt massaging, but expect limited protection against adaptive attackers
