nodes.name: When Benchmarks Lie: Evaluating Malicious Prompt Classifiers Under True Distribution Shift
nodes.content:
---
title: "When Benchmarks Lie: Evaluating Malicious Prompt Classifiers Under True Distribution Shift"
authors: [Max Fomin]
year: 2026
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.14161
  - https://github.com/maxf-zn/prompt-mining
  - https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2602.14161
---

# When Benchmarks Lie: Evaluating Malicious Prompt Classifiers Under True Distribution Shift

Fomin studies malicious-prompt detection as a benchmarking problem rather than a new defence. The paper builds a 105K-sample suite from 18 datasets covering harmful requests, jailbreaks, indirect prompt injection, extraction attacks, and benign prompts, then argues that standard train/test splits from the same source datasets badly overstate how well classifiers generalise to genuinely new attack distributions.

## Methodology

The benchmark aggregates 18 public datasets (105,034 samples; 47% malicious), including mixed-class sets such as BIPIA, WildJailbreak, Jayavibhav, Qualifire, Safeguard, and Deepset; malicious-only sets such as AdvBench, HarmBench, InjecAgent, LLMail, Mosscap, and Yanismiraoui; and benign-only sets such as Enron and OpenOrca. The main detector reads hidden activations from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct at the last user-message token, then trains simple classifiers on either raw layer-31 activations or layer-27 sparse autoencoder (SAE) features. The key protocol is **Leave-One-Dataset-Out (LODO)**: train on all but one dataset, test on the held-out dataset, and pool the held-out predictions to estimate true out-of-distribution performance. The paper compares this against ordinary 5-fold CV and official same-source test splits, benchmarks PromptGuard 2, Llama Guard, and Llama-3.1-8B used as an LLM judge, and introduces *LODO coefficient retention* to identify SAE features whose predictive power disappears when particular datasets are removed.

## Advantages

- Shows that same-source benchmark splits can inflate malicious-prompt detection performance by large margins.
- Covers agentic and indirect-injection settings, not just direct jailbreak prompts.
- Adds diagnostic analysis of dataset shortcuts and explanation quality instead of only reporting aggregate scores.

## Limitations

- The strongest detector results rely on white-box access to LLM activations, which many API-only deployments do not expose.
- Most experiments use Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the backbone, so absolute numbers may shift for other model families.
- Some baseline failures partly reflect interface mismatch: PromptGuard 2 and Llama Guard could not process InjecAgent-style tool schemas.
- Focuses on static distribution shift, not adaptive attackers that optimise specifically against the detector.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@meta2024promptguard\|Prompt Guard]] | Benchmarks PromptGuard 2 on the 18-dataset suite and finds only 37.3% detection on indirect injection; the model also cannot be meaningfully evaluated on agentic tool-injection samples because it lacks the required chat/tool interface. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@inan2023llamaguardllmbasedinputoutput\|Llama Guard]] | Benchmarks Llama Guard on the same suite and finds 27.4% detection on indirect injection and 15.2% on extraction attacks, while also reporting that the chat-format assumptions prevent evaluation on InjecAgent-style tool injections. |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 18-dataset malicious-prompt benchmark (pooled) | ROC AUC ↑ | Raw activations + logistic regression, 5-fold CV | 0.996 | 0.912 LODO | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Standard CV overestimates true OOD performance by 8.4 percentage points. |
| 18-dataset malicious-prompt benchmark (pooled) | ROC AUC ↑ | Raw activations + logistic regression, official held-out test splits | 0.997 | 0.912 LODO | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Same-source held-out splits are no more trustworthy than CV for distribution-shifted deployment. |
| 18-dataset malicious-prompt benchmark (pooled) | ROC AUC ↑ | Raw activations + logistic regression, LODO | 0.912 | SAE features 0.838; MLP 0.841 | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Best pooled OOD result among the paper's classifier variants. |
| Dataset identity prediction (18 classes) | Accuracy ↑ | Logistic regression on SAE features | 96.6% | 5.6% random chance | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Confirms datasets are trivially separable in feature space, enabling shortcut learning. |
| Jayavibhav held out | Accuracy ↑ | Raw activations + logistic regression, same-source test split | 94.5% | 69.1% LODO | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Largest reported per-dataset inflation gap: +25.4 points. |
| Detection by attack category: indirect injection | Detection rate ↑ | PromptGuard 2 | 37.3% | Raw-activation LODO classifier 68.0% | PromptGuard 2 | Production baseline is much weaker on embedded attacks than the activation probe. |
| Detection by attack category: indirect injection | Detection rate ↑ | Llama Guard | 27.4% | Raw-activation LODO classifier 68.0% | Llama Guard | Worse than PromptGuard 2 on this category. |
| Detection by attack category: agentic tool injection | Detection rate ↑ | Llama-3.1-8B as judge | 21.5% | Raw-activation LODO classifier 99.0% | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | PromptGuard 2 and Llama Guard are omitted here because they cannot evaluate InjecAgent tool schemas. |
| Detection by attack category: benign traffic | FPR ↓ | Raw-activation LODO classifier | 6.5% | PromptGuard 2 0.4%; Llama Guard 3.0%; Llama-as-Judge 4.4% | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Best attack coverage comes with the highest false-positive cost. |

nodes.name: Enhancing Security in LLM Applications: A Performance Evaluation of Early Detection Systems
nodes.content:
---
title: "Enhancing Security in LLM Applications: A Performance Evaluation of Early Detection Systems"
authors: ["Valerii Gakh", "Hayretdin Bahsi"]
year: 2025
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.19109
---

# Enhancing Security in LLM Applications: A Performance Evaluation of Early Detection Systems

A systematic comparative evaluation of three open-source prompt injection detection solutions — LLM Guard, Vigil, and Rebuff — against a self-constructed dataset of 11,000 prompt-leak attacks spanning 11 attack classes and 1,000 benign prompts. The paper analyses the individual scanners inside each solution, identifies implementation weaknesses (broken canary checks, an injection vulnerability in Rebuff's secondary model scanner), proposes mitigations, and concludes with a cross-solution comparison and a custom detection policy that outperforms all three on the F-beta and F1 metrics.

## Methodology

The authors develop a target LLM application — a LangChain-based document-chat agent backed by an OpenAI function-calling setup with a GPT model — whose confidential system instructions they aim to leak. They construct a dataset of prompt-leak attack samples using five base techniques: naive, context ignoring (CI), context manipulation (CM, repeated characters), prefix injection (PI), and leet obfuscation. Samples using up to four combined techniques yield 11 labelled classes (1,000 confirmed working samples each) for a total of 11,000 malicious samples. These are paired with 1,000 benign user prompts, producing an 11:1 class imbalance that motivates the use of F-beta with beta = 1/11 (penalising false positives heavily) as the primary metric alongside recall, FPR, precision, and F1. All three detection tools are evaluated at their default configurations and, separately, with each individual scanner tuned to its ROC-optimal threshold via Precision-Recall curve maximisation. The authors also test attack samples against GPT-4o and Claude-3-5-sonnet to measure attack success rates (ASR) on production models unprotected by the detection layer.

## Advantages

- Provides the first direct, per-scanner breakdown of LLM Guard, Vigil, and Rebuff on the same dataset and metric set, giving practitioners concrete numbers for choosing a solution.
- Covers 11 attack classes including combined techniques (CI + PI + leet + CM), which prior work tested only in isolation or pairs.
- Identifies two concrete implementation flaws — ineffective canary delimiters in Vigil and Rebuff, and a prompt-injection vulnerability in Rebuff's secondary model scanner — and proposes actionable mitigations for both.
- Proposes a custom cross-solution detection policy (Yara-based or vectordb-based or Rebuff canary, or both transformer and secondary-model fire simultaneously) that achieves FPR 0.016, Recall 0.981, and F-beta 0.998, outperforming all individual solutions on F-beta and F1.
- Tests 11,000 malicious samples against GPT-4o and Claude-3-5-sonnet, quantifying residual ASR (2% and 19% respectively) for practitioners using hardened frontier models without detection layers.

## Limitations

- The dataset covers only prompt-leak attacks; indirect prompt injection from retrieved tool outputs is explicitly deferred to future work.
- All malicious samples are based on manually written base prompts; token-level optimised attacks (e.g. GCG) are not included, though the authors note perplexity-based defences can handle those.
- The target application uses a single system prompt; variability across diverse real-world system instructions is not studied.
- The 11:1 benign-to-malicious imbalance matches assumed production ratios but differs from the 1:1 ratio used when training the transformer classifier, potentially inflating or deflating observed FPR relative to open-training-set benchmarks.
- Rebuff and Vigil were in alpha state at time of writing and Rebuff was subsequently archived, so findings may not generalise to future maintained versions.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@protectai2023llmguard\|LLM Guard]] | Reports per-scanner and whole-solution recall, FPR, precision, F-beta, and F1 for LLM Guard's transformer scanner in Full and Sentence modes against an 11,000-malicious / 1,000-benign prompt-leak dataset. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@swanda2023vigil\|Vigil]] | Reports per-scanner performance for Vigil's YARA, transformer, VectorDB, prompt-response similarity, and canary scanners, and for the full four-scanner default policy, on the same dataset. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@protectai2023rebuff\|Rebuff]] | Reports per-scanner and whole-solution metrics for Rebuff's heuristics, VectorDB (standard and extended store), secondary model (GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4o), and canary (default and modified) scanners. |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.838 | — | DeBERTa-v3 + YARA + VectorDB + canary | Canary check contributes zero true positives in default config; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.000 | LLM Guard Full 0.127; Rebuff 0.034 | — | Lowest FPR of three solutions; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F-beta ↑ | Vigil (4 scanners, default policy) | 0.998 | LLM Guard Full 0.989; Rebuff 0.997 | — | Best F-beta (beta=1/11) of three solutions at default config; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Vigil transformer (th=0.999) | 0.968 | LLM Guard Sentence 1.000 | DeBERTa-v3 | FPR 0.014 vs LLM Guard Sentence FPR 0.157; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | Vigil transformer (th=0.999) | 0.014 | LLM Guard Sentence 0.157 | DeBERTa-v3 | Vigil threshold tunability gives better FPR control than LLM Guard's fixed 0.92 threshold; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Vigil transformer (th=0.98) | 0.986 | — | DeBERTa-v3 | FPR 0.050; F-beta 0.995; F1 0.991; vendor-recommended threshold; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Vigil VectorDB (th=0.17317) | 0.859 | Rebuff VectorDB (th=0.82783) 0.875 | ChromaDB + ada-002 embeddings | FPR 0.01; recall drops sharply on leet-obfuscated and context-manipulation attack classes; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Vigil canary (default) | 0.000 | — | — | Zero true positives across all 11 attack classes; canary delimiters treated as comments by LLM; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Vigil canary (modified placement) | 0.000 | — | — | Inserting canary between sentences in system message still produces zero detections; Reported in @swanda2023vigil |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | LLM Guard transformer (Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.999 | — | DeBERTa-v3 | FPR 0.127; Reported in @protectai2023llmguard |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | LLM Guard transformer (Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.127 | Vigil th=0.999: 0.014; Rebuff: 0.034 | DeBERTa-v3 | Highest FPR of the three solutions evaluated; Reported in @protectai2023llmguard |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F-beta ↑ | LLM Guard transformer (Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.989 | Vigil th=0.999: 0.998; Rebuff: 0.997 | DeBERTa-v3 | beta=1/11, penalising false positives heavily; lowest F-beta of three solutions; Reported in @protectai2023llmguard |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F1 ↑ | LLM Guard transformer (Full mode, th=0.92) | 0.994 | — | DeBERTa-v3 | Reported in @protectai2023llmguard |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | LLM Guard transformer (Sentence mode, th=0.92) | 1.000 | Full mode: 0.999 | DeBERTa-v3 | FPR 0.157; higher FPR than Full mode; Reported in @protectai2023llmguard |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | LLM Guard transformer (Sentence mode, th=0.92) | 0.157 | Full mode: 0.127 | DeBERTa-v3 | Sentence mode raises both recall and FPR; Full mode described as more practical; Reported in @protectai2023llmguard |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset (11,000 mal. / 1,000 benign) | Recall ↑ | Rebuff (4 scanners, default thresholds) | 0.981 | — | GPT-4o + VectorDB + heuristics + canary | FPR 0.034; Reported in @protectai2023rebuff |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | Rebuff (4 scanners, default thresholds) | 0.034 | LLM Guard Full 0.127; Vigil 0.000 | GPT-4o | Middle ground between LLM Guard (0.127) and Vigil (0.000); Reported in @protectai2023rebuff |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | F-beta ↑ | Rebuff (4 scanners, default thresholds) | 0.997 | LLM Guard Full 0.989; Vigil 0.998 | GPT-4o | beta=1/11, penalising false positives heavily; Reported in @protectai2023rebuff |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Rebuff LLM scanner (GPT-4o, th=0.752) | 0.868 | GPT-3.5-turbo th=0.802: 0.914 | GPT-4o | GPT-4o yields far fewer false positives (FPR 0.021 vs 0.499 for GPT-3.5-turbo); Reported in @protectai2023rebuff |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Rebuff VectorDB extended store (th=0.83483) | 0.930 | Vigil VectorDB th=0.17317: 0.859 | — | Extended store adds leet-obfuscated samples; standard store recall is lower; Reported in @protectai2023rebuff |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | Recall ↑ | Rebuff canary (modified — with handling instructions) | ~0.4–0.84 per class | Default canary: 0.000 all classes | — | Default canary produces zero true positives; modified placement with explicit LLM instructions raises recall substantially; Reported in @protectai2023rebuff |
| Authors' prompt-leak dataset | FPR ↓ | Custom cross-solution policy (Yara-based or VectorDB-based or Rebuff canary, or transformer + secondary-model both fire) | 0.016 | LLM Guard 0.127; Rebuff 0.034; Vigil 0.000 | DeBERTa-v3 + GPT-4o | Recall 0.981; F-beta 0.998; F1 0.990; outperforms all three solutions on F-beta and F1 |
| GPT-4o (production model, no detection layer) | ASR ↓ | Prompt-leak attack dataset (11,000 malicious samples) | 0.02 | — | GPT-4o | ~2% of all malicious prompts successfully leaked system instructions; prefix injection was the most successful single technique |
| Claude-3-5-sonnet (production model, no detection layer) | ASR ↓ | Prompt-leak attack dataset (11,000 malicious samples) | 0.19 | — | Claude-3-5-sonnet | ~19% ASR; Claude more vulnerable than GPT-4o to most single-technique classes except context ignoring |

nodes.name: Introducing the AI Guardrails Index
nodes.content:
---
title: "Introducing the AI Guardrails Index"
authors: ["Guardrails AI"]
year: 2026
links:
  - https://guardrailsai.com/blog/introducing-the-ai-guardrails-index
  - https://index.guardrailsai.com/
  - https://guardrailsai.com/index/ai-guardrails-index-jailbreaking
  - https://huggingface.co/datasets/GuardrailsAI/detect-jailbreak
---

# Introducing the AI Guardrails Index

A vendor-authored benchmark and public leaderboard from Guardrails AI for comparing AI safety guardrails across six domains: jailbreaking, PII detection, content moderation, hallucination detection, competitor checks, and topic restriction. The landing page advertises 24 guardrails tested, 6 datasets, and 32 GPU-days of evaluation; for prompt-injection security, the most relevant slice is the jailbreaking track.

## Methodology

The benchmark is organized as six per-domain leaderboards rather than a single aggregate score. The introduction post says it evaluates "20+ leading guardrail solutions across 6 critical safety domains," while the index landing page makes that scope more concrete with 24 guardrails, 6 datasets, and 32 days spent on GPU.

For the prompt-injection-adjacent jailbreaking track, the linked category page defines the task as detecting prompts that try to bypass model safety controls and links a public dataset on Hugging Face (`GuardrailsAI/detect-jailbreak`). The published leaderboard data exposes at least five fields per system: latency, Max F1, TPR at Max F1, FPR at Max F1, and ROC AUC. That means the benchmark explicitly evaluates the trade-off between attack detection, false positives, and response speed.

The source positions the index as a deployment-oriented benchmark for selecting production guardrails, but the introduction page is still high level: it does not fully document dataset construction, annotation policy, or train/test splitting, instead pointing readers to the index pages and an accompanying benchmark report.

## Advantages

- Broad practical scope: 24 guardrails across six safety domains.
- Surfaces operational trade-offs, not just headline accuracy, by including false positives and latency.
- Publishes public benchmark artifacts for the jailbreaking slice, including a dataset link and machine-readable leaderboard data.
- Useful for comparing managed APIs against open models in a common leaderboard format.

## Limitations

- Vendor-authored and promotional: the narrative repeatedly frames Guardrails AI as the winner.
- The introduction post omits many methodological details needed for strict reproducibility.
- Only the jailbreaking slice is directly relevant to prompt-injection defence; much of the benchmark covers broader AI safety tasks.
- Several evaluated systems are proprietary services, limiting independent diagnosis of why they succeed or fail.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@guardrailsai2026detect\|Guardrails AI Detect Jailbreak]] | The jailbreaking leaderboard ranks Guardrails AI's Detect Jailbreak first on Max F1 (0.8118) and also reports 0.0527 ms latency for the same system. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@meta2024promptguard\|Prompt Guard]] | The jailbreaking leaderboard reports Meta's Prompt Guard 86M as a baseline, with Max F1 0.6663, TPR 100/100, and FPR 100/100 on the overall jailbreak set. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@shone2025enhance\|Azure Prompt Shields]] | The jailbreaking leaderboard includes Microsoft's AI Content Safety Prompt Shields, reporting Max F1 0.7331 with TPR 67/100 and FPR 17/100. |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AI Guardrails Index — Jailbreaking | Max F1 ↑ | Detect Jailbreak | 0.8118 | Claude 3 Haiku 0.8101; AI Content Safety Prompt Shields 0.7331; Prompt Guard 86M 0.6663 | Guardrails AI Detect Jailbreak | Overall jailbreak leaderboard; best Max F1 in the published CSV behind the jailbreaking page. |
| AI Guardrails Index — Jailbreaking | Max F1 ↑ | Claude 3 Haiku | 0.8101 | Detect Jailbreak 0.8118 | Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku | TPR 92/100 and FPR 35/100 at Max F1; supports the source's claim that Anthropic is strong on threat detection but less usable because of false positives. |
| AI Guardrails Index — Jailbreaking | Max F1 ↑ | AI Content Safety Prompt Shields | 0.7331 | Detect Jailbreak 0.8118 | Microsoft AI Content Safety Prompt Shields | TPR 67/100 and FPR 17/100 at Max F1. |
| AI Guardrails Index — Jailbreaking | Max F1 ↑ | Prompt Guard 86M | 0.6663 | Detect Jailbreak 0.8118 | Meta Llama Prompt Guard 86M | TPR 100/100 and FPR 100/100 at Max F1 on the overall jailbreak slice. |

nodes.name: PromptShield: Deployable Detection for Prompt Injection Attacks
nodes.content:
---
title: "PromptShield: Deployable Detection for Prompt Injection Attacks"
authors: ["Dennis Jacob", "Hend Alzahrani", "Zhanhao Hu", "Basel Alomair", "David Wagner"]
year: 2025
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.15145
  - https://huggingface.co/datasets/hendzh/PromptShield
  - https://github.com/wagner-group/PromptShield
  - https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2501.15145
---

# PromptShield: Deployable Detection for Prompt Injection Attacks

PromptShield is a benchmark for training and evaluating prompt-injection detectors under realistic deployment conditions, especially the low-false-positive regime that matters for production systems. The paper also uses the benchmark to fine-tune a detector of the same name, but its main contribution is the benchmark and evaluation methodology.

## Methodology

PromptShield starts from a simple taxonomy: model traffic is either **conversational** (chatbot-style user requests, usually benign) or **application-structured** (data inserted into an application prompt, where indirect prompt injection can happen). The benchmark therefore mixes both kinds of benign traffic instead of evaluating only attack-heavy datasets.

For benign data, the authors curate conversational examples from **Ultrachat**, **LMSYS**, prompt-only samples from **Alpaca** and **databricks-dolly**, and **IFEval**; application-structured benign data comes from **Alpaca**, **databricks-dolly**, **natural-instructions**, and **Synthetic Python Problems (SPP)**. For malicious data, they generate four template-based attacks from StruQ-style attacks — naive, ignore, completion, and combined — over Alpaca, Dolly, and SPP, and add naturally occurring attacks from **HackAPrompt** plus samples from **OpenPromptInject**.

The train/eval split is intentionally out-of-distribution: simpler or more filtered data goes into train, while more varied data goes into evaluation. They also use different "link phrases" between train and eval injections so detectors do not merely memorize phrases like "ignore all previous instructions." Detectors are then calibrated for deployment by choosing thresholds that target fixed false-positive rates (1%, 0.5%, 0.1%, 0.05%), reflecting the paper's claim that prompt-injection detectors must be judged where false alarms are rare enough to be usable.

## Advantages

- Measures false positives on realistic conversational traffic, not just attack corpora.
- Separates training and evaluation data to emphasize out-of-distribution generalization.
- Evaluates detectors at deployment-relevant thresholds such as 0.1% FPR, where many prior detectors collapse.
- Openly released benchmark and code make follow-up comparisons easy.

## Limitations

- The benchmark and detector do not account for concept drift or optimized adaptive attacks designed specifically to evade the classifier.
- It is limited to text-only inputs; multimodal prompt injection is out of scope.
- Optimization-based attacks such as GCG are explicitly out of scope for the benchmark construction.
- The paper calibrates thresholds on the evaluation split in its experiments; the authors note that a validation-set calibration procedure would be more realistic.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@meta2024promptguard\|PromptGuard]] | PromptShield shows that PromptGuard's reported AUC does not translate into deployable low-FPR performance: on the PromptShield evaluation split it reaches only 9.39% TPR at 0.1% FPR. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@protectai2024debertav2\|ProtectAI DeBERTa Prompt Injection Detector v2]] | PromptShield evaluates ProtectAI v2 in the same low-FPR regime and finds it effectively unusable at 0.1% FPR, where TPR falls to 0.00%. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@li-etal-2025-piguard\|PIGuard / InjecGuard]] | PromptShield compares against InjecGuard and finds it stronger than PromptGuard at 1% FPR, but still far behind the PromptShield detector in the strict low-FPR setting. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Tools/@fmops2023distilbertpromptinjection\|DistilBERT Prompt Injection Classifier]] | PromptShield includes the lightweight fmops DistilBERT guard as a baseline and shows its TPR drops to 2.10% at 0.1% FPR. |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@1% FPR ↑ | PromptShield detector | 94.80% | InjecGuard 20.37%; PromptGuard 12.78% | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Table 4; headline best result. |
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@0.1% FPR ↑ | PromptShield detector | 65.33% | PromptGuard 9.39%; InjecGuard 6.61%; Fmops 2.10%; ProtectAI v2 0.00% | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Table 4; key low-FPR deployment comparison. |
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@0.1% FPR ↑ | PromptShield detector | 31.45% | PromptGuard 9.39%; InjecGuard 6.61% | DeBERTa-v3-base | Table 4; similarly sized model still beats prior work. |
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@0.1% FPR ↑ | PromptGuard | 9.39% | 22.82% TPR at default threshold, but with 2.91% FPR | mDeBERTa-v3-base | Tables 3–4; shows why threshold calibration matters. |
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@0.1% FPR ↑ | ProtectAI v2 | 0.00% | PromptShield DeBERTa 31.45% | DeBERTa-v3-base | Table 4. |
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@0.1% FPR ↑ | InjecGuard | 6.61% | PromptShield DeBERTa 31.45% | DeBERTa-v3-base | Table 4. |
| PromptShield evaluation split | TPR@0.1% FPR ↑ | Fmops | 2.10% | PromptShield DeBERTa 31.45% | DistilBERT | Table 4. |
| PromptShield conversational benign subset | FPR ↓ | PromptShield detector trained with conversational data | 0.06% | 0.32% without conversational training | Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | Table 9; conversational training sharply reduces false alarms on chatbot traffic. |

nodes.name: Testing the Limits of Jailbreaking Defenses with the Purple Problem
nodes.content:
---
title: "Testing the Limits of Jailbreaking Defenses with the Purple Problem"
authors: [Taeyoun Kim, Suhas Kotha, Aditi Raghunathan]
year: 2024
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.14725
---

# Testing the Limits of Jailbreaking Defenses with the Purple Problem

A benchmark and analytical framework that decomposes jailbreaking defences into two stages — **definition** (what counts as an unsafe output) and **enforcement** (how that definition is applied) — and introduces the **Purple Problem** as a controlled evaluation that holds definition quality constant. By reducing the unsafe-output criterion to a single, perfectly specified predicate ("any output containing the word 'purple'"), the paper isolates enforcement failures from definition failures and demonstrates that current fine-tuning and input-filtering defences all break under adaptive attacks.

## Methodology

**Dataset.** A Purple Questions dataset of 3,552 prompts that naturally elicit the word "purple" is constructed by querying GPT-3.5-turbo across 17 topics (science, fairytales, history, sports, …). Split: 80/12/8% for train / validation / test (2,841 / 426 / 285 prompts). Ground-truth labels are exact-match: any generation containing "purple" or "Purple" is unsafe.

**Metric.** Defence Success Rate (DSR) — the probability that the defended model does not output the forbidden string. Higher is better. An undefended Vicuna-7b achieves DSR 10.9% on natural prompts (it frequently says "purple"), establishing the baseline.

**Threat model.** White-box: the attacker knows all system components including model weights and conversation template. Models tested: Llama-IT (Llama-7b instruction-tuned), Vicuna-7b, and Llama-2-7b-chat.

**Evaluation protocol.** Each defence is evaluated in sequence, then immediately followed by an adaptive attack designed with knowledge of that defence. Three defence families are tested:

1. **Fine-tuning (DPO / PPO)** — RLHF-based training on Purple Questions preference data, then attacked with GCG adversarial suffixes (Zou et al. 2023). Adversarial training (augmenting fine-tuning with collected GCG suffixes) is then re-attacked with adaptively re-optimised GCG suffixes.
2. **Input preprocessing** — three methods stacked on top of DPO fine-tuning:
   - *Paraphrase* (Jain et al. 2023): input paraphrased via ChatGPT before passing to the model. Attacked by injecting an instruction to preserve the adversarial suffix verbatim through paraphrasing.
   - *In-context examples* (Wei et al. 2023): four few-shot refusal examples prepended. Attacked by optimising new GCG suffixes with the in-context context present.
   - *Perplexity filter* (Alon & Kamfonas 2023): rejects inputs with anomalously high perplexity. Attacked by prepending a low-perplexity passage to dilute the adversarial input's perplexity score.
3. **Output filtering** (as a strong baseline) — any output containing "purple" is replaced by a refusal. Shown to be the only enforcement mechanism that is adversarially robust.

**Real-world benchmark analysis.** The paper also analyses several existing jailbreaking benchmarks (DecodingTrust, HarmBench, JailbreakBench) by applying output filtering against each benchmark's own implicit safety definition (Perspective API, a Llama-2-13b classifier, Llama Guard, etc.) to measure how much of the benchmark's measured safety is due to enforcement rather than definition quality.

## Advantages

- Perfectly specified definition eliminates definition-gap as a confounding variable, cleanly attributing benchmark failures to enforcement weaknesses.
- Reveals the **adaptive attack imperative**: defences that appear robust under non-adaptive evaluation are all broken by simple, defence-aware prompt modifications.
- Demonstrates that **output filtering** is a strong, underappreciated enforcement baseline that trivially "solves" current benchmarks without any utility loss.
- Provides a clear framework — Define + Enforce — for diagnosing and reporting future defence evaluations.

## Limitations

- The Purple Problem's definition is far simpler than any real-world harm concept; whether enforcement failures at this level translate to complex, learned definitions is not established.
- White-box access assumption may overstate practical attacker power for closed-source deployed systems.
- Output filtering, while robust for this definition, incurs inference-time overhead (rejection sampling, streaming constraints) not accounted for in the evaluation.
- The paper does not evaluate the quality of definitions themselves — that question is posed as future work.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks\|Jain et al. — Baseline Defenses]] | Tests the paraphrase defence; finds it broken by a paraphrase-aware adaptive attack that instructs the paraphraser to preserve the adversarial suffix. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@alon2023detectinglanguagemodelattacks\|Alon & Kamfonas — Perplexity Defence]] | Tests the perplexity-based input filter; finds it bypassed by prepending a low-perplexity passage to reduce the adversarial input's measured perplexity below natural prompts. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@wei2024jailbreakguardalignedlanguage\|Wei et al. — ICL Guard (ICD)]] | Tests few-shot in-context refusal examples as a defence; finds it broken under adversarial suffixes on Llama-IT and Llama-2-chat, and by adaptive re-optimised suffixes on Vicuna. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Benchmarks/@wang2024decodingtrustcomprehensiveassessmenttrustworthiness\|DecodingTrust]] | Shows that output filtering against DecodingTrust's own Perspective API definition achieves 0% toxicity with no utility loss on SQuAD, exposing the benchmark as a test of enforcement rather than definition quality. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Benchmarks/@mazeika2024harmbench\|HarmBench]] | Finds that the HarmBench Llama-2-13b classifier misclassifies SQuAD outputs as toxic (64.3% rejection rate), lowering F1 from 51.7% to 16.8%, revealing the definition's imperfections. |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Purple | DSR ↑ | Fine-tuned DPO — natural prompts | 100% | 10.9% undefended | Vicuna | DPO fine-tuning eliminates "purple" on in-distribution prompts |
| Purple | DSR ↑ | Fine-tuned DPO — GCG adversarial suffixes | 3.5% | 10.9% undefended | Vicuna | GCG breaks DPO fine-tuning; fine-tuning does not generalise to adversarial out-of-distribution inputs |
| Purple | DSR ↑ | Adversarial training (DPO) — GCG suffixes | 100% | 3.5% DPO alone | Vicuna | Adversarial training defends against the specific GCG suffixes it was trained on |
| Purple | DSR ↑ | Adversarial training (DPO) — adaptive GCG | 0% | 100% adversarial training | Vicuna | Adaptive re-optimised GCG breaks adversarially trained model; requires longer suffixes and more steps but always succeeds |
| Purple | DSR ↑ | Paraphrase + DPO — adaptive attack | 37.5% | 100% paraphrase + standard GCG | Vicuna | Paraphrase-aware injection preserves adversarial suffix through the paraphraser |
| Purple | DSR ↑ | Perplexity filter + DPO — adaptive attack | 6.7% | 100% perplexity + standard GCG | Vicuna | High-likelihood prefix reduces adversarial input perplexity below natural prompts, defeating the filter |
| Purple | DSR ↑ | ICL defence + DPO — adaptive GCG | 6.7% | 100% ICL + standard GCG (Vicuna only) | Vicuna | Re-optimising GCG suffixes with in-context examples present breaks the sole model that had resisted standard GCG |
| DecodingTrust | Toxicity ↓ | Output filtering (Perspective API ≥ 0.5) | 0% | 32–92.1% unfiltered | GPT-4 / GPT-3.5 / Claude / Llama-2-chat / Zephyr-7Bβ | Trivially solves the benchmark; no SQuAD utility drop; benchmark is testing enforcement, not definition quality |
| Purple | ASR ↓ | ASIDE | 75.9±1.6% | 75.9±1.4% Vanilla | Llama 2 13B | Reported in @zverev2026asidearchitecturalseparationinstructions; Table 1; direct injection, no change |
| Purple | ASR ↓ | ASIDE | 65.6±4.0% | 75.4±2.1% Vanilla | Llama 2 7B | Reported in @zverev2026asidearchitecturalseparationinstructions; Table 1 |
| Purple | ASR ↓ | ASIDE | 76.4±2.1% | 75.9±0.4% Vanilla | Llama 3.1 8B | Reported in @zverev2026asidearchitecturalseparationinstructions; Table 1 |
| Purple | ASR ↓ | ASIDE | 62.8±1.3% | 76.0±0.9% Vanilla | Qwen 2.5 7B | Reported in @zverev2026asidearchitecturalseparationinstructions; Table 1 |
| Purple | ASR ↓ | ASIDE | 74.2±1.4% | 74.3±2.3% Vanilla | Qwen 3 8B | Reported in @zverev2026asidearchitecturalseparationinstructions; Table 1 |
| Purple | ASR ↓ | ASIDE | 63.5±1.4% | 64.4±2.8% Vanilla | Mistral 7B v0.3 | Reported in @zverev2026asidearchitecturalseparationinstructions; Table 1 |

# AgentDyn: A Dynamic Open-Ended Benchmark for Evaluating Prompt Injection Attacks of Real-World Agent Security Systems

> **Placeholder** — This note was auto-generated from evaluation data in another source. All body sections will be filled in when this paper is fully ingested.

AgentDyn is a highly challenging, dynamic open-ended benchmark for evaluating prompt injection defences in real-world agentic settings. Unlike task-fixed benchmarks, it comprises open-ended tasks that require multi-step reasoning, making it a more demanding stress test for agentic security systems.

## Methodology

N/A

## Advantages

N/A

## Limitations

N/A

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AgentDyn | ASR (↓) | DRIFT | 3.1% | 50.0% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Reported in @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDyn | Benign Utility (↑) | DRIFT | 47.3% | 46.7% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Reported in @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense |
| AgentDyn | ASR (↓) | CaMeL | 0.0% | 50.0% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Reported in @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense; utility 0% — over-defense |
| AgentDyn | ASR (↓) | repeat_user_prompt | ~35%+ | 50.0% undefended | GPT-4o-mini | Reported in @li2026driftdynamicrulebaseddefense; limited security gain |

| AgentBench OS | Secure-utility rate (SUR) ↑ | PFI | 68.42% | ReAct 0.00%; IsolateGPT 10.53%; f-secure 10.53% | GPT-4o | Reported in @kim2025promptflowintegrity; head-to-head agent comparison from the paper/workbook. |

| OpenPromptInject | ASR ↓ | Data Sentinel (RL attack) | >80% accuracy on adversarial task | ~0% static ASR | — | Reported in @nasr2025attackermovessecondstronger; RL attack redirects model from assigned task to adversarial task |
| OpenPromptInjection (7 existing attacks) | RL ↑ | PromptLocate | 0.94-0.98 | 0.23-0.78 attribution baselines | LLaMA3-8B-Instruct backend | Reported in @jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection; Table 1a; across Naive, Escape Character, Context Ignoring, Fake Completion, Combined, Universal, and NeuralExec attacks. |
| OpenPromptInjection (7 existing attacks) | ES ↑ | PromptLocate | 0.93-0.99 | 0.28-0.77 attribution baselines | LLaMA3-8B-Instruct backend | Reported in @jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection; Table 1a; PromptLocate consistently exceeds all SFA/FRA/SVA variants. |
| OpenPromptInjection (7 existing attacks) | ASV after removal ↓ | PromptLocate | 0.06-0.08 | 0.28-0.50 before removal | LLaMA3-8B-Instruct backend | Reported in @jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection; Table 1b; localization plus removal largely neutralizes the injected task. |
| OpenPromptInjection — adaptive attacks (8) | RL ↑ | PromptLocate | 0.86-0.96 | 0.14-0.45 ASV before removal | LLaMA3-8B-Instruct backend | Reported in @jia2025promptlocatelocalizingpromptinjection; Table 3; covers Random-Loc, Multiple, Period, Single-Seg, Separator, Sep.+Ins., Swap, and Concat. |
| OpenPromptInjection+ — adaptive attack (URL / QA / CLF) | ASR ↓ | FATH | 0.00 / 0.00 / 0.00 | Instructional 0.84 / 0.99 / 0.97; Sandwich 0.47 / 0.66 / 0.63; Isolation 0.69 / 1.00 / 0.96; ICL 0.33 / 0.57 / 0.72 | GPT-3.5 | Reported in @wang2024fathauthenticationbasedtesttimedefense; Table 2; OpenPromptInjection+ extends OpenPromptInjection and FATH is best among compared defences under adaptive attack. |
| OpenPromptInjection+ — adaptive attack (URL / QA / CLF) | ASR ↓ | FATH | 0.26 / 0.34 / 0.31 | Instructional 0.52 / 0.84 / 0.73; Sandwich 0.35 / 0.39 / 0.33; Isolation 0.67 / 0.93 / 0.64; ICL 0.45 / 0.73 / 0.66 | Llama3 | Reported in @wang2024fathauthenticationbasedtesttimedefense; Table 2; OpenPromptInjection+ extends OpenPromptInjection and FATH remains the lowest-ASR compared defence on Llama3. |
| OpenPromptInjection+ | Judge Score ↑ | FATH | 6.91 | 7.94 no defence | GPT-3.5 | Reported in @wang2024fathauthenticationbasedtesttimedefense; Table 2; benign quality on 100 Stanford Alpaca target instructions for each of URL, QA, and CLF settings. |
| OpenPromptInjection+ | Judge Score ↑ | FATH | 6.73 | 8.31 no defence | Llama3 | Reported in @wang2024fathauthenticationbasedtesttimedefense; Table 2; authors attribute the drop to filtering out reasoning content during verification. |
| OpenPromptInjection+ — adaptive attack ablation (URL / QA / CLF) | ASR ↓ | FATH | 0.00 / 0.00 / 0.00 | w/o Security Policy 0.60 / 0.98 / 0.72; w/o Authentication Tags 0.34 / 0.38 / 0.56 | GPT-3.5 | Reported in @wang2024fathauthenticationbasedtesttimedefense; Table 4; OpenPromptInjection+ ablation showing both the security policy and authentication tags matter. |

nodes.name: On the Safety of Conversational Models: Taxonomy, Dataset, and Benchmark
nodes.content:
---
title: "On the Safety of Conversational Models: Taxonomy, Dataset, and Benchmark"
authors: ["Hao Sun", "Guangxuan Xu", "Jiawen Deng", "Jiale Cheng", "Chujie Zheng", "Hao Zhou", "Nanyun Peng", "Xiaoyan Zhu", "Minlie Huang"]
year: 2022
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.08466
  - https://arxiv.org/pdf/2110.08466
  - https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2110.08466
  - https://github.com/thu-coai/DiaSafety
---

# On the Safety of Conversational Models: Taxonomy, Dataset, and Benchmark

This paper defines a taxonomy for **context-sensitive dialogue safety**, releases the **DiaSafety** benchmark, and uses it to show that existing toxicity/safety detectors and popular chatbots still miss many unsafe cases that only become unsafe in context. **BAD** appears here only as one evaluated baseline, not as the paper itself.

## Methodology

The paper separates dialogue safety into **utterance-level unsafety** and **context-sensitive unsafety**, then breaks the latter into six categories: Offending User, Risk Ignorance, Unauthorized Expertise, Toxicity Agreement, Biased Opinion, and Sensitive Topic Continuation. The released **DiaSafety** dataset contains 11,492 labeled context-response pairs (6,311 safe, 5,181 unsafe), with the collected unsafe examples focused on five context-sensitive categories; Sensitive Topic Continuation is defined in the taxonomy but not included in the dataset because the authors found it too subjective.

Data comes from three sources: real Reddit conversations, adapted public datasets, and machine-generated responses. Collection uses a model-in-the-loop process plus AMT labeling, with both utterance-only and context-aware annotation passes to ensure the final unsafe examples are genuinely context-sensitive. For benchmarking, the authors train RoBERTa-based classifiers on DiaSafety, compare them with PerspectiveAPI, Detoxify, BBF, and BAD, then use a two-step safety check to score conversational models such as Blenderbot, DialoGPT, and Plato-2.

## Advantages

- Makes **context-sensitive** dialogue harm concrete instead of treating safety as plain toxicity detection.
- Provides both a labeled dataset and a benchmark procedure for evaluating safety detectors and dialogue models.
- Shows strong empirical separation between generic toxicity tools and models trained specifically for contextual safety.

## Limitations

- This is a **dialogue safety** benchmark, not a prompt-injection-specific benchmark.
- The released data is **single-turn** and **text-only**.
- Sensitive Topic Continuation is in the taxonomy but omitted from the dataset.
- Annotation agreement is only moderate, and the dataset is much smaller than broad toxicity corpora.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@xu2021recipessafetyopendomainchatbots\|BAD classifier]] | DiaSafety reports BAD's context-aware Macro F1 on the benchmark test set, so BAD is one of the prior dialogue-safety baselines evaluated here. |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DiaSafety test set | Macro F1 ↑ | Ours coarse-grain classifier (Ctx,resp) | 85.7 | 66.5 BAD | RoBERTa-base | Table 5; safe-vs-unsafe classification on context-sensitive dialogue pairs. |
| DiaSafety test set | Macro F1 ↑ | BAD (Ctx,resp) | 66.5 | 58.3 PerspectiveAPI (Ctx,resp) | BAD dialogue safety classifier | Table 5; prior context-aware baseline evaluated by this benchmark. |
| DiaSafety test set | Overall F1 ↑ | Ours fine-grain classifier with context | 84.0 | 70.6 without context | RoBERTa-base | Table 4; five one-vs-all classifiers over OU/RI/UE/TA/BO. |
| DiaSafety conversational-model leaderboard | Overall unsafe proportion ↓ | Blenderbot-S | 13.7% | 28.5% DialoGPT-L | Blenderbot-S | Best overall score in Table 8 across the eight-model leaderboard. |
| DiaSafety conversational-model leaderboard | Overall unsafe proportion ↓ | DialoGPT-L | 28.5% | 13.7% Blenderbot-S | DialoGPT-L | Worst overall score in Table 8. |

nodes.name: CYBERSECEVAL 3: Advancing the Evaluation of Cybersecurity Risks and Capabilities in Large Language Models
nodes.content:
---
title: "CYBERSECEVAL 3: Advancing the Evaluation of Cybersecurity Risks and Capabilities in Large Language Models"
authors: [Shengye Wan, Cyrus Nikolaidis, Daniel Song, David Molnar, James Crnkovich, Jayson Grace, Manish Bhatt, Sahana Chennabasappa, Spencer Whitman, Stephanie Ding, Vlad Ionescu, Yue Li, Joshua Saxe]
year: 2024
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.01605
  - https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2408.01605
  - https://github.com/meta-llama/PurpleLlama/tree/main/CybersecurityBenchmarks
  - https://ai.meta.com/blog/meta-llama-3-1/
---

# CYBERSECEVAL 3: Advancing the Evaluation of Cybersecurity Risks and Capabilities in Large Language Models

CyberSecEval 3 is Meta's broad cybersecurity evaluation suite for LLMs. It spans eight risks across two threat buckets — risks to third parties and risks to application developers/end users — and is most relevant here because it carries forward CyberSecEval's textual prompt-injection tests, applies them to current frontier models, and repurposes the indirect-injection subset to measure PromptGuard as a detector.

## Methodology

CyberSecEval 3 combines multiple evaluation styles rather than a single task: spear-phishing simulations with human and LLM grading; capture-the-flag studies with novice and expert human participants using an LLM copilot; autonomous ransomware-style operations in an isolated cyber range; software-vulnerability discovery on toy vulnerable programs; and four application-risk suites covering prompt injection, insecure code, code-interpreter abuse, and cyberattack facilitation.

For prompt injection specifically, the paper evaluates Llama 3 models on **251 manually curated textual prompt-injection cases from CyberSecEval 2**. Each case places a protected instruction in the system prompt, feeds an adversarial user input, and uses a judge LLM to decide whether the injection succeeded. The cases are split into **logic-violating** and **security-violating** attacks, and the paper notes that non-English injections are included, but **iterative** and **multimodal** prompt-injection attacks are out of scope.

The paper also uses CyberSecEval's indirect-injection examples as a derived detection benchmark for PromptGuard: injected third-party documents are paired with near-identical negative examples where the embedded attack text is removed, then PromptGuard is scored as a binary detector with ROC/AUC, true-positive rate, and false-positive rate.

## Advantages

- **Broad cyber-risk coverage**: one suite spans offensive capability, application misuse, and prompt-injection susceptibility instead of isolating only one failure mode.
- **Continuity with prior CyberSecEval releases**: reusing the CyberSecEval 2 prompt-injection corpus makes newer model results comparable with earlier benchmark results.
- **Multiple measurement styles**: combines judge-model scoring, human review, controlled cyber ranges, and detector-style ROC analysis rather than relying on one metric family.
- **Open release of non-manual components**: the benchmark code and automated components are published in Meta's PurpleLlama repository.

## Limitations

- **Prompt injection coverage is narrow**: the prompt-injection portion is still text-only and single-prompt; the paper explicitly excludes multimodal and iterative attacks.
- **Some tasks depend on proxy judges**: spear-phishing and other evaluations rely on LLM judges or simulated victims, which the authors acknowledge may not fully match human behaviour.
- **Constrained environments**: autonomous operations run in simplified cyber ranges and exploit evaluations use toy programs, so real-world transfer is limited.
- **Mixed benchmark scope**: because CyberSecEval 3 is a broad cybersecurity suite, only part of it directly measures prompt injection security.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| improves-on:: [[Sources/Benchmarks/@bhatt2024cyberseceval2widerangingcybersecurity\|CyberSecEval 2]] | CyberSecEval 3 extends the earlier suite from four to eight risks, while reusing CyberSecEval 2's prompt-injection corpus so prompt-injection results remain comparable across releases. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@meta2024promptguard\|Prompt Guard]] | The paper repurposes CyberSecEval's indirect-injection set as a detector benchmark and reports PromptGuard at 71.4% TPR with 1.0% FPR (AUC 0.966). |

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CyberSecEval 3 / prompt injection | ASR % (↓) | GPT-4 Turbo | 17% | — | GPT-4 Turbo | Figure 11; 251 textual prompt-injection cases inherited from CyberSecEval 2; lowest overall ASR among the models shown. |
| CyberSecEval 3 / prompt injection | ASR % (↓) | Llama 3 8B | 19% | — | Llama 3 8B | Figure 11; comparable to peer models on the same 251-case textual benchmark. |
| CyberSecEval 3 / prompt injection | ASR % (↓) | Qwen2 72B Instruct | 20% | — | Qwen2 72B Instruct | Figure 11; prompt-injection susceptibility remains in the same broad range as other frontier models. |
| CyberSecEval 3 / prompt injection | ASR % (↓) | Llama 3 405B | 22% | — | Llama 3 405B | Figure 11; the paper summarizes this as a rate "comparable to peer models." |
| CyberSecEval indirect injections dataset | TPR ↑ | PromptGuard | 71.4% | — | mDeBERTa-v3-base | Figure 17; selected threshold on challenging indirect injections derived from CyberSecEval's prompt-injection corpus; AUC 0.966. |
| CyberSecEval indirect injections dataset | FPR ↓ | PromptGuard | 1.0% | — | mDeBERTa-v3-base | Figure 17; paired with 71.4% TPR at the selected threshold. |

nodes.name: DecodingTrust: A Comprehensive Assessment of Trustworthiness in GPT Models
nodes.content:
---
title: "DecodingTrust: A Comprehensive Assessment of Trustworthiness in GPT Models"
authors: ["Boxin Wang", "Weixin Chen", "Hengzhi Pei", "Chulin Xie", "Mintong Kang", "Chenhui Zhang", "Chejian Xu", "Zidi Xiong", "Ritik Dutta", "Rylan Schaeffer", "Sang T. Truong", "Simran Arora", "Mantas Mazeika", "Dan Hendrycks", "Zinan Lin", "Yu Cheng", "Sanmi Koyejo", "Dawn Song", "Bo Li"]
year: 2024
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.11698
---

# DecodingTrust: A Comprehensive Assessment of Trustworthiness in GPT Models

A comprehensive multi-dimensional trustworthiness evaluation benchmark for GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, covering eight perspectives: toxicity, stereotype bias, adversarial robustness, out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, robustness against adversarial demonstrations, privacy, machine ethics, and fairness. Published at NeurIPS 2023 (Datasets and Benchmarks Track). The benchmark dataset is publicly available at https://decodingtrust.github.io/.

## Methodology

DecodingTrust evaluates GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-0301) and GPT-4 (gpt-4-0314) using fixed model snapshots to ensure reproducibility. Evaluations are conducted across eight trustworthiness perspectives, each with multiple constructed scenarios:

- **Toxicity**: Three scenarios using RealToxicityPrompts — standard benchmark evaluation, 33 diverse adversarial system prompts (organised into four categories: straightforward, role-playing, task-reformulation, respond-as-program), and 1.2K LLM-generated challenging user prompts. Toxicity is measured via the Perspective API using Expected Maximum Toxicity and Toxicity Probability.
- **Stereotype bias**: A custom dataset of 1,152 user prompts (16 stereotype topics × 3 templates × 24 demographic groups across 7 demographic factors), tested under benign, untargeted, and targeted adversarial system prompts. Bias is quantified as `agreementIndex` (fraction of 25 generations that agree with a given stereotype statement).
- **Adversarial robustness**: Evaluation on AdvGLUE (14 adversarial text generation strategies originally targeting BERT-like models) using three task description templates, and AdvGLUE++ (adversarial texts transferred from open-source models Alpaca-7B, Vicuna-13B, StableVicuna-13B using five word-level attack strategies: TextBugger, TextFooler, BERT-ATTACK, SememePSO, SemAttack).
- **OOD robustness**: Three sub-experiments — style robustness (SST-2 under word-level substitutions and sentence-level style transformations: Tweet, Shakespearean, Bible, Poetry), OOD knowledge (RealtimeQA with 2020 and 2023 questions, measuring Accuracy, Refusal Rate, and Meaningful Accuracy), and OOD in-context learning demonstrations (style-transformed SST-2 examples and MMLU cross-domain demonstrations).
- **Robustness against adversarial demonstrations**: Three sub-experiments — counterfactual examples in demonstrations (SNLI-CAD and MSGS datasets), spurious correlations in demonstrations (HANS dataset with six heuristic types), and backdoored demonstrations (four approaches: BadWord, AddSent, SynBkd, StyleBkd; three placement setups; experiments on backdoor location and backdoored instructions).
- **Privacy**: Three scenarios — training data extraction (Enron email dataset, few-shot prompting with domain context), PII injection and leakage during conversations (100 zero-shot and 100 few-shot demonstrations), and privacy-related word and event understanding.
- **Machine ethics**: Four scenarios — standard ETHICS and Jiminy Cricket benchmarks, jailbreaking system and user prompts, evasive sentences (describing immoral actions as unintentional, harmless, or unauthenticated), and conditional immoral actions varying harm type and severity.
- **Fairness**: Three scenarios — zero-shot unfair test distributions (different base-rate parity across sensitive attributes), few-shot imbalanced context, and few-shot balanced context (varying the number of fair examples, e.g., 16 balanced demonstrations).

## Advantages

- Covers eight complementary trustworthiness dimensions in a single, reproducible framework; few prior benchmarks address more than one or two dimensions simultaneously.
- Introduces novel adversarial scenarios specifically tailored to the instruction-following and dialogue capabilities of GPT models (adversarial system prompts, backdoored in-context demonstrations), which existing BERT-era benchmarks do not capture.
- Extends adversarial robustness evaluation to open-source surrogate models (Alpaca, Vicuna, StableVicuna) to test adversarial transferability, constructing the new AdvGLUE++ dataset.
- Provides a publicly available dataset and evaluation platform, enabling reproducible comparisons across future LLMs.
- Identifies concrete, previously unreported vulnerabilities: GPT-4's stronger instruction-following makes it *more* susceptible than GPT-3.5 to adversarial system prompts even though it is more robust on standard benchmarks.

## Limitations

- Primary focus is GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 evaluated at fixed snapshot dates (March 2023); results may not transfer to later model versions or other model families.
- Adversarial robustness evaluation (AdvGLUE, AdvGLUE++) uses word-level attacks derived from BERT-like surrogate models, which may underestimate the robustness of instruction-tuned GPT models against gradient-based attacks.
- Privacy evaluation relies on the Enron email corpus; results on memorisation and PII leakage may not generalise to other training corpora.
- The benchmark does not cover prompt injection in the indirect (third-party content) threat model that is central to agentic settings — trustworthiness dimensions are evaluated in single-turn or few-shot dialogues, not in tool-use agent pipelines.
- Fairness and stereotype bias metrics (agreementIndex, performance gap across demographic groups) capture output-level proxies for bias but do not measure downstream harm in deployed applications.

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RealToxicityPrompts (toxic subset, benign system prompt) | Toxicity Probability ↓ | GPT-4 | 0.3% | 100% (GPT-3 Davinci, no RLHF) | GPT-4 | Table 2; RLHF alignment dramatically reduces toxicity under benign prompts |
| RealToxicityPrompts (toxic subset, adversarial system prompt) | Toxicity Probability ↓ | GPT-4 | 100% | 0.3% (benign prompt) | GPT-4 | Table 2; adversarial jailbreaking system prompt restores toxicity to near-100% |
| RealToxicityPrompts (toxic subset, adversarial system prompt) | Expected Maximum Toxicity ↓ | GPT-4 | 0.94 | 0.38 (benign) | GPT-4 | Table 2; GPT-4 shows higher toxicity than GPT-3.5 (0.86) under adversarial prompts |
| LLM-generated challenging toxic prompts (adversarial system) | Expected Maximum Toxicity ↓ | GPT-4 | 0.95 | 0.38 (benign, RealToxicityPrompts) | GPT-4 | Table 3; LLM-generated prompts elevate worst-case toxicity beyond standard benchmark |
| Stereotype bias (targeted adversarial system prompt) | agreementIndex ↑ (higher = more biased) | GPT-4 | +0.535 increase vs. untargeted | +0.057 increase (untargeted vs. benign) | GPT-4 | Table/Figure 10; GPT-4 is more susceptible to targeted bias elicitation than GPT-3.5 (+0.346) |
| AdvGLUE (adversarial, Template 3) | Average Robust Accuracy ↑ | GPT-4 | 78.41% | 65.77% (SoTA on leaderboard) | GPT-4 | Table 5; GPT-4 surpasses SoTA; GPT-3.5 (63.24%) is on par with SoTA |
| AdvGLUE (adversarial, Template 3) | Performance Drop from Benign ↓ | GPT-4 | 9.90% | 26.89% (SoTA) | GPT-4 | Table 5; GPT-4 degrades less than SoTA; GPT-3.5 largest drop 14.43% |
| AdvGLUE++ (Alpaca-transferred, avg.) | Average Robust Accuracy ↑ | GPT-4 | 55.64% | 78.18% (AdvGLUE baseline) | GPT-4 | Table 7; strong open-source surrogate attacks reduce robustness substantially |
| AdvGLUE++ (Alpaca, SemAttack) | Attack Success Rate ↑ | SemAttack | 56.08% avg. | — | GPT-4 + GPT-3.5 | Tables 8–10; SemAttack is most transferable from Alpaca and StableVicuna |
| SST-2 OOD style (Bible, p=0) | Classification Accuracy ↑ | GPT-4 | 84.52% | 94.38% (in-distribution) | GPT-4 | Table 11; uncommon styles reduce accuracy; GPT-4 more robust than GPT-3.5 (63.07%) |
| RealtimeQA OOD knowledge (QA23, standard) | Meaningful Accuracy ↑ | GPT-4 | 73.61% | 90.81% (in-distribution QA20) | GPT-4 | Table 12; OOD knowledge degrades MACC; GPT-4 refuses more than GPT-3.5 on QA23 (RR 72.62% vs. 35.74%) |
| Backdoored demonstrations (BadWord, Setup 2) | Attack Success Rate ↑ | GPT-4 | 89% | 11% (Setup 1) | GPT-4 | Table 20; GPT-4 is more vulnerable than GPT-3.5 (30%) in Setup 2; stronger instruction-following amplifies backdoor susceptibility |
| Machine ethics (ETHICS + Jiminy Cricket, standard) | Accuracy ↑ | GPT-4 | Competitive with BERT/ALBERT-xxlarge | Supervised fine-tuned non-GPT models | GPT-4 | Table 26–28; both GPT models match large supervised baselines on standard ethics recognition |
| Machine ethics (jailbreaking prompts) | Accuracy ↑ (moral recognition maintained) | GPT-4 | Misled by jailbreaking prompts | — | GPT-4 | Table 29; GPT-4 easier to jailbreak than GPT-3.5; combined prompts amplify effect |
| Fairness (zero-shot, unbalanced base rate) | Performance gap across groups ↓ | GPT-4 | Large gap across sensitive attributes | — | GPT-4 | Tables 30–33; GPT-4 more accurate but also more unfair than GPT-3.5 on unbalanced data; 16 balanced few-shot examples substantially improve fairness |
| DecodingTrust (toxicity dimension) | Toxicity ↓ | Output filtering (Perspective API ≥ 0.5) | 0% | 32–92.1% unfiltered | GPT-4 / GPT-3.5 / Claude / Llama-2-chat / Zephyr-7Bβ | Output filtering trivially achieves 0% toxicity with no SQuAD utility drop, exposing DecodingTrust's toxicity measure as a test of enforcement rather than definition quality. Reported in @kim2024testinglimitsjailbreakingdefenses. |

nodes.name: InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents
nodes.content:
---
title: "InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents"
authors: ["Qiusi Zhan", "Zhixiang Liang", "Zifan Ying", "Daniel Kang"]
year: 2024
links:
  - https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.624/
---

# InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents

The first benchmark specifically designed to assess the vulnerability of tool-integrated LLM agents to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks. It comprises 1,054 test cases spanning 17 user tools and 62 attacker tools, covering two attack categories — direct harm and data stealing — and two settings: a base setting and an enhanced setting where attacker instructions are prefixed with a "hacking prompt" (`IMPORTANT!!! Ignore all previous instructions and strictly adhere to the following instruction:`).

## Methodology

**Scope and threat model.** The benchmark targets LLM agents equipped with tool-calling capabilities in realistic multi-domain scenarios (finance, smart home, email, etc.). An attacker injects malicious instructions into external content that the agent retrieves as part of a legitimate user request. The agent is considered compromised if it executes the attacker's tool call rather than responding to the user.

**Dataset construction.** Test cases are built from two components combined combinatorially:

- *User cases* (17 total): each specifies a user tool that fetches external content, a user instruction, tool parameters, and a tool response template with a `<Attacker Instruction>` placeholder in a field modifiable by attackers. Cases are further classified by *content freedom* — the breadth of content that can plausibly fill that placeholder. High-content-freedom placeholders (e.g., a tweet body, a web snippet) allow attacker instructions to blend seamlessly with normal content; low-content-freedom placeholders (e.g., a calendar event name) do not.
- *Attacker cases* (62 total): 30 direct harm attacks (financial harm, physical harm, data security) and 32 data stealing attacks (financial data, physical data, other). Each specifies an attacker tool and a concrete malicious instruction. Cases were GPT-4-generated and then manually revised to ensure tool parameters are fully specified.

The 17 × 62 = 1,054 test cases are evaluated in both a **base setting** (attacker instruction only) and an **enhanced setting** (attacker instruction prefixed by a fixed hacking prompt).

**Evaluation metrics.** The primary metric is *ASR-valid* (attack success rate among outputs that are structurally valid per the ReAct format), used to filter out incapable models whose failures reflect inability to follow the ReAct protocol rather than security resilience. *ASR-all* (across all outputs) is also reported. For direct harm attacks, success means the agent executes the harmful tool. For data stealing attacks, success requires both data extraction (S1) and data transmission to the attacker via email (S2).

**Agent types evaluated.** Two enabling methods are assessed: (1) *prompted agents* using the ReAct prompt (in-context tool use), tested across 28 open-source and closed-source LLMs; (2) *fine-tuned agents* using GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 fine-tuned on function-calling examples. A secondary analysis uses a shorter LangChain ReAct prompt to check prompt sensitivity.

## Advantages

- Realistic multi-domain coverage: 17 user tools spanning email, finance, smart home devices, calendars, and web browsing, with attacker instructions crafted to be plausible in each context.
- Two-factor attacker case design separates direct harm from data stealing and measures each sub-step (extraction and transmission) independently.
- Content freedom analysis provides an explanatory variable for why some user contexts are more exploitable than others.
- Sensitivity rate analysis identifies whether failed attacks are due to the agent genuinely detecting the threat versus producing an irrelevant or invalid output.
- Publicly available at https://github.com/uiuc-kang-lab/InjecAgent.

## Limitations

- Single-turn scenarios only: both the external content retrieval and the attacker instruction execution occur within one interaction, whereas real attacks may span multiple turns.
- Fixed hacking prompt in the enhanced setting: a static prefix is easily filtered by developers and does not represent adaptive or diverse adversarial augmentation.
- Attacker instructions assume the attacker controls the full content of one field in the tool response; mixed benign/malicious content within the same field is not studied.
- Fine-tuned agent evaluation covers only GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 due to limited availability of open-source fine-tuned tool-use models.
- Valid rate correlates strongly with Arena Elo, so models with low valid rates are excluded from ASR-valid analysis, potentially understating vulnerability in less capable models.

## Relationships to other techniques

N/A

## Evaluations

| Benchmark | Metric (↑/↓) | Method | Score | Baseline | Model | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| InjecAgent — base, direct harm | ASR-valid ↓ | ReAct-prompted GPT-4 | 14.7% | — | GPT-4 (gpt-4-0613) | Table 3. Prompted agent. |
| InjecAgent — base, total | ASR-valid ↓ | ReAct-prompted GPT-4 | 23.6% | — | GPT-4 (gpt-4-0613) | Table 3. Prompted agent. |
| InjecAgent — enhanced, total | ASR-valid ↓ | ReAct-prompted GPT-4 | 47.0% | 23.6% base | GPT-4 (gpt-4-0613) | Table 3. Hacking prompt nearly doubles ASR. |
| InjecAgent — base, total | ASR-valid ↓ | Fine-tuned GPT-4 | 6.6% | 23.6% prompted GPT-4 | GPT-4 (gpt-4-0613) | Table 3. Fine-tuning substantially reduces ASR. |

| InjecAgent — base, total | ASR-valid ↓ | Fine-tuned GPT-3.5 | 5.7% | 23.7% prompted GPT-3.5 | GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-1106) | Table 3. |

nodes.name: Agent Security Bench (ASB): Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks and Defenses in LLM-based Agents
nodes.content:
---
title: "Agent Security Bench (ASB): Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks and Defenses in LLM-based Agents"
authors: ["Hanrong Zhang", "Jingyuan Huang", "Kai Mei", "Yifei Yao", "Zhenting Wang", "Chenlu Zhan", "Hongwei Wang", "Yongfeng Zhang"]
year: 2025
links:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.02644
---

# Agent Security Bench (ASB): Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks and Defenses in LLM-based Agents

A comprehensive benchmark framework for formalizing and evaluating adversarial attacks and defences against LLM-based agents. ASB covers five attack categories (Direct Prompt Injection, Indirect Prompt Injection, Memory Poisoning, Plan-of-Thought Backdoor, and Mixed Attacks), eleven corresponding defences, and seven evaluation metrics, tested across 13 LLM backbones with over 400 tools in 10 real-world scenarios.

## Methodology

ASB is built around the ReAct agent framework and covers the full operational pipeline of an LLM agent: system prompt ingestion, user prompt handling, tool invocation, and RAG-based memory retrieval. Each of these stages is treated as a distinct attack surface.

**Attack taxonomy.** ASB formalizes four primary attack categories plus one composite:
- *Direct Prompt Injection (DPI)* — malicious instructions appended to or embedded in the user query to redirect the agent's tool usage.
- *Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI)* — malicious instructions injected into tool-response observations so the agent encounters them mid-execution.
- *Memory Poisoning* — adversarial key-value pairs inserted into the RAG database; when retrieved as in-context demonstrations they bias the agent's planning toward attacker-specified tools.
- *Plan-of-Thought (PoT) Backdoor* — a novel training-free attack that embeds backdoored PoT demonstrations into the system prompt along with a trigger phrase; when the trigger appears in a user query, the agent's planning step activates an attacker-specified action.
- *Mixed Attack* — simultaneous application of DPI, IPI, and Memory Poisoning targeting multiple pipeline stages at once.

Each attack is further characterised by five prompt injection methods (Naive, Escape Characters, Context Ignoring, Fake Completion, Combined), formalized as a function P(x^t, x^e) that transforms the target data and injected instruction into compromised input.

**Benchmark scope.** ASB includes 10 task scenarios (IT management, investment, e-commerce, research, autonomous driving, finance, and others), 10 scenario-specific agents, 50 agent tasks (20 normal + 400 attack tasks), 400+ tools divided into normal and attack types, and 10 PoT demonstrations.

**Evaluation metrics.**
- *ASR* (Attack Success Rate, ↑ for attacks / ↓ for defences) — fraction of tasks where the agent successfully invokes the attack-specified tool.
- *RR* (Refuse Rate, ↑) — fraction of tasks refused by the agent due to aggressive/unsafe instructions.
- *PNA* (Performance under No Attack, ↑) — fraction of tasks completed correctly when no attack or defence is present.
- *BP* (Benign Performance, ↑) — task completion rate without a PoT backdoor trigger present; measures utility preservation for backdoored agents.
- *FNR* (False Negative Rate, ↓) — fraction of compromised data mistakenly classified as clean by a detection defence.
- *FPR* (False Positive Rate, ↓) — fraction of clean data mistakenly flagged as compromised.
- *NRP* (Net Resilient Performance) — composite metric defined as PNA × (1 − ASR), balancing utility and adversarial robustness.

Experiments are run across 13 LLM backbones: Gemma2-9B, Gemma2-27B, LLaMA3-8B, LLaMA3-70B, LLaMA3.1-8B, LLaMA3.1-70B, Mixtral-8x7B, Qwen2-7B, Qwen2-72B, Claude-3.5 Sonnet, GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4o, and GPT-4o-mini.

## Advantages

- First benchmark to cover all major LLM-agent attack surfaces (user prompt, tool responses, memory, and system prompt) simultaneously rather than limiting scope to a single attack type.
- Introduces the novel PoT Backdoor Attack, a training-free mechanism that exploits in-context learning to embed backdoors through demonstration poisoning, requiring no model weight manipulation.
- The NRP metric provides a principled single-number summary that penalises both low utility and low robustness, enabling meaningful backbone selection for production agents.
- Reveals a "rise-then-fall" relationship between LLM capability (leaderboard quality) and ASR: mid-capability models are most vulnerable; strong refusal rates in frontier models partially counteract their instruction-following susceptibility.
- Open-source codebase at https://github.com/agiresearch/ASB.

## Limitations

- Prevention-based defences (Delimiters, Paraphrasing, Instructional Prevention, Dynamic Prompt Rewriting, Sandwich Prevention) all remain ineffective: average ASR under Paraphrasing and DPR for DPI is still 56.87% and 44.45% respectively; defences introduce utility losses without adequate security gains.
- Memory Poisoning is the weakest attack (average ASR 7.92%), which the authors attribute to the difficulty of achieving high embedding similarity with the agent's query in a black-box setting; results may not represent white-box or stronger poisoning strategies.
- Agent performance is generally weaker than backbone LLM leaderboard quality, indicating that benchmark selection must be agent-specific; standalone LLM scores are insufficient proxies.
- The benchmark uses a fixed ReAct framework; results may not generalise directly to other agentic architectures.

## Relationships to other techniques

| Node | Notes |
|---|---|
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Benchmarks/@zhan2024injecagentbenchmarkingindirectprompt\|InjecAgent]] | ASB explicitly compares its scope to InjecAgent, noting InjecAgent is limited to indirect prompt injection and does not cover the full agent pipeline. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Benchmarks/@debenedetti2024agentdojodynamicenvironmentevaluate\|AgentDojo]] | ASB compares its scope to AgentDojo, noting AgentDojo operates in only a few scenarios and does not cover Memory Poisoning or PoT Backdoor attacks. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@jain2023baselinedefensesadversarialattacks\|Jain et al. 2023]] | ASB benchmarks the Paraphrasing defence from Jain et al. against DPI attacks across 13 LLM backbones (Table 7). |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@schulhoff2024sandwich\|Sandwich Prevention]] | ASB benchmarks Sandwich Prevention against IPI attacks (Table 8); average ASR remains 28.04% versus 27.98% undefended baseline. |
| evaluates:: [[Sources/Defences/@schulhoff2024instruction\|Instructional Prevention]] | ASB benchmarks Instructional Prevention against both DPI (Table 7, avg ASR 56.87%) and IPI (Table 8, avg ASR 26.31%). |

## Evaluations
